item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] The Most Passive Aggressive Letter in History – Ada Palmer
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一封跪着递出的耳光

这封信最迷人的不是它的"无礼"，而是它的完美礼仪。洛伦佐没有违反任何规则——他派了儿子（合法替身），用了最恭敬的措辞，履行了所有外交义务。真正的攻击藏在那句"我没有兄弟了"里——一句陈述句，没有指控，没有形容词，却让整个教廷无法回应。最锋利的武器，是让对方无法否认的事实本身。这是一种"零毒性"的毒药：教皇若装作没看见，就承认了恐惧；若回应，就承认了罪行。

跨领域连接：礼仪即区块链

这种文艺复兴的外交语法，与现代密码学里的零知识证明结构惊人相似——洛伦佐"证明了"他知道教皇做了什么，却没有"披露"任何具体指控。更深一层，这套"亲吻教皇脚趾的同时给他一记耳光"的技艺，与日本茶道中千利休向丰臣秀吉敬茶的姿态、与今天Twitter上"祝贺"竞争对手获奖的发文，是同一棵进化树上的不同枝叶：当公开冲突成本过高时，人类发明了"语义双轨制"——表层服从仪式，底层传递警告。

一个让人意外的观察

大多数人读这段历史会聚焦"教皇竟然组织暗杀"，但真正反常的是另一件事：洛伦佐居然还在玩这个游戏。他的弟弟被屠杀、家族险些覆灭，他却仍然派儿子去亲吻凶手同党的脚。这不是软弱，而是一个深刻的系统洞察——他意识到仪式本身是一种基础设施，一旦退出，就等于宣布整个国际秩序的崩溃，而崩溃中受损最大的恰恰是佛罗伦萨这种依赖贸易和外交的商业共和国。

由此引出一条隐藏的系统真相：在权力极度不对称的关系中，"继续参与"往往是最强硬的反抗。彻底翻脸是弱者的奢侈，而强者的克制是一种持续施压。洛伦佐没有发动战争，他发动的是一场关于"我们都记得"的长期消耗战——这与冷战中的核威慑、与现代企业间的专利诉讼"冷战"、与家庭中那种几十年不和却年年聚餐的亲戚关系，共享着同一种深层语法：用礼貌的在场，惩罚对方的存在。

那句"我没有兄弟了"——七个字，却是一座纪念碑。

📝 转录文本

引子：历史上最被动攻击的一封信

Right. So, let me use what a fun example — the most passive aggressive letter ever written in the entire history of time, in my opinion.

教廷的服从仪式

There's a type of ceremony that happens when the new pope is elected, which is the giving of oaths of obedience. A major ambassador from every polity in Christendom comes to Rome, and they wait in line for a long time, and then they give a speech — a long-winded speech about how great the monarch is that they're there to represent, and how vast his power is, blah blah blah blah blah, and how pious he is, and how glad he is, "Your Holiness, that you're the pope now. Congratulations on behalf of my wonderful king."

And you're supposed to send like the highest status possible person who can leave your polity without it falling down. You might send a younger son of the king. You might send the lord chancellor. In the case of Florence, you're going to send the most prominent citizen you can.

洛伦佐与教皇西克斯图斯

Though when Pope Sixtus was elected, it was Lorenzo de' Medici himself — not the dedicatee of The Prince, the grandfather and namesake of the dedicatee of The Prince — who went to deliver this oration of obedience, which means literally prostrating yourself in front of the pope, literally kissing his feet, and giving this oath.

Lorenzo did this for Pope Sixtus, with whom he was negotiating to try desperately to get a cardinalship for his brother. Pope Sixtus instead organized the Pazzi conspiracy to try to butcher the Medici family, killed Lorenzo's brother, killed a number of his allies as well, and attempted to have a coup take over Florence.

下一任教皇：同一派系的傀儡

When the next pope was elected after Sixtus — Pope Innocent, who was, as everyone knew, a puppet of the same faction that Sixtus was from — so we go from this very dangerous pope who had tried to wipe out Lorenzo's family, to a puppet of the same faction.

那封信

Lorenzo sent his son instead of himself to go give this oath, and accompanying his son to go deliver the message, apologized to his Holiness that:

"I could not come myself, but the last time this duty fell upon me, I had a brother upon whom I could leave the burden of the state in my absence. Since now I have no brother, I cannot come in person."

It's a very respectful letter, but it's also very overt about the fact that he does not trust — and will not again trust — this faction.

结语：仪式与现实的角力

Right? So they negotiate very carefully how to deal with the fact that the popes have this great spiritual power, but sometimes the popes are acting as horrifically selfish warlords.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [Business of Sport] World Cup Update: FIFA’s 99% Attendance Claim & The Managers Making Their Mark
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、99% 出席率：一场关于"会计语言"的认知战

最反直觉的洞察是：FIFA 并没有撒谎，它只是用了与观众完全不同的字典。

"Reported attendance（报告出席）"在足球俱乐部 CEO 的内部账本里，从来就不是"现场人头数"，而是售票口径——只要票从 FIFA 手里转移到代理机构、赞助商、企业客户手里，它就在"会计意义上"被消化了。这本质上和上市公司"sell-in vs sell-through"（厂家发货 vs 终端卖出）的把戏一模一样：宝洁、可口可乐财报里那些"渠道库存"，FIFA 翻译成体育语言就是"空座位"。

所以 99% 与现场目测的 85–90% 并不矛盾——它们是两个不同维度的真相。真正值得警惕的不是数字本身，而是 FIFA 第一次把 B2B 的会计动作，包装成 B2C 的情感故事卖给球迷。

二、FIFA Logo 横扫屏幕：一个被忽视的"品牌僭越"信号

Charlie 注意到的那个细节——精彩瞬间转场用的是 FIFA Logo 而不是 World Cup 2026 Logo——比 99% 的争议更值得玩味。

这是一种主办方对赛事 IP 的"反向蚕食"。类比一下：如果奥运会转场不再是五环而是 IOC 的盾牌、戛纳电影节字幕不再是金棕榈而是组委会缩写——你会立刻嗅到一种"机构想把自己摆到艺术之上"的味道。

这暗合一个跨领域规律：当一个治理机构开始焦虑自己的存在感时，它会试图把自己从"裁判"挪到"主角"位置。央行行长抢镜头、风投合伙人把自己 P 进创始人照片、私募 CEO 把基金 logo 印得比被投公司还大——都是同一种组织心理学症候。Infantino 时代的 FIFA 正在经历这种身份漂移。

三、Anthony Barry 中场怒火 vs Ken Griffin 的 600 万美元：同一条"边际优势链"的两端

这一集最隐秘的连接，是把助理教练的选择和对冲基金大佬补贴主帅工资放在同一个分析框架下看。

Charlie 说"经理对结果的影响只有 5–10%"，但他真正想说的是：精英表现是一条由无数 5% 叠加而成的复利曲线。Tuchel 选 Barry 而不是选"忠诚的应声虫"，是在助理席位上抢 5%；Ken Griffin 替美国足协补 Pochettino 工资的差额，是在主帅席位上抢 5%；Barry 在 2-0 领先时仍然中场暴怒，是在心理标准上抢 5%。

跨领域的惊喜在这里：这套逻辑其实是 Renaissance Technologies 这类量化基金的核心信条——单一信号的 alpha 都微不足道，但把几十个 5% 的弱信号叠在一起，年化就能跑赢市场。Griffin 自己就是干这行的，所以他对足协说的话——"为什么把这一点点边际优势留给运气"——根本不是球迷语言，是对冲基金语言入侵体育治理。

更深一层：这预示着未来十年国家队足球的真正分水岭，可能不在青训体系，而在哪个国家率先允许"私人资本以慈善方式补贴公共体育治理的人才市场"。这是一种全新的、介于公私之间的体育组织形态，美国正在无意中成为第一个实验场。

四、一个尾注式的彩蛋观察

Southgate 与 Tuchel 的差别被压缩成了一句话："Southgate 做的是 reset，Tuchel 做的是 extract。"

把它放大看，这其实是所有"接班型领导者"和"突破型领导者"的永恒分野——前者修复文化、后者压榨极限。乔布斯回苹果是 reset，库克是 extract；瓜迪奥拉去拜仁是 extract，齐达内回皇马是 reset。英格兰队的故事，本质上是一个国家终于从"修复创伤"阶段，走到了"敢于被压榨"的阶段。这才是这届世界杯对英格兰真正的隐喻。

📝 转录文本

开场：世界杯进行时

World Cup. World Cup. Um we've been getting um

Have you been enjoying it?

Oh, still. Yeah. Again, the way the groups fall, you seem to have Mbappe, Haaland, Messi all playing pretty much within 12 hours of each other. And again, recording it slightly early this week on a Tuesday — you've had Messi scoring two, you've had Haaland scoring two, you've had Mbappe scoring two. This Golden Boot race is heating up. My old tip for the Boot, Julian Alvarez, barely barely barely featured.

But isn't it brilliant? Like, still, you know, all of these top players seem to be performing, which is exactly what FIFA would have wanted.

关于"看走眼"的坦白：World Cup 比预想中好

And Charlie, we've had a lot of people come in and go, "Oh, all those things you were saying about tickets and all of this — and it seems all right."

I think firstly, the point that we really made — and this is a time now two weeks into the tournament where you can probably have a look and make a reasonable assessment — we were not sitting here gleefully saying that this was going to be a disaster. This is a World Cup. We desperately want this to be the event that we enjoy as fans as well.

A point that we made at the beginning, you know — with South Africa, with Russia, with Qatar — there had been that similar negativity, and quite often how that feeling can change when the football starts, because the product World Cup is just so good. And it really seems like that has happened again. Attendances have been nowhere near as bad as feared.

上座率争议：99% 是什么意思？

And just as I was about to give FIFA some credit — uh, you know, because I think, you know, looking at the majority of the stadiums, they have been pretty full. They release this — by pretty full, pretty full…

We mean pretty full. Not full.

No, they they haven't been full. I mean, look, there are the odd game which is full, but I'm very very aware: if you look closely that there are vacant patches in — I would say if not most, than close to most — most of the games.

Yeah. But they came out with 99% stadium occupancy statistic yesterday. 2.3 million through the gates, highest ever in 36-match attendance, 64,000 average attendance.

I think it was just the 99% average I was looking at, going: just absolutely no chance that the stadiums have been 99% full, right? Forget it. So that's absolute rubbish.

CEO 视角：Reported Attendance vs In-Stadium Attendance

I occasionally get into like backs and forwards with fans when I'm running football clubs about this point about reported attendance.

Reported attendance is basically a "ticket sold" metric.

So in other words, let's just say for sake of argument — I'm not saying it definitely has happened, but we have suggested it might have happened — that FIFA have shifted a certain number of tickets onto agencies, let's say. Those tickets will then largely be recorded as being sold.

Right? Because they're no longer in FIFA — they've been moved elsewhere. If some of those tickets in turn don't get sold, that doesn't really… FIFA won't even necessarily know that. So when you do see chunks of stadiums full, that will be where somebody has taken those tickets off FIFA's hands and have then not reported them.

What we often find in league football in this country is that our reported attendance number includes all the season tickets, for instance. So let's say you've got a stadium of 20,000 and your home end is 18,000, and you're selling 12,000 season tickets — you count all 12,000 season tickets as attending every game, because that ticket has been sold. Now whether that person then turns up or not doesn't affect the reported attendance — it affects the in-stadium attendance.

So as a Chief Executive, I'll see both numbers. I'll see the reported attendance (tickets sold) and the in-stadium attendance, because legally you have to know how many people are in your own stadium.

What we're seeing here is the difference between reported attendance and in-stadium attendance. So reported attendance is 99%, right — which means that FIFA have only kept back a very small number of tickets for themselves — and in-stadium attendance, looking at it so far, I would suggest is probably somewhere between 85 and 90%.

但这是世界杯，本该爆满

Yeah, right. But it's the bloody World Cup, right? I mean, the World Cup should travel. You're not asking people to go to Russia, which is huge distances, not necessarily a country everyone wants to spend time in — things like food and drink offerings not quite the same as in America, etc. Qatar — other complications, alcohol, etc. In America: huge domestic population, very close to Latin America, which provides a lot of the fans for a World Cup.

It should be sold out in stadium, not just reported attendance. So I absolutely do not withdraw — and I think, you know, nor does most of the serious football population withdraw — any of the criticisms aimed at the way in which Infantino and FIFA have set up the ticketing for this World Cup.

Even leaving aside the fact that there are empty spaces in the stadiums, the fact that ticket prices have been so high of course inevitably has blocked out an awful lot of what you would call the real fans, for whom this should be the fiesta to end all fiestas. And of course it has meant that probably more of those spaces have been taken by the US domestic market, even for games that don't involve America.

So look — all of that stuff is criticism of the way in which the World Cup has been organized, and it's perfectly possible to hold that together with at the same time saying the football itself is terrific.

一个细节：FIFA Logo 的"上位"

Yeah. There's one other element I noticed which I find quite extraordinary on the FIFA piece, which is: if you've been watching the broadcast, every time there's a highlight, a big FIFA logo moves across the screen.

This is the World Cup, right? It's like being at the Olympics — instead of having the Olympic rings transition across, you have the IOC. They're putting themselves front and center of this rather than it being about the World Cup, the World Cup logo.

Interesting point.

You know, every single time, you'll notice it tonight. FIFA. Not US 2026 — FIFA.

英格兰之夜：理论上、理论上

Now, we're recording this, Charlie, of course, as has happened previously, on the day in which England are due to play in the evening. So in theory, this is the evening at which England qualify for knockout stages. In theory.

In theory. Football is —

England, Chaz, is England.

It's England, and it's football, and we've seen some — let's face it — we've seen some pretty mighty upsets so far, which is part of what's made the whole thing so entertaining.

So it's going to be really interesting to see. In the first match, England were playing against a team that came to play. You know, in the first 10 minutes, Croatia pretty much enacted a full-court press on England to say: "Right, let's see how you like it trying to play out from the back if we really press up on you like this."

It's not that likely that Ghana are going to play that way — quite interesting forward back-and-forth, which I thought quite suited England's players, who are very athletic, can run really really well. But in those tighter games where you have to manoeuvre the ball quickly, it's going to be fascinating to see how they get on.

Anthony Barry：英格兰的"隐藏武器"

And without us falling into tactical analysis — it is nice as England fans, isn't it, to see us tactically at half-time obviously get a bit of a boot and come out playing a way that was pretty expansive and impressive.

I did think it was very interesting that that half-time interview with Anthony Barry. For those who don't know, Anthony Barry is the assistant manager of England. So Thomas Tuchel is the head coach; his assistant is an English guy called Anthony Barry, who's not very well known because he's never actually managed before.

Well known to me and Chelsea fans — and that was actually where he met Tuchel.

That's right. And has now moved on with him — so he went with him to Bayern Munich, he's moved on with him. So he's somebody who retains ambitions to become a manager. He's not one of these assistants who wants to stay as an assistant — he's very clear: ultimately I want to be a manager. But Tuchel's taken him to such heights as an assistant that it would be kind of like, crikey, do I really want to go and start my managerial career in League One when I can be England assistant manager?

助理教练的哲学：忠诚 vs 增值

Anyhow — I'm a great believer that generally speaking, football managers betray themselves by their weak choices of assistant. They like to surround themselves by people who they think are quote "loyal" — right? — which basically means: when I'm in danger of getting the sack, does this guy brief about me to the board, or does he have my back? Right? That's how most English football managers think about the assistant.

This is not so much the case on the continent. The tradition on the continent is that the assistant manager is a much meatier position. So Tuchel views that position as critical. He wants to have an assistant who is value-additive to him.

那个 fuming 的中场采访

So I thought it was so interesting at half-time in that game against Croatia to see Anthony Barry taking the half-time interview, and rather than the usual bromides of sort of like, "well, you know, game of two halves, Gary, you know, all this type of stuff" — he was absolutely fuming.

He was fuming.

I — he was fuming much harder than most England fans. I think most England fans looked at that first half and said, you know, "crikey, okay, looked okay going forward, but looked a little bit shaky at the back" — you know, this type of stuff, basically what we'd expected. But he had isolated a very particular element, a psychological element, which meant that England weren't actually playing the game plan that he and Tuchel had laid down — because he felt that if they did enact that game plan, they beat Croatia comfortably.

And you could feel that level of professional obsession about him that the very top football people have. The very top football people are so detail-oriented — not just football people, but other sports as well, but talk about football — are very detail-oriented, and they are obsessive. They are absolutely obsessive, and they're much more focused on performance than result.

In other words, I think that you would have got the same half-time interview from Barry if England had been 2-0 up as 2-2 or even 2-1 behind, because he's looking at the performance level and what he and Tuchel want them to do.

标准如何在组织内部传染

And that just gave me a little bit of hope for this tournament — which is, and we're going to come on to the moment about how amateurishness infects an entire elite sporting organization internally and makes it almost impossible for that organization to perform well, but the reverse is also true.

When the standards are being set constantly at a certain level internally, everyone starts puffing their chest up and saying, "Oh okay, that's what it's like. This is serious. Like these guys are serious. We might do quite well with these guys, right? I need to look at myself a bit here. I need to make sure that I'm not the one letting the side down, because clearly the standards are going to be kept right up here."

So I'm just going to be really interested to see how this goes. That partnership looks to me to be an elite partnership — Tuchel and Barry.

5–10% 理论：俱乐部 vs 国家队

So someone asked a question specifically to your 10 to 15% theory at club level of how much influence a manager has. They said: "What would you say? Well, 5% — does that increase when you move to international football?"

I think in international football, even more, it's about the quality of the players that you have. But again, I think that managers — when I say there's a 5 to 10% difference, I really mean on the upside, but there can be a much more considerable difference on the downside if a manager gets it wrong.

So if a manager sets the team up wrong and doesn't enable the players to get the best out of them, effectively, then that can be quite a significant deterioration in performance.

I'm really talking about: let's just assume that par performance is that the manager sets them up pretty well, that he's not a terrible man-manager, that the whole thing is sort of okay. The difference between that and excellence is 5 to 10%. The difference between that and a manager getting it wrong — well, that can be anything. You can fall off a cliff when a manager really gets it wrong.

I mean, ultimately, as we all know, managers at international level don't get as much time with the players as they do at club level. So the softer skills — in terms of man management, in terms of how you extract what you need to extract from players, because you aren't going to be able to coach in the patterns of play —

Was that the Southgate skill, really?

Southgate vs Tuchel：reset 与 extract

Well, I was always a bit skeptical about that. I think what we saw with Southgate was that when he came in, there was a need for a reset. Okay. Things had been going wrong, broadly speaking, for English national team football. And I think that he did bring back a sense of "team England" — we're all in it together, we can all be professional, we can all enjoy it more, and that stuff. I think that was all absolutely fair enough.

But elite man management is what Tuchel's done with Bellingham. That's different. That can be quite harsh sometimes. That can be sometimes hanging somebody out to dry to then bring them back in with fire in their belly.

Now, you know, because Tuchel's a manager at Chelsea — it's in those moments that they extract their 5 to 10%. Right? That's when you get a Tuchel who's 5 to 10% better than Southgate, which his career clearly indicates that he is. I don't think anyone doubts that the clubs that employ Tuchel are not about to appoint Gareth Southgate.

So that's when you see it — it's in those moments, and then in that moment with that interview with Barry, where his choice of a really strong assistant means it's not all on Tuchel in the dressing room. Tuchel can sit back, let Barry take the half-time team talk, and he can then observe how the players are reacting to it. It's these little details.

美国队、Pochettino，与 Ken Griffin 的支票

It's — and clearly America realised that, because the USA have had a really good start, which obviously is great for the competition because you want the home nation — one of the home nations — to be performing well. And actually all of them are doing pretty well.

They've all qualified.

They've all qualified. And the USA have obviously had Pochettino, I think, for the last nearly two years. But as I said to you, there's a really interesting thing around Pochettino which plays right to the heart of, you know, America and how they view business and sport and philanthropy.

Because Pochettino is currently on a $6 million a year contract, which is well above what the US Soccer Federation would be able to pay him. It's a non-profit organization. And so in classic American way, a billionaire — hedge fund billionaire, Citadel CEO, who we'll talk about Citadel shortly — Ken Griffin — has played a huge part in funding the salary of Pochettino. He's basically ponied up the difference, hasn't he. Was one other mate of his as well.

There was — Ken Griffin's worth $51 billion at present. So you know, the 6 million salary is not going to stretch too far. But he actually stated that, you know, the logic: growth of the game, better results, expand US Soccer's US reach, and makes players kind of role models for millions of American children, while soccer is still trying to develop its place within that ecosystem.

为什么把边际优势留给运气？

So why leave that little bit to chance? — is what he's saying, right? For me as a multi-multi-billionaire, lobbing philanthropically a few million bucks at the US Soccer Federation is not a big deal. But in terms of our chances of the US Men's National Team doing well enough at the World Cup to engage the attention of the whole country, and to drive the sport in the whole country, is transformational.

And we keep on talking about marginal differences. I don't think anyone would doubt that the difference between Poch and the type of coach who they would be able to hire — I think previously their managers have been on sort of five or six hundred thousand bucks a year.

Yeah. Last one was up to a million.

Right. So you're not going to attract an elite manager at that level. So the difference between that and Poch is absolutely giving the US the best possible chance.

You know, obviously works nicely for Ken, because the gifts are tax-deductible. So he gets a bit of a halo, and he gets a nice little tax break as well. Mind you, even as we said — tax breaks on 6 million or less when you've got 51 billion of the stuff — but working well for them. It'll be really interesting to see how far they can take it.

收尾

Um, so yeah — World Cup.

Let's see where we are. Enthusiastic next week if we can —

Actually, well, yeah — Panama would have played England as well by that time, so all the groups will be…

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、99% 出席率：一场关于"会计语言"的认知战
最反直觉的洞察是：FIFA 并没有撒谎，它只是用了**与观众完全不同的字典**。
"Reported attendance（报告出席）"在足球俱乐部 CEO 的内部账本里，从来就不是"现场人头数"，而是**售票口径**——只要票从 FIFA 手里转移到代理机构、赞助商、企业客户手里，它就在"会计意义上"被消化了。这本质上和上市公司"sell-in vs sell-through"（厂家发货 vs 终端卖出）的把戏一模一样：宝洁、可口可乐财报里那些"渠道库存"，FIFA 翻译成体育语言就是"空座位"。
所以 99% 与现场目测的 85–90% 并不矛盾——**它们是两个不同维度的真相**。真正值得警惕的不是数字本身，而是 FIFA 第一次把 B2B 的会计动作，包装成 B2C 的情感故事卖给球迷。
## 二、FIFA Logo 横扫屏幕：一个被忽视的"品牌僭越"信号
Charlie 注意到的那个细节——精彩瞬间转场用的是 FIFA Logo 而不是 World Cup 2026 Logo——比 99% 的争议更值得玩味。

item.title: [All-In Podcast] Socialists Sweep NYC, China Catches Up in Coding, AI Memory Crunch, Micron's Blowout Quarter
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反常识的观察：DSA 不是"穷人的革命"，而是"过剩文凭阶层"的政治起义

把 DSA 简单理解为"穷人造反"是被表象骗了。播客里有一个被几位嘉宾从不同角度反复触碰、却没被明说出来的真相——DSA 的选民基本盘是"向下流动的高学历白人"，而不是工人阶级、黑人或拉美裔（后者实际上正在流失给共和党）。

这其实是一个被低估的"学历通胀"后果：当大学不断扩招、博士产能远超学术岗位时，社会就批量生产出一批"消费习惯像中产、收入和资产像穷人"的过剩文凭人口。他们既无法回到工人阶级身份认同，又被资本市场拒之门外，于是必然在意识形态上寻找出路——而马克思主义恰好给了他们一个"我是先锋队，不是失败者"的叙事容器。

跨领域的连接：这跟历史上所有大规模革命运动的"知识分子—边缘人结盟"结构惊人一致——从1789年的法国律师与巴黎暴民、到1917年的俄国知识分子与士兵水兵、到1960年代美国校园新左派与黑豹党。革命的火种从来不是最穷的人点燃的，而是"教育水平和经济地位严重错配"的中间层点燃的。Mamdani 现象只是这条历史规律在 TikTok 时代的最新一次复现。

二、最出人意料的系统级关联：HBM 内存的物理瓶颈 = 社会的"缓冲时间"

Gavin Baker 顺嘴说了一句几乎被略过的话，但它其实是整集最深的洞察：DRAM 涨价、iPhone 涨价、数据中心成本通胀，可能反而是一件好事，因为它给社会争取了适应 AI 的时间。

把这个想法推到底，你会得到一个让人毛骨悚然的系统性发现：硅的物理极限正在充当人类社会的"减速带"。如果 HBM 像软件一样可以无限复制，AI 资本支出曲线会是纯指数的——社会契约根本来不及调整，就业、税收、教育全都会被冲垮。但因为 HBM 是"科学与魔法的交界"（Gavin 原话），全世界只有三家公司能做，所以扩张速度被硬卡在了物理工程节奏上。

这个连接更狠的地方在于：第一个真正决定 AI 革命速度的，不是算法、不是算力、不是能源，而是"原子级别提纯的硅"这种你能拿在手里的固体物质。 一个号称要数字化一切的革命，最后被一块物理晶圆决定了节奏。这是典型的"软件吃掉世界，但被硬件回吃了一口"的递归讽刺。

跨领域映射：这与生物学里"代谢限速酶"的角色一模一样——整个生化反应链再快，瓶颈酶的速率决定了所有上游分子的命运。HBM 就是 AI 经济的"代谢限速酶"，而它的扩产速度，可能是未来五年决定社会能否承受 AI 转型的最关键变量——比白宫任何 AI 行政令都重要。

三、被所有人忽略的隐藏闭环：Mamdani 的崛起、Anthropic 的监管游说、SpaceX 的轨道计算，是同一个故事

播客里这三个看似不相关的话题，其实构成了一个完整的反馈环：

  1. Mamdani 们之所以崛起，部分原因是 AI 焦虑被"反 AI 团体"（Sacks 暗示由 Anthropic 系资助）放大成了"AI 抢走年轻人未来"的民粹叙事。
  2. Anthropic 的"FAA for AI"游说，反过来又把 AI 推向了一个被政府审批锁死的赛道——而中国的 GLM 5.2 已经追到 Opus 4.8 水平，对手不在你的司法管辖内。
  3. SpaceX 的轨道数据中心计划，则是对前两件事的终极对冲：当地面被政治、能源、监管三重锁死时，唯一的解法是把算力发射到法律之外的真空里。

Jason 没意识到他自己开了一个绝妙的玩笑：当 Mamdani 在地球上谈"公共所有制"时，Musk 已经在筹划把生产资料搬到地球大气层以外了。这是人类历史上第一次，"逃离监管"从一个比喻变成了字面意义上的物理位移。

跨领域的类比：这与中世纪欧洲犹太银行家被驱逐时把资本搬到威尼斯、与香港制造业北上深圳、与加密货币逃往新加坡——本质上是同一种"管辖权套利"模式的太空版本。每一次政治氛围向左收紧，资本和技术都会找到一个新的"无主之地"。这次，那块无主之地恰好在 400 公里高的近地轨道上。

而这意味着一个真正可怕的预测：未来十年最关键的政治冲突，可能不是左右之争，而是"地表司法权 vs 轨道司法权"之争。当 Mamdani 想征收企业 50% 的股权时，那家企业的核心计算资产可能已经在他无法引渡的轨道上每 90 分钟绕地球一圈。这是科幻小说里才有的画面，但 Gavin 用电子表格算过——它在经济上已经成立了。

📝 转录文本

开场：嘉宾介绍

All right, everybody. Welcome back. Welcome back to the number one podcast in the world, the All-In podcast, episode 278.

And uh Freeberg, he took a mental health day today after the socialist sweep in New York City. So, we invited two guests. They both said yes.

Travis Kalanick is here from Adams. How you doing brother?

I'm good. I'm good. Good to see you.

Good to see you. Good to see you. And after a triumphant week at Starbase, the one, the only, everybody's favorite, Gavin Baker of Atreides Management. How are you doing, Gavin?

Great, man. Thanks for having me.

You're still floating on Cloud 9 after the SpaceX IPO.

Yeah, that was a very special moment and it was a culmination of, you know, some decades of hard work.

But man, to quote uh Bill Belichick — onto Cincinnati.

Yeah. Yeah. Exactly right.

Yeah. Zero zero — that's how the Knicks talked about the next game in every series. They're like, "It's 0-0. We come into this as if it's like the first game of the series, even if we're up two games or three games."

话题一：社会主义者横扫纽约民主党初选

The socialists have swept New York in the congressional Democratic primaries. On Tuesday, New York City Mayor Mamdani went three for three in the candidates which he endorsed, and they all won their primaries.

第10区 — leader Brad Lander defeated two-term incumbent Dan Goldman. The 10th is one of New York's richest districts — includes the West Village, all those townhouses, Wall Street, Dumbo, Cobble Hill, Carroll Gardens, Park Slope. That's some weird geography put together there.

第13区 — In New York's 13th, Giovalier beat a five-term incumbent who was backed by House Speaker Hakeem Jeff. And apparently the socialists are coming for him. The 13th is one of the poorest districts — Harlem and the West Bronx, the Boogie Down Bronx. She's a 32-year-old Democrat socialist with a history of spicy remarks.

第7区 — New York's 7th district. Claire Valdez won the open seat over the handpicked successor of the incumbent. The 7th is a DSA stronghold — Bushwick, Williamsburg, Long Island City, Greenpoint, known as the Commie Corridor. A lot of hipsters and baristas with suspenders in that neighborhood.

According to our partners Polymarket, this was a pretty big upset. The Mamdani sweep chances were just 26% before election day. Yeah, that would be like the trifecta there if you were gambling.

These candidates, just like Mamdani did, did a lot better with younger, college-educated, and high-income folks — folks who can afford to be socialists. And these are all safe Democratic seats. The DSA will very likely win.

That's the best way you just said — people who can afford to be socialist. It's always the rich poors, you know, they're rich but they pretend to be poor. It's perfect.

I mean, this is the history of our country from the 30s, the 50s, Red Scare, Black Panthers. I mean, we'll get into it, Chamath, but basically, if you're intelligent and you get into business, you become a capitalist. If you're super intelligent and you go into academia, eventually you have the luxury of belief that socialism is awesome.

Chamath：硅谷在 AI 叙事上输掉了战场

What's your take here, Chamath? And then I'll go to you, Sax Poo, cuz I know I can see you're chomping on something over there. You're just ready to go.

Honestly, I think that we are losing the script and part of it is because we've been our own worst enemy. I'll just keep saying this — that I think AI is a very good prism into this problem. I think AI is the greatest economic leveler we'll ever find in our lifetime. I think it's the thing that can create the greatest amount of equality. I think that it can even the starting line for every single person on earth.

But we've done such a poor job in representing it, in bringing it to market, in talking about it. We've let all of our own personal trials and tribulations and insecurities and fights spill out into the open. As a result, Silicon Valley has lost even more credibility with the people at large. And in that vacuum, what other people can paint is a picture of how anything other than what capitalism looks like today is a better version of what they see.

And this is why you're seeing, I think, a lot of these people get a lot of momentum. I think if you look at some of the key congressional races, they were a referendum on AI. And the good news is we were able to hold the line in some of these key places, but just barely. In Utah and in New York, there was a couple of very important races where it was essentially Anthropic-funded anti-AI groups against — in some ways — OpenAI-funded pro-AI groups, and the pro-AI groups won.

You just explained that it's a great leveler. Can you give a couple of examples and just expand on that briefly? How is it a great leveler?

The best way to explain it is — I think that the first real major unlock of economic productivity was when the internet and specifically Google went and harvested and collected all the world's knowledge and all the world's information and they made it available via search.

What we figure out though 25 years later, despite the fact that they built a great business, is what was missing was then being able to take that knowledge and information and transform it into expertise and intelligence. And that's effectively what AI does. It takes that world's knowledge and it allows you to act upon it so that every single man, woman, and child has an equivalent Travis Kalanick — in, you know, as his co-founder — a super-founder, this brilliant person that can think through all your problems, can out-engineer people, can out-think people, and they sit beside you and you have that.

And there is no gatekeeping that can prevent you from having that. And so now you're only as good as your ability to direct that energy into something productive that you value. That is an incredibly powerful thing.

And instead we've gotten caught up in doomerism and jobs being lost and you know water being consumed — all of which are lies, all of which are complete fabrications and misinformation. And these have been created in order to specifically help one small set of actors inside the AI race, and it's been fed and funded by those folks. So in this vacuum, Jason, we've allowed all these other people to paint the other version. And right now, the other version looks way more compelling than the current version because the current version has very poor brand ambassadors.

Sacks：DSA 的纲领与建制派民主党的覆灭

Sax, what's your take on this? I guess some people are looking at this as the left's version of the populist takeover that Trump did over the last 10 plus years. What's your take on what we're seeing here with socialism and its amazing appeal and it's winning at the uh election box?

I think there is some truth to that. I mean, I think the choices of the future are going to be communism — or if you want to call it socialism — in the Democrat party, or nationalism in the Republican party. I mean, that is where we're headed. Those are the two populist directions.

But let's look at what these DSA candidates stand for. So, let's look at what their platform is. They actually say they want to abolish the Senate. They want to abolish the carceral state — that means basically police forces and prisons. They want to abolish ICE and grant amnesty for all. They do not support any deportations whatsoever. They want to replace the president and Supreme Court with an executive and judiciary that is chosen by and subordinate to Congress, which basically now I guess just means this house.

And with respect to House elections, they want to abolish the electoral college. They want to replace the two-party system with a multi-party democracy. And they want to expand the House of Representatives, implement proportionate representation and ranked choice voting in all elections.

So this would be a total makeover of our constitutional system. They want to free Palestine. They want public ownership of major corporations. They want to defund the Department of War. This is a very radical organization. And you would laugh at a lot of these types of proposals, but you can't really laugh at it anymore because these guys are taking over the Democratic party.

And you can see the Democratic establishment is in complete panic right now because they have lost control of the party to Zohran Mamdani and his allies.

So Jason, like you said, let's take this one race here, New York 13. You've got this ally of Hakeem Jeff, longtime incumbent congressman, Espaillat I guess is his name. He is the chair of the Congressional Hispanic Caucus. And he was defeated by an unemployed 32-year-old PhD candidate. She's never had a job. She's been in college for 10 years, I guess, writing this PhD thesis.

And I think even by DSA standards, she might be kind of a lunatic. So, she has declared that she wants to end Western civilization. She wants to eradicate Western civilization.

Wait a second. What?

We're soaking in it.

Yeah. She actually said she used the American flag as a napkin to clean her hands.

She attended a rally one day after October 7th celebrating the slaughter of Israeli civilians. I mean, she's very pro-Palestine, but even to the point of celebrating Israeli civilian deaths. She calls white women "ugly colonizers." She's called for the complete defunding of the police and abolishing all prisons and borders. Doesn't want a single deportation. Hates the police, openly calls them "pigs," or has on social media before. Calls US service members "war criminals," and says the US is a "disgrace of a country." She's written favorably about communism and seizing the means of production, and on and on and on.

So, this is basically the new Democratic party. Even if the Democrats do take the house in November and Hakeem Jeff becomes speaker, this is going to prove to be a huge headache for him managing all these new DSA members because they do not actually see the traditional establishment wing of the Democratic party as an ally. They see it as an obstacle.

This is the DSA co-chair Josh Block: said, "We're using the Democratic Party as a ballot access vehicle."

Oh my gosh.

Not because we share its goals. We build our own organization, get elected under the Democratic label, caucus with Democrats when it's useful, and push our own agenda from the inside. We see the Democratic establishment as an obstacle, not a home.

So, the DSA is coming for the Democratic Party. It controls the base now. It's where all the energy is. I think this takeover will continue. I think the DSA will gradually take over more and more of the Democratic party.

And all I can say to these establishment Democrats is — play stupid games, win stupid prizes. You supported this open border policy that brought in this wave of mass migration. That is a huge part of the base of this new DSA wing. Mamdani would not have won the mayoral election in New York if it had just been native-born New Yorkers voting. It was the mass migrant vote in New York that swung it to Mamdani.

It's not exclusively the DSA base. It's the migrants plus these overeducated white progressives — I say overeducated because they're more downwardly mobile. They end up going to work in academia or NGOs, that kind of thing. They're hard left-wing. They're kind of the vanguard. And it's this combination of these recent college graduates who are kind of the organizers and this migrant movement who are really taking over the Democratic party.

But again, this all goes back to Democratic Party policies. They may not have intended for this and they may not have intended for them to lose control, but it was their open border policy. It was also the fact that they have cracked the melting pot and the policy we had for many years in America of assimilating migrants, right? Immigrants.

Yep.

That all got cracked by multiculturalism and wokeism. We don't really do that anymore. So now you've got these candidates like Giovalier openly declaring they're not just anti-American, they're post-American. They don't have any respect for the American system, our constitutional order, our free enterprise system. They want to introduce something much different and it's going to look a lot like the countries where these migrants are coming from.

Yeah.

So again, if you import massive numbers of migrants, don't assimilate them into our system, and then you have these Marxist leaders, you're going to end up with the American system coming to resemble the countries from which they came.

It's really interesting. You mentioned how she wants to get rid of all of these units. It's very similar to Trump's playbook — he wanted to delete a separate set of things like the IRS and USAID, Department of Education, EPA. They each use that playbook as well, like, "Hey, we're going to delete some of this stuff."

Travis, you uh famously had —

Trump didn't want to delete the US Constitution.

No, definitely not.

Slight difference.

Slight difference. I agree. He didn't want to delete Western culture.

I don't think he wants to delete Western culture. No, you might want to delete USAID because it was a festering — it was basically a front for all these NGOs, the CIA.

Yeah. By the way, you may have noticed that in South America, we've had seven elections where the countries have swung to the right. And I think a big part of the reason why might be — you don't have the USAID there conducting all these left-wing regime change operations. The polarity disappeared and the incentives disappeared with it.

Travis：真相和正义是社会的免疫系统

Travis, you uh famously had The Fountainhead and Rand's novel as your Twitter avatar for a decade or two.

No, they've been writing about it for a decade or two. This is like a six-month thing where I would read a book and then rotate my avatar.

Yeah. You had Hamilton for a while. I had Ender's Game for a while. But yeah, it became — I mean you, having known you for a while, I think you believe in the individual and their exceptionalism and trying to have a little ruggedness there. This is obviously the exact opposite. What's your take on what's happening in these pockets? Cuz it's not national yet, but it's definitely notable.

All right. I've got two aphorisms for us. Okay.

First is: truth and justice is the immune system for society. When the immune system is suppressed, all the social ills flare up. Okay? So if you're seeing us losing truth — like social media, mainstream media, whatever you call it — that's an early indicator of bad things happening in society. And it's not just social media, mainstream media, it's just everything around us.

And the same on the justice side too. If people commit crimes and there's no consequences, it's a nice early indicator. So you can watch these things and say: is truth winning today or is it losing? Is justice winning today or is it losing? And what is the trajectory will tell you — are we going to get worse? Are we going to get better?

Right. COVID makes — and the Fauci case now we're seeing a lot more information come out. We have a really hard time getting the truth and maybe we'll get justice eventually. It seems like we're getting a little more truth 5 years later, but it's like a perfect example of what you're discussing.

Yeah, there's a lot packed into that, but you can unpack it. If we had a lot of time, we could unpack it. But those become the sort of atomic things that you look at.

And then the other thing I'd say — and some people get a little surprised when I say this — which is: communism is in all of us. Communism is in our blood as humans.

And people go, "What the hell are you talking about? You're crazy. What do you mean?"

Well, I'm like, "Well, have you ever in your life been lazy?" And everybody's like, "Yes, I've been lazy in my life before." I said, "Have you ever in your life wanted something for nothing?" Yeah. The difference is, do you make that a way of life? And when you have ecosystems that essentially allow you to do both of those things without consequence, those ecosystems get a critical mass and start taking hold. And I think that's what we're seeing.

Gavin：DSA 是富裕白人下行流动者的运动

Well said. Yeah, I think it's well said. Uh Gavin, I I know you uh I don't know you to talk too much about politics, but talk about markets, but these two things do relate, I think. So give us the economic perspective here on why this is happening because obviously different generations have had different economic experiences.

Yeah. Well, so I do think — obviously AI is going to be the defining political issue of the midterms and for sure the next presidential election. But I feel like what is going on with the DSA is really a fusion of two things.

So, the Democratic Party — I was a Democrat for most of my life — and it was the party of the working-class people that was trying to create opportunities, you know, maybe level out inequality, help black Americans, help Hispanic Americans. And, you know, you can agree or disagree with their methods, but I think those are all noble goals. And to a large extent, none of those are really present in the DSA.

None of them. If you look at who voted for which candidate, the voting base of the DSA are relatively wealthy white liberals who are downwardly mobile. They're losing votes with working-class people. They're losing votes with poor people. They're losing votes with black Americans. They're losing votes with Hispanic Americans — those people that the Democratic Party, I think, for a long time tried, whether they failed or succeeded, tried to represent and give a voice to — they are not of interest to the DSA.

And I think the DSA is dangerous. It's tragic that we are electing profoundly anti-American candidates who in some cases have called for violence to eradicate America. And there's a whole class of people who went to an elite school. They grew up in really nice circumstances and instead of going into industry, they went into this kind of giant NGO nonprofit machine and their outcomes have been very different from people who did productive things for the world.

You know, I've said on this show many times — Elon has done more to decarbonize the planet than every activist combined.

Yeah. Times 10, by the way.

Times 10. And I think these NGOs and the fact that the government is increasingly outsourcing a lot of funding to them is a really big part of this problem, and they are pursuing policies — you know, I think we maybe talked about it last time, but the Curley effect, a mayor of Boston — you pursue policies that you know are going to be disastrous for your constituents, but they drive out your rivals. And then you can give jobs, $600,000-a-year jobs, run an NGO that does nothing productive, to your friends and allies. And it's really organized corruption happening at a massive scale.

You know, whatever you want to say about the Trump administration — a lot of people are up in arms about this new plane he's getting from the Qataris. Well, one, sounds like we needed a new plane, but two, it's right there for you to see. I think there are tens of billions, maybe hundreds of billions of these payments flowing to these NGOs. And if you look in California and New York, the per capita spending on homelessness has more than doubled and it's all gone to NGOs, and outcomes have gotten worse. In California, it's quadrupled and outcomes have gotten worse.

So, I think this is dangerous and I hope that the mainstream Democrats can find a compelling candidate. Because I think the reason that the DSA is increasingly ascendant is not because of their ideas. I think their ideas appeal to this very narrow subset of downwardly mobile rich white people who increasingly maybe live in quasi-genteel poverty because they're not doing productive things. They may be well-intentioned but they're not doing productive things.

I think the reason they're ascendant is Zohran Mamdani. I think he is one of the most talented politicians I've ever seen in my lifetime. He can give a great speech. He's good in an interview. He can tap into all of this. He's kind of a chameleon who can shift.   • 744 billion parameters
  • 1 million token context window

The most open source. The most open source of all open source.

关键成绩： - Scored 51 points on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index — the highest score of any open-weight model ever - Stacks up nicely next to the frontier models - Beat GPT 5.5 on the frontier SWE coding benchmark - Trails Claude Opus 4.8 by less than 1 percentage point - API usage cost much cheaper — 85% cheaper in fact than GPT 5.5 for comparable performance

Z.ai founder told Elon Musk open-weight Fable capabilities will be here sooner than Q1 2027.

Gavin：蒸馏（distillation）已经发生，未来属于组合模型

I do think how good GLM 5.2 is has challenged some of my beliefs. And there was a great post from a TPU engineer that for sure distillation has happened. There's been an immense amount of distillation. No question.

Please explain to the audience what that means.

Distillation is when you have — you know, we all have seen videos of these Chinese iPhone farms. Just picture a farm like that. Tens of thousands of phones, iPads, and computers that are asking the cloud API through masked accounts very specific questions, and then these what's called reasoning traces are being harvested because if you're on the API, you get to see every token.

And those reasoning traces are then fed back into the model during the reinforcement learning process and probably during the pre-training process. And that is a way that you can get really, really close to the frontier at a fraction of the cost. And this is for sure going on and has gone on for a long time.

It's a cheat sheet. It's a cheat sheet for other models to catch up.

It would be like asking Google every question you could ever imagine, every search imaginable, getting all the results and then putting your own search engine together to make it very simple.

Exactly.

But now that this model is so good, it is good enough to do its own RL, and the cat may be out of the bag. Now I don't think we really know how good Mythos is. We don't really know how good the next OpenAI model is. We haven't seen the next SpaceX model. So maybe that gap opens up again.

But either way, I profoundly believe the future is composable models and every enterprise — you're going to have a what Andrej Karpathy called "a council of LLMs." You're going to have Grok, you're going to have Anthropic, you're going to have OpenAI, Google. You're going to have at least two of those. I would argue Grok should always be one of the two because of its dedication to the truth and it will tell you as a business owner a politically inconvenient truth that you need to know for your data.

But you're also going to have your own open-weights model that you trained on your data. And you're going to put those two together — the frontier models and your own model — and you are going to get real Pareto-dominant outcomes. And half the queries are going to go to the open source model — maybe 85% — and only the hardest ones are then maybe they all go to open source first and only the hardest ones are then checked by the frontier models.

So I think this is the future. It's coming. And a misconception that a lot of people have is that open-source models are somehow bad for AI. They're awesome for the AI infrastructure providers. They just shift economic value from the margins of the frontier labs to the infrastructure. And that's not bad for AI.

That's great for them.

It's great for them. But I do think there's still a role for these frontier models, and it may be true to date frontier tokens are capturing 90% of the economic value and open-source tokens are probably 80%+ of tokens processed — and those ratios may be here to stay — but I just think composable models are the future.

Roll out the patches before they can be exploited.

The reality is that if you just clamp down in a way that doesn't even allow these models to be used, the Chinese are going to have these capabilities imminently anyway. They're already at Opus 4.8 level. And the founder of Z.ai, he said that before Q1, they'll have Fable level capability.

I believe them because the Chinese have been, I'd say, 9 months behind our models, plus or minus 3 months depending on capability. But when they know there's been a breakthrough around something like cyber, they can deploy more resources against that particular problem and catch up faster.

So, like I said, we're on a shot clock here. I've been saying for months that we're on a shot clock. We have to do smart things. We can't just slow everything down because that will not slow down the Chinese. They're not under our jurisdiction. We have to basically get these tools in the hands of our cyber security industry. They're the force multiplier. They're the enabler. We have to basically go out and do this big upgrade cycle quickly because we only have a few months left.

They're six months behind on the model and they're 24 months behind on the silicon yet they're only a few months behind in total. So what game are we playing? This is insane. We are going to lose if we keep doing this stuff to ourselves.

Sacks：中国押注华为芯片、AI 即包装出口

So let me make a point about that. So on the silicon, there's been a huge push in China by the government to push their AI labs to develop and train on Huawei chips. And look, you can take these claims with a grain of salt — maybe they're not true — but it was claimed that DeepSeek V4 was trained on Huawei chips. And now Z.ai — they are saying that the GLM 5 family was trained entirely on clusters of Huawei Ascend 910B chips.

So now look, maybe they're lying, maybe they smuggled in some Nvidia chips, but the claim is that this was all done on indigenous chips. And what I believe is that China is engaged in a strong indigenization push right now. They want to prop up Huawei as the national champion. They want all their companies using Huawei chips.

They still need to scale some of the manufacturing, but they're going to do that pretty quickly. And then what they're going to do is they're going to take these Huawei chips — Huawei-optimized models — remember that GLM 5.2 inference is optimized for the Huawei chips. Okay, we know that. And they are basically going to package these things up. They call it "AI in a box." They're going to sell it at a fraction of the cost globally.

Which is what they do with every technology, right? Better, cheaper, faster.

Or almost as good. And that's another thing — as I've been saying since the beginning of this administration — we have to be pro-export because China is going to be there within one or two years. As I said, we're going to be kicking ourselves because we could have had the whole global market to ourselves. We invented reasons not to sell abroad to our friends and partners. And now China is going to be there imminently.

Yeah. And with a lower price segment.

When you play with this new GLM, you get some really interesting responses. I asked it about the country of Taiwan, was not pleased and didn't give me an answer. I asked it about Tiananmen Square — no answer as well. I'm using the hosted version at Z.ai. But when I asked it places to visit in Paris, it did an exceptional job. Except when I said "make these ideas into an infographic and make me a 3-day agenda," it was like, "Hey, we don't have enough time to do that." And it said use the other model because this model's too busy. But you can go play with it at z.ai.

One quick point about the censorship. There's no question that these Chinese models have, you could say, censorship — and there's political bias in there out of the box. But American companies have taken Chinese models and then essentially worked around and basically fixed the censorship inside their own forked version. So for example, Perplexity did this very early on with Chinese models — they showed that you could sort of put back the content on Tiananmen Square and things like that.

So I think Jal, you're absolutely right about the censorship, but it's not a fatal problem. It's something that American companies can fix when they take an open-source model and fork it and customize it.

Yeah, in the hosted version, you're not going to get a great answer of what agenda you should use for tourism in the great country of Taiwan or your visit to Tiananmen Square.

话题三：Micron 财报炸裂与 HBM 内存的全球瓶颈

All right, let's keep moving here on the dock. Micron smashed their earnings. If you don't know Micron, they are one of only three companies on Earth that make high-bandwidth memory. Micron earnings： - Revenue up 4x year-over-year — $9 billion to $42 billion - Beat expectations by 16% - Big jump in guidance for Q4 — $50 billion versus $43 billion - Their stock is up 10x

I'm not crying in my soup.

You're not crying in your soup.

Everybody knows Apple has been a beneficiary of the run-local-models movement that I'm part of, and people are buying 128 gig, 256 gig MacBook Pros, Mac Studios. But the gig is up apparently because now Apple, which had not passed on those costs to customers, is having to pass those increases on. So everything from the new MacBook Neo, which is their $699 laptop kind of competing with Chromebooks, is now $799, up 14%. Mac Studio up 25%. The costs are going to be very significant. Inflation has come to the desktop.

Your thoughts, Gavin, on Micron and the impact on the industry — is this a temporary bottleneck or does this mean everybody has to get into this business quickly?

Gavin：DRAM 是 AI 革命的"代谢限速酶"

No. Well, DRAM is the most important bottleneck. There's a whole segment of people on X who are very focused on bottlenecks. I call them the "bottleneck bros." They'll do some work with Claude, find some esoteric Japanese company. The bottleneck that matters is DRAM.

DRAM and HBM DRAM — this is the most important bottleneck simply because memory capacity and bandwidth are foundational to the performance of every AI model. So, this is the most important bottleneck. Elon is focusing the TerraFab on memory because he sees it as the most important bottleneck. Not lasers, not capacitors, not power supply semiconductors, not NAND flash, not HDDs — DRAM.

And I think this bottleneck is going to be with us for a while and it is kind of astonishing.

A few thoughts — what was important about the quarter: they announced that they have these SDAs, these supply chain agreements, that have a floor and a ceiling for prices with an increasingly large group of large customers. And this covers essentially 50% of their revenue, I think, with just four customers. And the floor pricing in these new contracts is ahead of prior cycle peaks from a gross margin perspective. And so this is really, I think, maybe end up being very transformational for the industry.

Most other parts of the semiconductor supply chain have rerated. Lam Research — the wafer fab equipment suppliers — they all trade at huge premiums to DRAM relative to prior cycles and their business models have improved. But so has the industry structure and business models of DRAM because HBM DRAM is increasingly a customized chip.

But as far as other people being able to do this — look, CXMT is going public in China. They are going to — they may be the cure for Apple's ills. They will flood the market with to some degree cheap consumer-grade DRAM. But for the DRAM you need in these AI servers, there are three companies that can make it. It's really hard to do. This is as close to magic as science can get.

And TerraFab is going to be an important part of this solution. But these stocks still trade are cross-sectionally cheap relative to the rest of AI.

Something I've been thinking about — memory and DRAM is probably going to be 30 to 40% of all hyperscaler CapEx next year. The hundreds of billions of dollars that are spent going straight to DRAM. It's wild.

But this may actually be very valuable for society because it is probably going to inflate the costs of building a gigawatt data center to the point where even for the hyperscalers, economics matter. We're caught in this prisoner's dilemma. And this may give us as a society time to adapt — to adapt what our friend Brad Gerstner calls the social contract.

So the high iPhone prices — one, CXMT is coming for consumer-grade DRAM. But two, this may be good for AI. It may be good for us as a society.

And making it is just really pure silicon, right? Like making memory is just incredibly refined silicon.

And that might be the pre-bottleneck. Making the HBM DRAM, making what Nvidia calls SOCAMM, making LPDDR — these are the types of DRAM that are really hard to make, not consumer-grade DRAM, and they are increasingly what you need in these AI data centers.

Yeah. Semiconductor grade. Yeah.

Sacks：HBM 堆叠工艺与全球电子产品涨价

So my understanding of HBM stands for high-bandwidth memory. Again, this is part of the GPUs that go in the data center to run AI. Is that you take the DRAM wafer or die and you actually stack them. And so I think HBM 3 is like 8 — it's stacked 8 dies high — but now they're increasing to 12 and even 16. And to basically stack them and then package them all together, that's an advanced technology in and of itself.

So you're seeing now, like Gavin's saying, there's only three companies that can do it. But also, this is creating significant price pressure for all the consumer electronics businesses. Apple had huge news today where they announced massive price increases. And again, it's because DRAM now is less available because it's just being hoovered up by all the data centers.

And if you're a data center and you need to buy GPUs, those chips are using immense amounts of DRAM because one HBM chip is using multiple stacks of DRAM. So it's just getting slurped up and then it takes a couple years to ramp up new capacity. So these companies are going to do that, but that could take a while.

We saw that in New York — remember that was that Micron plant that was had just broken ground and then got shut down the same day because of some crazy environmental issue. So it's not easy to ramp this stuff in the US. Although Micron is the one provider that's in the US. SK Hynix is in South Korea. Samsung's in South Korea too.

But anyway, we're going to see more of this — AI tariff they're calling it — it is in this narrow area of consumer electronics where there's competition for DRAM, it is leading to price inflation.

Now, Microsoft raised the price of the Xbox. You know, it's coming for the Switch. It's coming for the PlayStation. There's demand destruction because of prices in consumer whereas AI demand is relatively price insensitive.

David, I would modify one statement. It's hard to build a Micron. It's hard to build a new fab in a deep blue state. You can build fast.

Why were they trying in New York? It's kind of crazy.

New York gave them all these incentives, but none of those incentives matter. It's a little bit like solar power. You can be as pro-environmental as you want. But if you can't build and install solar because of regulations, it doesn't matter. So maybe that Micron plant ends up getting built in my home state of Texas.

The incentive game has kind of flip-flopped. It used to be the states were courting the factories and the fabs. Now it's the fabs are like, "Which state can actually build this? We'll pay you whatever you want. Just tell us where to send the envelope. We'll drop a couple of envelopes off. It's not a problem."

Gavin, can you say how long it's going to take to stand up the fab at TerraFab?

Well, I mean, if it was a normal fab, it would be a 2, 3, 3-and-a-half year process. But we've seen what Elon has done to other construction processes, and he's starting with some advantages with the Intel partnership. So I don't think anyone knows, but based on past history, he's probably going to stand up TerraFab faster than other fabs have been stood up. But this is really hard. It's the intersection of magic and science. You can't believe how complicated this is. So it's going to be hard. But he has a track record of doing what Jensen called impossible — superhuman. And so we'll see how long it takes.

数据中心成本：从地面到轨道

You know, one other point here that I guess is — it might be relevant to SpaceX AI, although it's don't have to limit it to this — there's an assumption that over time it would get cheaper and easier to stand up new data centers. But what you're saying is actually it might be getting harder. It might be getting more expensive. Because there's competition for these components. The memory is getting more expensive. I'm not sure that the GPUs are getting any cheaper.

I guess some of these transformers, the switch gear —

The energy might be getting cheaper, and then the entitlements are getting harder and the political situation is getting harder. There's very few places you can even stand up new data centers. So, is it the case that actually it's going to get more and more expensive?

100%. So to stand up a 1 gigawatt data center, it's $35 billion in semiconductors — Nvidia semiconductors — and it's $25 billion of power and cooling equipment. And that is clearly inflationary because a lot of that $25 billion is the human labor required to install it.

So the calculation that needs to be done for orbital compute is — it's $35 billion of silicon in each space, in literally outer space and orbit and on ground. But if you can get the cost of launch significantly below that $25 billion, then the math starts to really mass.

And when Starship is reusable, it's going to cost $5 billion to put a gigawatt of compute into space. And something that drives me crazy is people picture these Pentagon-ish data centers. No, it's racks in space linked with lasers. It's kind of a virtual data center in space.

Wait, $5 billion. Is that $5 billion of launch cost or what?

$5 billion of launch cost. Now you're at $40 billion to put the gig into space. You're at $60 billion terrestrially. And the $25 billion that is power and compute is clearly inflationary. So it may be that in three or four years it's $70 billion versus $40 billion. And that $5 billion as Starship becomes rapidly reusable is likely deflationary. So this is the economics that underpin orbital compute from first principles.

And then on an ongoing basis, you are paying maybe a billion dollars a year for the power to run those chips and cool them.

If I had to make a guess, I think what's going to happen is that since 2021 about 40% of all data centers get contested. I think that number is going to go up. You have a couple of issues right now to turn on compute terrestrially. So, assume you have land — that's relatively straightforward. Assuming you can get it zoned, less straightforward. Assuming you can get power, very difficult. Then you have a very critical design decision.

So all of these folks publish these things called the basis of design, and your BOD essentially tells you here's the Anthropic spec, here's the OpenAI spec, here's the CoreWeave spec, here's the AWS spec, here's GCP. And you get these 50- and upwards of 500-page documents of all these technical details.

The issue that we have is — I don't know if you guys have used OpenAI or Anthropic recently where you get the whole thing of "come back later." That "come back later" is completely unacceptable. It just means that they have no compute.

So in trying to find — surge pricing is coming.

So in trying to find this solution, what is happening is these basis of design — the restrictions, the specificity is being relaxed. And one of the key constraints is we've moved to an architectural model from Google and Nvidia that has said look, we have to liquid-cool these racks. These are very complicated big-girth supercomputer racks essentially. And now we're also going back and saying, "You know what, maybe some of these older stuff that's a little bit less proficient and a little bit less useful, but we can air-cool them — is useful." And everybody's like, "Yeah, you know what? We should try to use everything that's available in that second class."

What some very smart people are doing is like, "Wow, well, here's a shipping container that you can just drop on a concrete pad somewhere, plug in the power, and let it rip." And so, Jason, what you're seeing is that level of investment that's happening.

So there are companies like Dell makes these racks, companies like Vertiv makes these modules, and I think if Tesla can make them available, folks like us for my data center project, we would be enormous buyers of these things because we would just literally prefab them in a warehouse — get the chips, prefab them, truck them to the place, crane them in, turn it on, off to the races you go. You have like a 90-day build cycle, which is unheard of.

Gavin：推理的分解（prefill / decode）与硬件复用

And to riff on what Chamath said in all of this — there is actually a startup that is trying to put four GPU units with kind of a battery on people's houses and give them a discount on their power and then you can do inference for that neighborhood from those four GPUs. And it's like lock-sealed so nobody can get in.

But there's another dynamic that I think we should talk about with all of this — and can play into the Megapods and other people are kind of working on data centers. You know Crusoe is working on modularly assembling data centers — kind of like a data center and think of it as like an 18-wheeler shipping container.

But that is the disaggregation of inference into prefill and decode. When a model is answering your question, it's doing two things. The prefill part is understanding the question and its answer thus far. And think of that as: the more you can remember, the bigger your memory capacity — literally, the more words you can remember the better.

Decode is the process of generating the next token and that is a memory bandwidth-bound problem. And think of it as: the faster you can speak, the better.

And these two types of inference are increasingly being disaggregated. And Chamath was an investor in Groq, which Nvidia bought. And they're going to use this — Cerebras is the other solution today that is available. And you can put Groq or Cerebras decode-optimized chips — they both say you can do more than decode on them, and that's true — in front of old Nvidia GPUs like H100s. So you can lift H100s, A100s out of some old data center, put them in one of these Megapods, you know, a rack in a shipping container, put a Groq or Cerebras in front of it, and you can get a very competitive solution.

And so I do think the disaggregation of inference, we're going to be using GPUs for 7 years, 10 years, 12 years, and that's great because it lowers the cost to finance them, which makes this AI revolution more financeable.

Chamath, you want to riff on Groq? Should we —

And I think there are two things that are worth discussing here. One is there is a whole generation of portfolio managers — there's a lot of people who are advocating for kind of squeezing the blood out of the stone on IPO prices. And the flip side of that is that there are a lot of portfolio managers who if a stock breaks deal price, they sell it no matter what. They consider it a promise that was broken.

And so this is what has happened with Cerebras to some degree over the last two days. And this may seem irrational but there are people who run giant funds who I know personally where if a stock breaks deal price, they sell no matter what. And so if a stock breaks deal price, it can sometimes go to places you wouldn't think it would go.

And this means that shorts — if a stock gets close to deal price, they short it because they want to break deal price and then they make a quick 10 or 20%. So it becomes a pile-on because you have this price-insensitive selling that can be triggered. And this is what has happened to Cerebras.

People talk about hate sale — hate selling — but they broke deal price. And so if you're going public and you're listening to this — tell your bankers price this in such a way that we're not going to break deal price in our first nine months as a public company. And that's what I always advise everyone to do and I think it's important. But I also think it takes companies a while to learn how to tell their story and communicate to public markets. It's a very different audience than VCs.

The way I would have respectfully told the Cerebras story — because what happened to Cerebras is they reported a quarter and they're growing fast but relative to the rest of AI they're not growing that fast in the March quarter.

So what I would have said is: we signed this transformational $202-$25 billion (I don't know the exact number) contract with OpenAI in December of 2025. We immediately ordered more wafers from Taiwan Semi — takes 4 months from when we make that order. Taiwan Semi says yes, they start producing — takes 4 months to make the chip. Then it takes us 2 months plus or minus to turn that chip into a server. And then if we're lucky and we can find the power, it takes us a month to energize that chip and start making tokens with it.

So the first time you're going to see the impact of this OpenAI deal at the earliest is probably around Labor Day. So you'll see a little bit of it in the third quarter, but then it really starts to build.

And just really simple math — let's just use some rough numbers. Let's take some Nvidia numbers. It takes them $35 billion to bring on a gigawatt and 15 billion that you can generate — $15 billion in token revenue, in cloud revenue out of that gigawatt. And somebody on the call talked about adding 50 megawatts a month. If they could add 50 megawatts a month in 2027 — forget '26 — that means they exit the year at roughly a $9 billion cloud computing run rate. And we're at less than 40 billion of market cap.

Now, that is going to be really hard to do and they've never done anything like that before. But I would focus, like as an investor, what matters here is not where they sit competitively, not what new demand they can bring on, but just how quickly can they bring on power.

And listen, outside of the hyperscalers, the only companies that have ever brought on more than a gigawatt I think are CoreWeave, Crusoe, and SpaceX AI bringing on 600 megawatts. It's really hard. And that is what I'm focused on as an investor — how many megawatts can they bring on? Because we know what they're going to monetize at. And that is the question. And we'll see.

Yeah. And I think that's well said. Yeah. The other companies that have had this happen of late — I guess Rivian, and famously Chamath — Facebook traded below IPO price in the first year, I think.

And so this isn't necessarily mean a bad company. It just means a lot of hype or maybe going for it in the IPO and pricing it to perfection. And it can go one of two ways. You can either grossly underprice, grossly overprice. And it's really hard to get right. And this is why doing auctions —

Or, you know — yeah, auctions are the way. Auctions are the way. And so it's not hard to price if you just do it the way it's supposed to be done. It's hard to price when you want it to be a certain number.

Yeah. And you have many mouths to feed and that influences the price.

I don't think that happened here. I think there was a good-faith effort to price this thoughtfully, but there was — it was such an in-demand IPO. I think it was a hard IPO to price.

You just auction it, Gavin. You just auction it.

I think that's different than underwriting it. You're underwriting —

But the bankers — like I think they just need to get more in the mode of just doing the auction, you know.

Yeah.

Final reflections：估值规模与 Uber 的"贵宾价"回忆

Final topic was this $3 trillion or actually now it's your $3 trillion call on Anthropic would make it like 6 trillion in offerings. What do we think broadly?

In offerings — because it's they're going to offer a small slice of that.

Sure.

And that small slice is very easy to absorb in the context. I believe we have no precedent. We'll see. I might be wrong, but it's not hard for global capital markets to absorb $50 billion of an offering. It's not like somebody has to come up — it's a 5% float is $150 billion. There's enough —

Guys. Remember when 15 billion used to be a whole lot of money?

Yeah. How weird is what's going on? How weird?

By the way, crazy. Where is all this money coming from?

Oh yeah. Where was it all this time?

Can we just reminisce, man?

We got — my — when I was at Fidelity, my colleagues and I, we got pilloried. I think we priced Uber at $14 billion. And we got pilloried in the press for not knowing what we were doing. And it was seen as — then of course like six months later, I think you guys did a round at, what, like $42 billion or something. But it's like, yeah, $14 billion. That was groundbreaking for a private company back then.

Yeah, for sure. It was 17, but that, you know, no 17. No, you're right. I wanted 14.

And we had like a big negotiation —

And we ran — we just ran an auction.

No, we just ran an auction.

Yeah, you wanted 20 and maybe we landed at 17. What we did was every person who wanted to be involved had to fill out a sheet of how much money they put it at 10, 11, 12, 13, 14 — all the way up to 20. And then we just did the Dutch auction. We said, "We want to clear $1.5 billion." We just did the auction and cleared it. Went back to people and said, "Hey, you're not going to get it. You have another shot." They update their Excel sheet and it just moves the number up a little bit and then you close it down. But yeah, it started — that round started at I think it was 9 or 10 or something like that. Ended up at 17.

Thank you for your service.

Yes, it used to be that was a lot of money and that was unheard of at the time.

And that was — well look, it was 10 years ago when we used to walk to school uphill both ways.

Yes. We used to eat potatoes with no butter, just hot potatoes. Sometimes we were lucky that they were cooked. Sometimes they were raw. No, my dad would tell me that story. It's a famous John the Beard story. He'd say his mom would put four potatoes in the oven. They'd wrap them in tin foil, one in each pocket because they couldn't afford gloves. So, you put your hands in your pocket with the hot potatoes. You get to school, you eat one for breakfast, one for lunch. Him and his sister Johanna — God rest, my aunt who died too young — and they would just go eat these potatoes. That was their life. Walking to school.

Yeah. And our kids are trying both ways.

Yeah. They're trying to get the new iPhone 16 or 17. I don't know.

Remember when it used to be hard to raise $5 billion?

Oh my lord. This is crazy.

Remember when it was hard to raise $1.5 million?

Dude, I remember. Yeah. I remember when it was hard to raise. Anyways, we don't have to do this. It's okay. We already sound like grandpas. It's fine.

We do sound like — it was so hard to raise that first 1.5.

Dude, it's just — we don't need to go there. It's so funny though.

What's with this art piece, Gavin, behind you? Is that like an art piece or you made it?

Yeah.

No, this is —

I'm staying at a rented house in Atherton.

Oh, okay. There you go. It's just Arabian art.

结束语

All right, everybody. For the dictator Jamal Pitia and for David Sacks TKGB. We'll see you next time on the All-In podcast. Bye-bye. Great job everybody.

We'll let your winners ride. Rainman David. We open sourced it to the fans and they've just gone crazy with it. Love you. I want your winners. Besties are gone. That is my dog taking your driveways. Oh man, my appetiter will meet up. We should all just get a room and just have one big huge orgy cuz they're all just useless. It's like this like sexual tension that we just need to release somehow. Your feet. We need to get merch. Going all in. I'm going all in.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反常识的观察：DSA 不是"穷人的革命"，而是"过剩文凭阶层"的政治起义
把 DSA 简单理解为"穷人造反"是被表象骗了。播客里有一个被几位嘉宾从不同角度反复触碰、却没被明说出来的真相——**DSA 的选民基本盘是"向下流动的高学历白人"**，而不是工人阶级、黑人或拉美裔（后者实际上正在流失给共和党）。
这其实是一个被低估的"**学历通胀**"后果：当大学不断扩招、博士产能远超学术岗位时，社会就批量生产出一批"消费习惯像中产、收入和资产像穷人"的过剩文凭人口。他们既无法回到工人阶级身份认同，又被资本市场拒之门外，于是必然在意识形态上寻找出路——而马克思主义恰好给了他们一个"我是先锋队，不是失败者"的叙事容器。
跨领域的连接：这跟历史上所有大规模革命运动的"知识分子—边缘人结盟"结构惊人一致——从1789年的法国律师与巴黎暴民、到1917年的俄国知识分子与士兵水兵、到1960年代美国校园新左派与黑豹党。**革命的火种从来不是最穷的人点燃的，而是"教育水平和经济地位严重错配"的中间层点燃的**。Mamdani 现象只是这条历史规律在 TikTok 时代的最新一次复现。

item.title: [a16z] This is New Media
item.dc:creator: a16z
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：「合法性焦虑」其实是旧媒体最后的护城河

视频里那句话特别值得停下来："legacy media somehow is like where the respectability is." 表面看，这是关于新旧媒体的代际更替；但更深一层，旧媒体真正卖的从来不是内容，而是「被允许存在的许可证」。

当 a16z 说"不再相信合法性来自传统媒体"，他们其实是在做一件比"创办播客"激进得多的事——拆除创作者大脑里那个看不见的审稿编辑。这个内化的编辑，比任何外部审查都更高效地压制表达。所以新媒体革命的真正战场不在 YouTube 或 TikTok，而在每个创作者起床后第一念头里：「这个能发吗？体面吗？」一旦这个念头消失，旧媒体就死了——不是因为没人看，而是因为没人再需要它来盖章。

二、跨领域连接：虚拟制作 × 加密货币 × 米其林餐厅，竟然是同一件事

视频提到"4 周内做虚拟制作，没人做过，但我们喜欢疯狂的想法"——这听起来是制作花絮，其实暴露了一个跨行业的统一模式：

旧范式 新范式 共同结构
好莱坞剧组 6 个月预制 虚拟制作 4 周上线 时间压缩 → 风险前移到决策瞬间
银行 IPO 18 个月路演 加密项目 token 发行 48 小时 同上
米其林 3 年备战评审 Pop-up restaurant 一晚试菜 同上

真正的"新媒体"不是介质的更新，而是「决策—执行—反馈」三段循环被压缩到几乎重合的状态。a16z 不是在做内容公司，他们在做一种金融逻辑的延伸：每一次内容发布等同于一次小型 VC 押注——快速下注、公开验证、立刻迭代。所以他们做媒体的方式，本质和他们做风投是同构的。这才是为什么他们能做、传统媒体不能做。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：「品牌」和「公司」正在反向重合

视频里有一句被很多人会略过的话："the brands were the companies." 在旧媒体里，CNN 这个品牌等于 CNN 这家公司。但在新媒体里，发生了非常诡异的反转——

  • 个人创作者：品牌（人）> 公司（壳）。MrBeast 这个人比 Beast Industries 重要 100 倍。
  • 机构创作者（a16z 是先驱）：公司试图把自己重新人格化，让"a16z"成为一个有声音、有观点、会冒险的"人"。

这意味着 21 世纪后期的组织演化方向是「公司人格化 vs 个人公司化」的双向收敛。中间地带——也就是过去 100 年我们称之为「企业传播」「公关」「企业品牌」的那一整套——正在被掏空。

最讽刺的洞察：当一家 VC 比大多数媒体公司更像媒体公司时，"投资"和"叙事"已经合并成同一个动作。 钱不再追逐故事，钱本身就是故事的一种语法。这是新媒体真正的暗面——不是民主化，而是叙事金融化。

📝 转录文本

旧媒体的局限与遗留焦虑

Old media, you had very restricted channels, very restricted formats, and the brands were the companies.

But there's still this anxiety that people have, which is: legacy media somehow is like where the respectability is, the prestige is.

I I don't believe that anymore, and I think it's very important for people to kind of get that out of their system. The world has changed. It can't be half and half.

全身投入：拥抱疯狂的想法

The whole motion — they pitched to us this idea of working with a virtual production.

And we look at the calendar. This is what — 4 weeks away. No one has ever done this before. It's such a crazy idea.

But we like crazy ideas. So we're like, let's do it.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] Way Catholics Waged War Against the Pope – Ada Palmer
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、"距离决定神圣性"——抽象与具体的反比定律
