低垂果实、AGI 路径与连续学习

主持人： Yeah. And I guess in terms of research ideas and what to work towards, do you think there's still a lot of low-hanging fruit or ideas that can still be improved a lot through just optimizing small parts of already implemented work? Or do you think right now there has to be a lot of research that are completely new bets that people take?

Mark： Um, yeah, that's a really great question. I feel like there are new bets, but probably not that many. Um, in some sense like uh — hopefully you feel like, you know, AGI is coming soon, right? And um I think everyone sees that these models are getting really capable. And — I think if you really imagine the implications of that, we're getting closer and closer to a world where the models can come up with more of the innovations on their own.

Yeah. They can kind of do self-sustained research. This is one of the big org goals that we've set for for our research work. And and so I think like — you know, what really matters is, are there big bets before that point in time? And um, I — I think the window is small, but there are still like some fairly significant ideas we're trying out.

主持人： Yeah. I mean there have been some researchers who have stated that to get to AGI, we still need, let's say, like two or three more breakthroughs, that be like continual learning or some other ideas. Um, do you follow that same view perspective, or do you think it's kind of more so like not as drastic as coming up with like three completely different paradigms?

Mark： Um yeah, I mean I — I don't know. I I don't know if that same framing — like continuous learning is a basic primitive that you have to unlock. Um, there's so many different techniques. I — I don't know — um, that I think, you know, we're trying a lot of ad — limitations of it. I don't know what would consider as a breakthrough versus not, but I think there are clearly many shots on goal, and I I'm pretty sure it'll work.

第二次 Flambé 与多任务能力

主持人： Great. Okay. Great. Okay. So the shrimp is basically done. Awesome. Okay. So do you want to do the flambé thing again to get more color?

Mark： Let's do it. Yeah.

主持人： I'll turn on the heat a little bit and then let me get some more oil. Great. Yeah, cuz we want to get like some dark color. I think mine has in here. Great. And then we can hopefully get another shot. Okay. Yeah. Do you want — do you want to go first? Um — same same amount as — yeah, same amount. We could hopefully get — cuz I think the heat wasn't as — yeah. Okay. Can you put it in? Yeah. Advance. There you go. Just touch the button.

Mark： Great. Wow. There it is. It's good — stretch.

主持人： Yeah, indeed. And it's like — add some good flavor here. Let me try it over here. Yeah. Let's see. Great. All right. Let's see. Let's go. There it is. All right. But we're in the final stretch. We have our shrimp all cooked. Yep. Some fire. So, now we can kind of — yeah, cook it off a little bit, and then um we should add our veg to the water.

Mark： I'm impressed by your multitasking abilities, you know. I think that's actually one thing — um, we need our models to get better at. Like it should just be able to do some thread like this and also just have a conversation with people.

单一模型 vs. 多模态

主持人： Yeah. No, I — that also reminds me — do you think images and audio and video and even text, like, that should all be one under one model? Or do you think it'll like break through like specific specialized like audio model or —

Mark： Well, yeah. I mean, I think for for a research lab, I think there are a lot of advantages for it to being under one. So, um you just have to maintain one infrastructure stack for instance. Um, I think the cost to like maintaining and scaling many infrastructure stacks at once — um, I think that's something that you shouldn't underestimate.

So I think there are a lot of benefits to just like, you know, you do some core research in — in your like in your fundamental stack, and that just carries over to whatever modality or whatever thing you want.

So um, I — I think there's a strong bias for us to keep it in as as few different ar— uh architectures as possible.

Vibe Researcher 与研究的未来

主持人： Gotcha. Yeah. Great. You know that that makes a lot of sense. I think like the architecture as well is something that isn't often considered is very important.

But one also term that I've been seeing a lot that you've also kind of mentioned is a "vibe researcher." You know, we have vibe coders obviously. Yeah. But I guess on vibe researching, what do you think is like the end state? Do you think the main value out of a vibe researcher is just the research taste of coming up with the right idea, or do you think it's more so the execution of going through and following through on the actual research?

Mark： Um yeah, so I — I — I think we're actually moving towards this world very quickly, right? Um, I think both at OpenAI and at other labs, um you're starting to see a lot of the work become mostly orchestration focused, right?

Like um, the the research is coming up with the ideas. Um, and the model's great enough to do the implementation execution by itself. Um, so I think — you know when you when it comes down to like, uh, you know, is is the value of coming up with ideas versus execution. Um, yeah, both are still important, but it does feel like there's there's this market shift um towards just kind of being able to come up with a lot of ideas, and then um the model can actually do the the execution and orchestration for you.

So um, I think it's very much going to be the future of doing research.

主持人： Yeah.

Mark： Um, we also said earlier, you know, like the models don't quite have the taste yet, and um — that's why you still need the researchers coming up with the ideas. Yeah, it's going to be hard to teach the models good taste. We noticed that. But in terms of actually accelerating the research, there's clear tangible benefits already.

主持人： Yeah. Do you think there'll ever be parity in terms of research taste with models?

Mark： I think so. I mean, when we look at our kind of three-year roadmap, right? Um the end goal that we want to reach is one where you know the the models are just doing end-to-end research. And um, I think a part of that problem is just being able to have the model come up with good taste. You point at some, you know, just generic benchmark or something, and it finds the right solutions.

失败的研究赌注与后验复盘

主持人： Yeah. Yeah. No, that's helpful. And in terms of research done by humans at OpenAI, how do you guys go about, I guess, the postmortem process of, let's say, a research bet that didn't turn out well? I assume that a lot of it is taking these, you know, best bets and some don't turn out well.

Mark： Well, I would say that is a big part of OpenAI's alpha. Because I think one thing that differentiates us from other labs is we take a lot of high-risk bets. I think it's what's allowed us to stay at the frontier so consistently over time.

Um but it also means that some of the bets are not going to pan out. And um, a hard corollary of that is when when a bet doesn't pan out — um, you have to, you know, not delude yourself into thinking that, you know, this is something that will work and and kind of uh disconnect from it.

So I think there are certain calls you have — you have to make, right? Like uh, kind of look back and be like, "Well, um, this was a promising idea at the time, but actually it's less important than we thought." You know, it's uh — there's some other approach that works better or — you know, um — there's some other kind of uh — some something that we discovered.

But I — I think many of that — much of that work is also very fruitful. So what we realized is like, uh, even sometimes when people fail at um proving out a technique, uh, their write-ups are very important. Because um they'll kind of — a lot — it will often be a natural idea, and you can kind of save a lot of people from going through the same thing.

主持人： Yeah. No, that's helpful. Yeah. So I guess when it comes to this positive view on failure, how do you balance that with, you know, a researcher, let's say, who takes a lot of bets, consecutive bets, and none of them pan out? Because I assume at a certain point you'd want a researcher to eventually have contributions that are actually beneficial, compared to only taking bets that maybe pan out to being not a good space.

Mark： Just through experience, I — I've definitely seen some people fall into this. Um, but I've also had several cases where, you know, they — it's just like bet after bet, it doesn't pan out, and just when you're like at the brink of frustration, you have something that's like a mega hit.

And um, this happened enough. So, it really just depends on kind of — are are the ideas themselves sound? Um, they can be ambitious, but they still have to be sound. And um, there's a certain kind of person who will will just take a lot of those ideas, and it's okay because they're somewhat on the riskier frontier, but they only have to justify it once in a while for it to make sense.

Maybe like a very trading-like kind of lens on the world, but yeah, it's it's just on on expectation, right? Like they — they need to add value.

收尾：调味与盲品

主持人： Yeah. No, that's that's great. Okay, so we're basically assembled. Now it's the finishing touches. So you can taste your soup and then we just add soy sauce if it's not salty enough. And if it's too salty, we can add some water. We can lower this down. But let's see our final creation. How is it?

Mark： It's pretty good.

主持人： Good.

Mark： Yeah. Healthy. Okay. Mine's good.

主持人： Yeah. Mine needs a little bit of water. Could you pass water?

Mark： Absolutely.

主持人： Look great. So how was that? How did that feel?

Mark： Um, this is a student distillation. Um, you're — you're clearly better than I am at this point.

主持人： No, no, no. I feel like you did a very great job, especially with even with the shrimp and flaming. Yeah. Smells great. Wow.

Okay, that sounds good. I guess just more generally, I'm kind of curious. Are there areas in research or topics that you think are right now overrated and underrated? Like what would you categorize under?

Mark： Um, well, I think if you still have a "pre-training is dead" view of the world, um, I think — I think, uh, pre-training is definitely — yeah. Yeah. Yeah. Not — not dead. It's it's, um, it's underrated.

Um, yeah. And honestly, um, I think products and kind of thinking about end uses and, you know, how you tie all the primitives you build in research to, you know, real agentic use cases in the world — that's also underrated. I think um, you really can't just kind of build everything in a vacuum and not connect things to utility.

终极环节：盲测 (External Eval)

主持人： Yeah. No, that's great. Great. Awesome. I think we are ready to taste. So, do we want to give it a go?

Mark： Let's do it.

主持人： We can move this. And I think there should be some plating. Yeah, I do want to take those plates over here. Okay, shrimp looks great. But everything else here. Great. And then we could just use these pots. So, we have our shrimp.

Mark： Yep.

主持人： You want to try it? Cheers. Cheers.

Mark： Cheers. Let's see. It may be a little sweet.

主持人： That's a little too sweet cuz we found way too much.

Mark： It's good for me, I would say. Cheers.

主持人： That's good. Okay, great. Um, Sean, do you want to come and taste our tofu soup? Smells so good. By the way, you guys can't smell it, but um — we'll pretend you're a researcher that's trying to approach, and I'm Zuck and he's uh, you know, trying to get you. So, do you want to grab the spoon over here? Soup.

Sean： Soup is really going to sway a decision here. Quality of soup. Yeah. All right. Try both. All right. What was the artistic uh direction here?

主持人： Um, artistic direction? Well, it was mimicry, I think. This great art in mimicry.

Mark： Yeah. Just letting them cook.

主持人： Good.

Sean： Wow. Yeah. Strong. I mean, I think um, one thing I definitely is like savory and spice that go together, but then also like the sort of sea— seafood-ness kind of really goes into it.

主持人： Great.

Sean： Okay. It's fine. Mine is mine.

主持人： Am I supposed to pick a winner or what?

Sean： No, no, no. You're supposed to try it and —

主持人： No, you do pick a winner. Yes.

Sean： Okay. This is a eval.

主持人： Yes — SweetBench.

Sean： External evals.

Okay. I got to say — I feel like there's too much water in this. I think like the the —

主持人： I think it's also a pot, cuz this is a big pot.

Sean： Okay. Um, I I would say I have to go for this. Uh, just like — you're our respected guest, but I want to be objective.

Mark： Of course. Of course. Yes. Yes.

Sean： Um, and like — yeah, the density I think really — a flavor really. Um —

主持人： I I'll do h— half the water. Half the water probably.

Sean： Okay. Makes solid sense.

Mark： I mean, I think it's very personal, right? Like —

主持人： Yeah. I think it's also very personal taste. You know, even when you do a lot of cooking, taste.

Mark： No, no, no, no. Okay. I know a couple recipes. Um, I follow them to the tee. I can't like — if you tell me, "Oh, cook something slightly different," I — I'm completely lost.

主持人： Right. Right. Yeah. Mhm. Yeah. Yeah. Oh, I I'm not going to lie. I kind of looked up in chat a couple things beforehand, like just as prep. But — no worries. But yeah, it was — it was great having you. I feel like you're always leading the field with a lot of research tastes as well, and it's great seeing — yeah — the work. So hopefully it was fun.

Mark： A lot of fun. Yeah.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一锅汤里的研究哲学
最让我意外的观察是：**Mark 把"做菜"和"做研究"的同构性暴露得淋漓尽致，却没人点破**。整段对话里他在多任务处理——锅里煎虾、嘴上谈 RL——而他自己半开玩笑地说"这正是模型还做不好的事"。这不是一句俏皮话，而是一个**隐藏的研究宣言**：OpenAI 内部对"智能"的定义正在悄悄从"解决难题"转向"边煎虾边聊天"——也就是**带状态的、低优先级的、并行漂浮的认知**。IMO 金牌只是 spike，真正的瓶颈在那口慢炖的锅。
## 交易员与研究员的隐性同构：一种"不可作弊性"的迁移
他对"为什么交易员适合做研究"的回答看似谦虚（"没那么特别"），但藏了一个跨领域的硬核洞察——**unhackable**。交易和前沿研究的共同点不是数学好，而是**它们都是少数无法用叙事自欺的领域**：市场会把你的错误结算成亏损，scaling law 会把你的错误结算成 loss curve。这其实和厨师的世界一样——汤咸了就是咸了，没有 PR 能洗白。所以你会发现 OpenAI 的"评估危机"本质上是同一个问题的另一面：**当 benchmark 可以被叙事化（

item.title: [YC Root Access] Rebuilding LatAm Banking
item.dc:creator: YC Root Access
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：ARC 的真正生意不是"美元"，而是"出身税"的对冲

表面上 ARC 在卖跨境美元账户，但深入看，它在做一件更隐秘的事——为新兴市场的"全球化中产"对冲他们的出生地折价（birthplace discount）。Fernando 自己说漏了嘴："这些人不想被 financial inclusion（金融包容）"。这句话的颠覆性在于：过去三十年拉美金融科技的整个叙事（Nubank、MercadoLibre）都建立在"向下普惠"的道德高地上，而 ARC 是第一家敢公开说"我们服务的是金字塔顶端 30%、他们要的是逃离本国系统而不是被纳入本国系统"的公司。这是一次品牌伦理上的"反向革命"——从 inclusion（包容）到 exit（出走）。Dollar App → Arc 的改名，本质上是把产品从"补偿者"重新定位成"建筑师"（arc 在西语里听感像 arquitectura）。

二、跨领域连接：他在用 Revolut 的"crypto 后台"思维，跑一个 Bridgewater 式的"主权风险套利"模型

Fernando 反复强调他在 Revolut 做 crypto GM 时领悟到的不是"加密资产"，而是"全球优先 vs 本地优先"的基础设施分野。把这个洞见放到对冲基金视角看会很有意思——Ray Dalio 用宏观 ETF 让机构投资者做主权分散，ARC 实际是用 stablecoin rails 让"个人"做同样的分散，只是把它包装成了"虚拟卡 + 美股投资 + 信用卡"。最惊人的一处是他们新推的 Prestige Card：以美股资产做抵押、8% APR 取代当地 50% APR——这其实是把高盛私人银行的"证券抵押贷款（SBL）"产品下沉给中产。一张信用卡背后，藏着私人银行的资产负债表结构。这种"上层产品下沉"在拉美几乎没人做，因为大家都盯着"下层产品规模化"。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：他们的组织设计在对抗一个生物学规律

Fernando 说"公司最终能走多远，取决于创始人有多想走"——听起来鸡汤，但他给出的运营机制非常硬核：每周重置标尺、每人一个"号码"（end-to-end 数字），没有人允许停留在功能 silo 里。这里有一个反常的洞察：他在用"创业公司的细胞分裂模型"对抗"大公司的器官分化模型"。传统 MBA 教科书告诉你"规模化要靠分工"，但他的设计是反着来的——让每个小团队都拥有完整 P&L 和 north star 的一个分量，像生物里的"全能干细胞"，而不是分化后的肝细胞、神经元。

更出人意料的一点：他坦白说自己"和痛苦共存得很舒服 (comfortable with enduring pain)"，并把这归因于"caveman + lion"的生存焦虑。这其实在神经科学上是有解释的——长期处于轻度威胁状态的人，前额叶皮层与杏仁核的耦合更强，决策时的"短期甜区"会被压制。换句话说，他不是在用意志力拒绝庆祝里程碑，他是用神经回路把"庆祝"这个奖励信号关掉了。这或许解释了一个让大多数 VC 困惑的悖论：最持久的复利型创始人，往往是最不会享受成功的人——这不是性格，是被生存反复打磨出来的认知结构。

四、一句话留给读者的"啊哈"

他最值钱的一句话不是任何融资数字，而是："理解问题的人不需要懂解决方案——'a monkey can build a solution'"。在 AI 时代这句话有更深的含义：当 90% 的代码可以由 AI 生成，所有公司的护城河都会从"会做"坍缩到"懂问"——也就是说，未来五年组织设计的真正区别，不在于谁有更多工程师，而在于谁的招聘流程能筛出"递归地问 why 直到见底"的人。ARC 已经把这件事写进了招聘 SOP，而绝大多数公司还在筛"能不能写出 LeetCode Medium"。

📝 转录文本

开场与公司介绍

主持人： I am excited to welcome Fernando from ARC, fresh off of $70 million series B, co-led by Sequoia and Founders Fund. So, Fernando, thank you so much for joining.

Fernando： Thank you for having me.

主持人： Yeah, it's awesome to have you here. We worked together 5 years ago in summer 21 in one of the COVID batches. The app was, or your product was called Dollar App at the time. Now, you have rebranded to Arc. Why don't you tell us a little bit about what ARC does and the current state of the business?

Fernando： Of course. So ARC provides global accounts for people in Latin America where there's a massive demand for dollar denominated financial services. So you can imagine people who want to spend internationally, people who want to work for a company in the states while they live from a country in Latin America and get paid in dollars. People who just want to have access to a global capital market system. So we use stablecoins to build that infrastructure and provide that service to the customers.

拉美跨境金融的真实痛点

主持人： Yeah, I feel like here in the US we're so insulated from a lot of the crossborder currency issues, and maybe for people that are not as familiar with the problem, why is that such a big problem in Latin America and other places in the world?

Fernando： So perhaps I can give you some examples of customer personas and how they interact with us compared to how they did it before.

Imagine you are someone from let's say Brazil and you're used to traveling, and when you travel you want to shop and buy things. Just because you are not having proper access to the US banking system, you may have to go through a horrible spread on every single FX transaction. So what should cost you 100 will actually end up costing you 102, 104, 105. So that would be one example.

Another one is, if you think about it, there's many people who work for US companies remotely. And they need to get paid through direct deposit. And turns out that if you look at the remittance system, it's built for a percentage fee system, which is fine if you're sending a small transaction, but if you are receiving like $5,000 per month in salary, 1% of that is real money.

Another good example would be people who want to be able to invest in stocks, ETFs in the States—that is not to be taken for granted. So imagine if every time you need to invest in a stock in the states, you need to send a remittance. You need to wait for whichever 3 to 5 days for it to settle, and you're late to buying deep, right?

So all of these inefficiencies are kind of the problem that we're trying to solve when we started the company. In time, what we ended up realizing is that the opportunity was bigger. It was not so much only on the global financial side of the equation, but rather building a product for the top let's say 20–30% of the pyramid.

It's kind of funny because if you look at fintech across the last few decades, most of the opportunity has been focused around democratizing access to FX, access to investments, access to credit. But no one has done "hey, if we were to build a product for the mass affluent demographic"—not necessarily the top 0.1% of the pyramid, but rather top 30 in terms of wealth, how would it look like? So it turns out that the global class is having needs on crossborder banking, but there's so much more beyond that that we need to tackle.

创业起点：从 YC 视频到离开 Revolut

主持人： So obviously a huge problem, and you guys are taking a unique approach. Go back to the early days and tell us more about your journey to actually starting ARC.

Fernando： When I was in uni, at some point I fell in the rabbit hole of YouTube and found these videos of YC startup school. Very old school videos of YC, which makes it kind of funny to be here today. I remember being like, "Man, these people they're explaining in plain English in very simple terms how to build a company that transcends, that impacts humanity." And I was like, "It just cannot be that simple. There's no universe—they must be full of [ __ ]. It cannot be true. I should go to consulting, where people actually know"—which is kind of funny to be here today, right?

Anyway, I spent a couple of years in consulting, and then eventually I was like, "Look, I want to see how are these people who are actually changing the world." I got the courage to quit and join what back then was a very promising startup called Revolut—a London-based fintech. I think it was series D, it was 2019. It's kind of funny because in reality it was pretty early stage in many things when it comes to operating.

I spent some time in the growth team, then moved over to what we call the special projects focused on making the company profitable, and eventually became the general manager of crypto. And it's kind of funny because I wasn't this sort of expert in crypto. So my job was, on the one hand, operating the business, but also figuring out what was crypto and building my sort of mental model of the industry. Which eventually became something like sort of two orthogonal axes: one being the asset class—Bitcoin, ETH, and then also the casino part of the equation. But sort of orthogonal to it was the infrastructure.

And for someone who has spent some time on payments, I was like, "Whoa, we're comparing a local-first system with a global-first system. We're comparing 'trust the counterparty and T+2 reconciliations' with an immutable ledger; 'bank holidays and manual processes' with a 24/7 fully automated system." So I was with the conviction it was going to be a platform shift.

与 Co-founder 的渊源 & 把握"切入点"

主持人： So you met before Revolut, then?

Fernando： With Álvaro—we had been working together since consulting at BCG. We've been working together for our life, like we've been suffering each other for long enough already. And also with Zach, my co-founder and CPO, who I met in Revolut—we had been in parallel brainstorming ideas on starting something. We had the idea of becoming entrepreneurs for quite some time.

So when there was this conviction around the platform shift on blockchain—again, not so much as an asset class but rather as an infrastructure for banks to be operating on at scale—my brain snapped back to when I was in consulting in Latin America. I knew that all my colleagues were traveling with plenty of cash in the suitcase when they were going to the States. My first reaction was, "What the hell, who would be traveling with 10 grand of cash in the suitcase?" And turns out that access to dollars is something that you may take for granted in the States, but it's something that in many parts of the world is a luxury. And that's a perfect insertion point to build on a platform shift, by starting with that need.

墨西哥城商场街访：第一手用户洞察

It's kind of funny because we were not really that aware of the whole situation. With the first investment from YC we grabbed a flight to Mexico City. I'm going to sound like not the smartest person here, but we went to a shopping mall and started to stop people asking, "Do you want to buy digital dollars?" People were like a bit scared of us, "What are these two up to?" Anyway, funny story.

主持人： Did you sell any?

Fernando： Well, we didn't have anything to sell. We were trying to. We didn't have the product yet.

But what we did learn there—and it's kind of funny because you only learn these things the tough way—is that it was not only about access to the dollar, but the utility of it. Why did people want it? What's the job to be done of dollar? People wanted to travel and spend internationally. People wanted to receive transfers. So it was more of: what is the actual use case, what is the actual need?

YC 批次中的早期探索

主持人： So you got this idea from seeing all the infrastructure that you were building as part of Revolut, and then tying that back to your own personal experience. What convinced you that this was actually going to stick and actually going to work? Did you know this during the batch when you were so early and you were interviewing these people and trying to figure it out, or did that come much later?

Fernando： I just knew it was a massive problem. I knew that it was worth solving because I had seen what people were doing. We see people doing weird stuff around a big problem—there's always something there. But we're completely clueless.

I think every founder has their own story, but we were not so hyper-rational, "Oh yeah, this is what we'll do." It was more like the FAFO sort of framework. We knew that there was something, and that if we spent enough time understanding it and going to the root cause of what customers need, we'll be able to solve a massive problem.

主持人： Do you remember what was your focus during the batch? You were obviously very early. I remember you were trying to build the first version of the product, and even going live is a big challenge for a product like yours.

Fernando： Initially we were thinking that it would be like more "store of value" sort of the equation. And eventually what we realized is it's more the utility component. That was a game changer because once we realized that, and we got the courage to say, "Hey, this is what we need to build, this is where we're headed," and then reverse engineer all the steps to get there, it made our life way easier.

But if you think about it, building a fintech product is not something that straightforward, which is very painful to be in the cave building that for a few months, because you cannot get so much customer feedback. And if you ship an MVP that is half-baked, you're going to get a signal that may not be the best representation of where the product should be going.

主持人： Do you remember how long it took you to actually get live with your first users?

Fernando： The first product was only access to store of value—pretty straightforward. But again, the feedback was, "Look, we need to do things with these digital dollars." Which, again, it sucks—you go through all these ups and downs. But if you're doing a proper truth-seeking exercise, you get that, and you ultimately learn what you're going to do.

It was a bit less than a year to have the first version of the product, which included virtual account details in the states, an international card, and then the store-of-value component. That was when we said, "Okay, this is working."

"这就是 PMF" 的那通电话

I remember a call we had—maybe you recall—I think I was with my co-founder Álvaro in Buenos Aires. We were very very worried because we saw that everything was compounding month after month, but it's not hockey stick. I said, "How does product market fit feel like?" And you were like, "No, guys, this is product market fit." And we're still with this imposter syndrome of "what the hell is going on?"

主持人： Right. And now it has continued.

Fernando： It has continued. And again, that's how it works. You keep doing all these tough things month after month, pushing yourself to be better. And eventually you get there.

谈"冒名顶替综合征"与永远的生存感

主持人： I'm sure there's a lot of people that are watching this that feel that imposter syndrome themselves. They see a video like this of a successful founder building a successful company. And maybe what's special about you is you would probably respond to that and say, "We're not successful yet. We have not done anything yet." But to a lot of the people watching, they probably aspire to be in the position that you're in. How do you overcome that imposter syndrome and find the confidence in yourself that actually you can do this just like anybody else has?

Fernando： Yeah, it's kind of interesting. If you reflect on it, maybe here in Bay Area it's normalized because everyone knows many people who have gone through a startup, have started their own startup, and been successful. But that's not something that you can take for granted in many parts of the world where we don't have so many cases of successful entrepreneurship in technology that are close to us.

There's sort of two answers I could give. One: there's something a bit positive in always feeling that you're under threat and you're fighting for survival. At the end of the day, feeling always like you're the caveman and there's a lion waiting for you outside—that helps you to reset expectations all the time and have this survival experience. I'm not sure if it's intentional or rather the result of years or decades, since I was a kid.

主持人： Where does that come from for you? I think that is actually a quality of some of the best founders—they're just perpetually never satisfied. Because human nature is, I don't know, you guys have grown to more than 10 billion annualized volume, and I think a lot of people would be like, "Wow, we're doing really good, maybe we can chill out." That's the natural human instinct. But the people that get to that level don't operate that way. I see that in you. How do you find that in yourself?

Fernando： It's an interesting reflection. We've gone through all these stages and we've never really celebrated milestones. We're happy about it, but we've never thrown a party. If you think about it, the reason why we're happy when these things happen, when these things come to be true, is not so much that we got there—but "hey, if we got here, where are we going to be in 12, 24, 36 months?"

It's hard to tell where it comes from. My intuition will be: being comfortable with enduring pain. At the end of the day, every week as a founder, you need to reset the bar and say, "Look, all of these things that we did great last week—they no longer matter. We're not going to discuss them. We're going to discuss the three things where we need to do better." You really need to do that every week. You need to build a culture that enforces that everyone is doing it this way.

And the moment that you get satisfied, you no longer think in this way and you're no longer going to keep compounding. I have this sort of theory that companies get as far as they want eventually, and everyone who stops growing at this exponential pace at some point is because they decided not to.

主持人： Which might be an oversimplified narrative, but I think it's true. Companies are only as ambitious as the founders. So you have that sense of urgency and you're perpetually never satisfied. How do you drive that amongst the rest of your team to make sure that everybody else operates that way?

文化练习与四条"不可妥协"

Fernando： Not so long ago we did the first culture exercise. When we were doing it, the temptation is to think about culture as "who you would want to be." But then at the end of the day, people converge with who they are. So if you do that, you're going to get to something that is not really aligned with how people behave.

So instead of that, with two of the most senior people in the organization who've been already for some time and they know how we operate, we asked them, "Hey, what are the non-negotiables?" It's kind of funny because what we did is—the three of us founders, for the first part of the session, we couldn't really speak. We had to sit there and listen to everyone saying which are the non-negotiables.

Eventually we landed with these four traits that, if someone doesn't really live by those four traits, it's like thrown out of the window. I think it's one of the most beautiful things that has happened to me in the company because I was like, "Whoa, without me speaking, these people are saying exactly the right things."

"Choosing unreasonable ambition"

Choosing unreasonable ambition—which has two interesting components. The first one is the idea of unreasonable ambition, and the second one is choosing.

Let me start with the first one. Every company, eventually when you scale—maybe in the early days not so much—it's sort of a model, like an FP&A model where you input resources you output more resources. But if you operate in that fashion, you're never going to compound exponentially. So part of the job of the founder is to think on how do you add some entropy to that model, to go to a direction where the only way for you to achieve it is to transform who you are. If you set a very rational objective, you're never forcing yourself to change who you are.

主持人： We see this a lot with the goal setting during the YC batch. A lot of times the instinct is, "Well, I'm talking to this many customers and if I close this many," they kind of project forward on the path they're already on, rather than picking something that is extremely ambitious and they're unlikely to hit, and then it forces a flip in their brain that goes, "Oh god, I just committed to that thing. How am I possibly going to do that?" And they have to start thinking outside of the current box to figure out how to get there.

Fernando： But here I go to the second part, which is the most beautiful, which is choosing. This is not something that comes natural to people. It's a choice. And this vow is about that need to renew every week. If at every point in time you stop choosing unreasonable ambition, you're cooked. You become a sort of FP&A company that sets a target that is reasonable, to grow at whichever 10% year-over-year, and you're no longer going to be a company that is transforming who you are.

如何让文化"渗透"组织：招聘 + 输出导向

主持人： What are some tactical ways that you kind of drive that and permeate that throughout the company? Like how do you manage the goals of the company or different teams within the company?

Fernando： There's two components. The first one, which is maybe a bit of a cliché, but it's hiring for culture. We're still relatively small in the grand scheme of things, but it's a humbling experience when you hire people that are way better than you at specific things, and you need to absorb this knowledge, you need to absorb these things that improve the way you think about operating and cascading across the whole organization.

But then again, we try to be extremely output-oriented. So basically—maybe there's many companies that have been successful being input-oriented companies—but what we try to do is we set a north star for the company, one metric that we define as success criteria, and then we try to cascade it down into different things that, if they are all true, ties up to the main north star of the company.

主持人： What's your north star metric for the company?

Fernando： So we're looking at core MAU, which is people who are engaged with us to a level that is representative of a deep relationship. So not just signed up one time, but actively using the product in meaningful ways. And then we can scale down into all the components that need to be true for that metric to go up—the growth component, the breakdown into every single one of the products.

And then we try to make sure we have this hardcore orientation around your number. So actually one of our values is also "on your number." I think we have observed that accountability very often matters more than specific knowledge. In the conventional way of structuring an organization, it's more like functions—you have all this weird situation in which the end-to-end process is not owned by anyone. Whereas if what you do is, "hey, you own the end-to-end number," people actually figure it out, especially great people.

So we try to make sure everyone has their own number, and they have to figure out. Every single person in the company has a number. Again, we're getting there. When you're getting started, it doesn't really make sense. But slowly we're moving in that direction. Sometimes "hey, this team has this number," and then eventually you go cascading it down. Sometimes it's not a number, it's like "hey, you need to deliver this feature by this date"—more like a binary type of outcome.

But we try to make sure that everyone has accountability. Which is the beautiful part, because then eventually every small part of the company is per se a startup. You create this compounding element where you have all these small groups of people that are working on multiple product lines, multiple businesses, and each of them treated as their own child to an extent.

从 Dollar App 到 ARC：品牌的重新校准

主持人： Maybe that kind of leads me into—you started as Dollar App and you rebranded recently as Arc. Talk about what went into that and maybe how the company has evolved to make that necessary to happen now.

Fernando： I was thinking before the story of when we went to Mexico with the $125K from YC—people wanted dollars and we didn't really understand fully all the nuances around it. But that had two problems.

The first one is that it became pretty self-limiting. How do you tell your friend that the best way for you to invest in US stocks is Dollar App, or the best way for you to save in Brazilian reais is Dollar App? Like, shouldn't be app, right? So it became pretty self-limiting.

Then the other component, which I think in hindsight might be the most interesting—if you think about our old brand, it was in a big chunk articulated around financial inclusion. Like, you cannot have dollars; now you can have a dollar—so the name is "dollar." If you go to our old website, the tagline used to be something like, "The world has borders, your finances don't have to." So we are a Latin American company that includes you in the US.

But what is kind of funny is that if you look at our customers, it's mostly top 30% of the pyramid in terms of wealth. It's people who travel, who have a global lifestyle, who work for international companies, who like to invest. These people, they don't want to be included. So it was a brand that was completely misaligned with who our customers were.

At some point you need to start becoming really good at marketing and at distribution at scale. And if you don't have a good brand for that, you're going to be in a really poor position at scale.

那些没有 party 的"里程碑"

主持人： That was obviously a big milestone with the branding change, but what are some of the other milestones along the way that you've hit, or what have been some of the inflection points that have gotten you to this point? You mentioned earlier you're at over 10 billion in annualized volume, which is a crazy number to think about.

Fernando： Back to my point from before, we don't really… nothing stands out. But actually I'm thinking more of the emotional ones.

I remember one in the early days—maybe people who are right on the batch can probably reflect on this. I was sending manually an email campaign. We've all done that—downloaded a CSV. And I remember I saw an email and I was like, "Wow, this rings a bell." It was the first name and last name of a friend of mine from uni that I hadn't spoken to in years. And I went to his LinkedIn and I saw that he's living in Mexico City. I was like, "What the hell?"

So I DMed him on LinkedIn: "Hey man, are you living in Mexico City?" And he's like, "Oh yeah, I moved here from Spain, blah blah." I was like, "Well, and you're using Dollar App." And he's like, "What the hell?" I didn't even have my LinkedIn back then. He's like, "What the hell? How do you know that?" "Okay, let's go for a beer tomorrow and I can tell you more."

I think it's all these inflection points that make you realize, well, there's real value. Give you the confidence. But not so much, I would argue, on the numbers component—where we throw a party because we got here—but rather this situation in which everyone all the time is thinking about the future. In the company, no one is using the real production app—we're all on TestFlight with the version of the app that has not yet been released. You're always living in the future.

招聘哲学：测试"问题求解者"

主持人： Well, intentionally, right? How do you keep that bar high? Do you vet people throughout the hiring process for those core values that are so important to you? Because if you let the wrong people in, then it's never going to work out.

Fernando： There's two types of companies: companies that try to maintain the talent bar, and then companies that say, "Hey, this is the worst the talent bar is ever going to be," and they try to just improve it. And this sort of question they always ask: "Hey, is this person better than 50% of the organization?" when you're hiring.

The way we do it is we define the things that correlate with people being successful for every single role in the organization, and we make sure we test them.

主持人： What are those things for you guys?

Fernando： Depends a bit on the role. In the case of engineering, for example, you need to do a live coding session. In the case of generalists—people who work in strategy operations, in product—we focus a lot on problem solving. And one thing that is kind of interesting is that if you look at the world of corporate or even big tech, very often problem solving is focused more on the solution part. I think that in a startup or in a fast-changing environment, it's much more the problem statement.

The truth is that if you really understand a problem well and you're able to go deep into the root cause, like a monkey can build a solution. But what is very complex is understanding deeply a problem statement. So we often look for people who are exceptional at understanding what is exactly the root cause of the problem statement, who are very analytical, and are willing to go dirty and keep asking "why" iteratively until they really understand the problem statement. Which is very rare, because people are not trained to operate in this fashion. That's a pretty good predictor of success in the company.

And of course the cultural component. Something funny you can do is just ask people to tell you their story. You can see if those people were choosing unreasonable ambition, if those people are solving the real problem, if those people are owning their number, if those people are raising the bar to themselves and to others constantly. You can get those things if you ask someone to tell you their story.

多国扩张：自下而上 + 自上而下

主持人： You're live in a number of countries now, right? Is it four? Four countries. I know that's always something that is a challenge for founders—figuring out, especially if you're location-based or you have multiple different types of customers, how do you expand to each of those? Like go into a new market, figure out how to roll out—what's the playbook for all of that? What have you found in expanding to all these different countries and actually making it work in each one?

Fernando： It's a discussion I've been having since the very very early days with one of our first investors, Hernán from Kaszek, who invested right at the end of the batch. He's been sitting on the board since then in the company.

He has these two success stories that he's seen. One is Mercado Libre, which he was a co-founder of, and then Nubank, where they were one of the first investors, if not the first investor actually. And two very different stories—one that was doing many many things in parallel, one that was very focused on the key bets. And both were wildly successful. So it's hard to say "this is the right model, this is the wrong model" or vice versa. People tend to behave in a way that ties up to who they are.

So in our case, the way we try to approach it is: we try to do more things than, in this case, Nubank, that was super focused surgically on credit in Brazil for a very long time. But we try to have the market pull from us. We try to do, "Hey, what is the minimum we need to do to get something out of the door and test that the market wants it and how customers behave with it, and then double down."

Sometimes you know there's something there and you need to be way more top-down than bottom-up when it comes to it. Going back to the early days, we knew that people wanted access to a dollar-denominated financial system. If we had gone, "Oh, people don't want store of value, scrap that," you can give up. But you know that there's something there, so you need to keep going. You need to apply those two mindsets.

进入巴西：infra + GM 模式

主持人： Like you're expanding to Brazil, right? Which you just launched. Do you send a small team in there to kind of be the team that's going to go in and launch this? Do you take your current larger organization and have everybody focus on that? What's the use of resources to be able to make sure that it's successful out of the gate?

Fernando： If you look at what we do, it's kind of interesting because you have the demand side and then the infrastructure. If you look at the demand side, our target customer is very similar in all the markets. There's always people who want to travel, who have a global lifestyle, who want to invest. So it's not that different when you compare our customers in Mexico to our customers in Brazil, Argentina. You have some local flavors, but roughly speaking it's very similar in terms of the needs that we solve for them.

But if you look at the infrastructure from a regulatory side, from a payment side, it does change a lot. So a big part of the work is putting in place that infrastructure and then eventually we have the component of cracking the go-to-market, which can be a bit different in every market. But the good thing is that our customer type is more or less the same.

I go up to this point that accountability is what matters most. So we always try to have one person that is accountable for that specific one. In case of Brazil, we hired Leo, who's our general manager leading the country, and he had to figure out the whole thing.

主持人： So you bring in the general manager, they're the responsible person.

Fernando： Nowadays, yes. Back in the day for Argentina, I went there. I figured out how to buy an AML license—we found a good deal—and then built integration with the engineering team and launched. But effectively, one person needs to have that as a success metric and live and die for it.

低谷与"几十年视角"

主持人： You don't like to celebrate the highs, but I'm sure the lows stick with you. What's something like any kind of tough moments? I think from the outside it looks like you've just been on this amazing rocket ship. What have been some of the challenging moments that stand out to you over the years?

Fernando： It's interesting. I'm not sure if we really dwell so much on the bad moments, to be fair, because if you do that, building a startup is going to be a nightmare. You need to have thick skin to an extent.

But there have been so many moments I would call very tough. I remember, again, we were in the batch in 21—pretty good momentum in the market, right? It was good for crypto, it was good for Latin America, it was good for fintech. So we were like a cool kid in the playground.

And man, you remember what happened, right? Crypto was suddenly a red flag. Latin America was suddenly a red flag. Fintech was suddenly a red flag. And it's a non-obvious moment.

But the interesting take is that all these things are not so much a point of what is the current reality, but rather what is your time horizon. And if you're very long-term minded, the fundamentals of the opportunity, the quality of the team didn't really change. But it's always hard in a world in which you're fighting for survival to remember that you're playing a multi-decade game, and where you are today is actually almost meaningless compared with where you're going to be and you want to be in two, three decades from now.

So it's very hard, because you need to all the time be putting yourself into the context of, "Hey, when you're in a low, things are not that bad. In three years from now, we'll have forgotten this. In three decades from now, the only thing that matters is the fundamentals."

未来 5–10 年：Prestige Card 与"私人银行下沉"

主持人： So what is the future for Arc then, the next 5 to 10 years?

Fernando： We're just starting to scratch the surface when it comes to building a product for the mass affluent demographic.

One of our new products, for example, is pretty cool—we're starting to release it slowly—is the Prestige Card. We've rethought how credit cards work. Effectively what we realized is that in many cases, credit cards have a relatively low credit line and very high APRs.

It's kind of interesting because if you look at how people who are in a private bank relationship manage their finances, they'll never borrow at 25, let alone 54% APR. But I do take a margin loan which is the federal reserve rate plus 100 bps or whatsoever. So we've built a credit card using that same logic.

We've designed a way that you will never be liquidated. We discount assets a lot for you to have a credit line, but still, if you have whichever 50K in S&P 500, you can get a 25K credit line. The rate, if you decide to finance your purchase, is like 8%. So instead of designing a credit card for people to have a heart attack when it comes to paying—and otherwise "I'm going to get a 50% APR" which is common in many markets in which we operate—actually the smart thing is to finance your purchase and keep your money in the S&P compounding for the long run. That's just one example of the direction in which we want to go.

公司内部如何使用 AI

主持人： How are you guys using AI within the company right now?

Fernando： When it comes to the capex piece, first and foremost, it's kind of obvious that more than 90% of the code that we produce is already done with AI. Again, probably these days that's almost taken for granted.

Beyond that, one thing that is very interesting is that we have this tool called Back Office where everyone builds mini-apps. Whenever we think of building a new process, doing a new marketing campaign, everyone is expected to be able to build those tools, including non-technical people. Pairing with engineers, sometimes more autonomously. Instead of going and outsourcing things, build it in-house in a way that's tailored very specifically to how we run the company. So those are tools to invest capital in more efficient ways.

运营侧：AI 客服 + AML

On the operating side—again, kind of obvious—but there are two things that are very interesting. One is building a support agent. It can already take a very meaningful chunk of the work and allows our human agents to be focused on the cases that matter the most, that are most complex, instead of the parts of support that are less relevant.

主持人： You've got millions of users to support as well, right? So it's the thing that enables you to scale.

Fernando： Correct. And it's also very interesting when it comes to financial crime and all the AML part of the equation. Because not only is it more efficient—which is the classic train of thought—but also you can do things now that you could not do before.

If you look at how traditionally you would think of the AML component: you cannot scan a huge number of transactions in real time because it's impossible, even if you wanted to hire such a big team to do it. It's not a matter of efficiency, you just can't do it in real time. You usually would go with a solution where you apply limits on the accounts, and then when you hit that limit then you go into a review—you have a small team reviewing everything that was flagged by a machine learning model.

In this case, you can actually have the machine learning model, a big team of agents that do the first pass, and then they escalate to the humans who are actually managing the team of agents. And that's great, because then you don't need to be so restrictive on the limit side of the equation, because you do have the bandwidth to do all these checks.

外部侧：私人银行机器人

And you have the external part of the equation, that we're starting to build into. One thing that makes me very excited about building in the next few months is the idea of creating a bot, a sort of private banker assistant for you that you can forward "send this wire for me, send it to my friend," and then having an approval mechanism similar to what you have in 3DS with credit cards in the app for transactions that are below a limit. So we always try to think of these two things and encourage it across the whole organization.

为什么从 Dollar App 改名为 ARC

主持人： You recently rebranded from Dollar App to ARC. What was the motivation for doing that, and why did you pick the name ARC?

Fernando： When we started, we knew that there was a massive demand for dollar-denominated financial services in the region. So the obvious thing was to name the company after that and go with Dollar App. And the truth is that it worked in the early days, but now it has become very self-limiting.

If we launch investments in stocks in the states, how do you tell your friends that Dollar App is the right company for doing that? Not the right product. If you're going to save in Brazilian reais, how do you tell your friends that Dollar App is the right app to do it? It really didn't serve the purpose anymore.

The second one is more aligned on the brand, the ethos and what it represented for customers. If you look at our old brand, it used to be around financial inclusion. "You cannot have dollars, now you can have digital dollars, therefore Dollar App." If you go to our website, the tagline used to be something like "The world has borders, your finances don't have to." We were a Latin American company that would include you in the US.

But again, if you look at our customer profile—really to what we were speaking about before—it's people who are on the wealthier side of the equation. People who like to travel, who work for a company abroad, who work in finance and technology, who invest. That group of people, they don't want to be included. If I try to tell you, "Hey, I'm going to include you," that might not resonate with you.

So not only the name, but rather the ethos of the brand was around financial inclusion instead of about the future and the excitement of a better financial life. It's not so much running away from it, but rather where you're going.

"ARC" 这个名字怎么来的

It was a funny exercise, and of course it took us a pretty long period of naming, because we knew we could blow it up. Even coming up with a name was not that obvious.

主持人： Yeah, it's hard, right? How did you come up with the name?

Fernando： The first thing that we thought is, "Hey, we need to have something that doesn't have already a very strong meaning." Because it's very hard for you to own that word if it's already in use. A very good example would be like "water." Water already means something in your head. It's very hard for a company to say, "We're Water"—for people to think about you the first time they hear the word. So we wanted something that didn't really have a strong usage, a strong meaning, so we could become the owners of the word.

At the same time, we wanted something that could have positive connotations. And Arc is a beautiful word because, on the one hand, if you think about it in Portuguese and Spanish, it sounds like architecture—you're building your financial life. It also sounds very similar to "arc" connecting two dots, or even the ark where you store your things. So you have all these positive connotations in a lateral way. It's also short, it's punchy, you can even use it as a verb eventually. It had all these beautiful things.

But at the end of the day, our sort of conclusion was what mattered the most was not so much that it was a perfect name, but rather that it didn't have any of the problems of the old name when it comes to being self-limiting, to being hard to architect the right ethos around it. So that was kind of the process.

YC 的影响 & 给下一代创始人的话

主持人： What kind of impact did YC have on you?

Fernando： The impact was massive. We wouldn't be here if it wasn't for YC.

主持人： Looking back to the early days and thinking about yourself thinking about applying to YC and going through the batch, is there any advice that you wish somebody had given you, or that you would like the next generation of founders coming up now to be thinking about in the context of YC?

Fernando： There's two things that were super interesting.

One, the first one, is making us believe. We had a very very rough idea. We knew that the opportunity was big, and that was pretty clear. But still, very often it's so non-obvious when you're in a good position in a great company like Revolut—we were in a very privileged spot, but we still didn't have the courage to do it.

I remember getting the call from Dalton back then, "We'd love to invest." And I was like, "These guys, they're so stupid. Why would they give us money?" That was the first reaction. A few hours later I was like, "Well, these people, they know what they're doing. They've backed some of the most impressive companies in history. There is something."

And then the other thing that has been a constant—you guys have this habit of slapping entrepreneurs in the face with truth. Back to what I was saying before with the YC startup school videos. All the lectures were sort of very obvious things, and that's what makes them so amazing. Sometimes it's very hard to outline in very plain terms things that are obvious but are really really important.

I actually remember in another call we had, we were so lost back then, and you said this thing like, "Guys, what I like about the product is that it's obviously the best product out there." And again, we hadn't stopped and thought about it.

All these times when you have all this chaos around building a startup, that clarity of thought is not to be taken for granted. All those pieces of advice that I received from you guys—again, I would go not so much in the direction of the specific advice, but rather helping get the clarity of thought. And being able to find this clarity of thought is non-trivial. Eventually you manage yourself to be able to do it. But in the early days, you guys were super helpful for that—from getting the courage to do it, from "hey, where are the fundamentals," and making sure you know the time direction in which you're going.

收尾

主持人： Yep. And sounds like you've built a lot of belief in yourself over the years, too, which has been really amazing to see.

Fernando： Yeah.

主持人： Very cool. Fernando, thank you so much for joining and sharing your story, and giving us a peek behind how you've been building such an ambitious company.

Fernando： Thank you for having me, Aaron. My pleasure.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：ARC 的真正生意不是"美元"，而是"出身税"的对冲
表面上 ARC 在卖跨境美元账户，但深入看，它在做一件更隐秘的事——**为新兴市场的"全球化中产"对冲他们的出生地折价（birthplace discount）**。Fernando 自己说漏了嘴："这些人不想被 financial inclusion（金融包容）"。这句话的颠覆性在于：**过去三十年拉美金融科技的整个叙事（Nubank、MercadoLibre）都建立在"向下普惠"的道德高地上，而 ARC 是第一家敢公开说"我们服务的是金字塔顶端 30%、他们要的是逃离本国系统而不是被纳入本国系统"的公司**。这是一次品牌伦理上的"反向革命"——从 inclusion（包容）到 exit（出走）。Dollar App → Arc 的改名，本质上是把产品从"补偿者"重新定位成"建筑师"（arc 在西语里听感像 arquitectura）。
## 二、跨领域连接：他在用 Revolut 的"crypto 后台"思维，跑一个 Bridgewater 式的"主权风险套利"模型
Fernando 反复强调他在

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] The Ruthless Ruler Behind Machiavelli’s The Prince
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：马基雅维利不是"冷血现实主义者"，他是被charisma俘获的目击者

整段叙述里最被忽略的细节是那一次人称的破裂——"He told me, first person."

我们一直把《君主论》读成一本冰冷的政治算计手册，但这里揭示了一个惊人的事实：马基雅维利在为切萨雷·博尔贾辩护时，作者的面纱滑落了。他不是在做案例分析，他是在替一个让他终生着迷的人作证。

这意味着我们读到的《君主论》其实是一份伪装成政治理论的目击者陈述——一个被恐惧、魅力和近距离观察永久改变的人，把自己的创伤包装成了普世法则。这本书的"客观性"是文学性的伪装，底层是一个幸存者对那个差点吃掉他的怪物的私人迷恋。

二、跨领域连接：博尔贾的"我最后吃你"=囚徒困境里的次优均衡 + 现代创业生态的"被收购恐惧"

马基雅维利向佛罗伦萨献策"献身、出卖盟友、只求最后被吃"——这其实是博弈论中一个非常精确的结构：当你无法改变玩家集合，无法改变支付矩阵，唯一可优化的就是"被消灭的顺序"。

这个结构在今天惊人地重现：

  • 硅谷小公司面对FAANG：知道自己迟早被收购或绞杀，最优策略是成为对方生态的"忠诚供应商"，用"我最后吃"的策略换取时间窗口。
  • 学术界面对AI实验室：传统NLP实验室对OpenAI/Anthropic的态度，几乎是Polyphemus式的——合作、献数据、当评测基准，只求不被第一波清洗。
  • 小国对大国：芬兰冷战时期的"芬兰化"政策，本质上是马基雅维利策略的国家级实现。

真正出人意料的洞见：佛罗伦萨的策略不是"求生"，而是赌教皇会死。这是一个押注在生物学波动性上的金融期权——他们购买的不是时间，而是Alexander VI身体的看跌期权。马基雅维利发明了一种政治衍生品。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：《君主论》是历史上第一本"反结果导向"的成功学

通常的解读说马基雅维利开创了功利主义、结果至上。但讲述者指出了一个被忽略的精妙之处：

"He says we need to evaluate their deeds based on what the most probable outcome was before fortune intervened."

这是反事实推理（counterfactual reasoning）的早期形式，比卡尼曼的"过程vs结果"区分早了五百年，比扑克玩家Annie Duke的《Thinking in Bets》早了五个世纪。

啊哈时刻：马基雅维利其实在攻击一种我们今天仍然深陷其中的认知偏误——结果偏误（outcome bias）。他在说：博尔贾家族被铲除、徽章被凿掉、披萨店墙上那道疤——这些都是幸存者偏差的反面（即"失败者偏差"）。我们以为他们的失败证明了策略错误，但实际上他们的失败只证明了概率的尾部事件偶尔会发生。

这意味着《君主论》最深的内核不是关于权力的——它是关于如何在一个一半被运气统治的世界里，仍然做出可被辩护的决策。它本质上是一本贝叶斯决策论的文艺复兴版本，而权力只是它的案例研究。

最后一个意外联系：罗马那家披萨店墙上被凿掉的博尔贾徽章——那道疤痕，就是马基雅维利整个论证的物理隐喻。历史只保留结果的疤痕，不保留概率的纹理。而《君主论》是人类第一次系统性地反抗这种遗忘。

📝 转录文本

引子：马基雅维利作为外交官的视角

Before Machiavelli writes The Prince, he's a sort of bureaucratic diplomat, and he meets through his career a lot of these famous figures. I want to know what he makes, for example, of King Louis of France, of Maximilian of Germany, the Holy Roman Empire. I want to know what he made of Cesare Borgia.

Yeah, he spends a lot of The Prince, in fact, trying to veil how much more he cares about Cesare Borgia than everyone else. It's so interesting. He tries to be balanced — he tries to talk about this example and this example, this is battle, and Valentino, and this example, and… and sometimes he just can't.

面纱滑落的瞬间：第一人称的闯入

And there's that incredible magical moment when he's discussing Valentino's fall. The moment he has amassed all this power — he successfully conquered almost everything within Italy — and then suddenly both his father the Pope and him fall ill at once.

And when Machiavelli describes this, and he's saying: everything Cesare Borgia did, he did right. He conquered this kingdom. He would have kept it. The only reason he lost it was fortune.

And what Machiavelli should say is: Valentino had planned for every contingency at his father's death except the possibility that he would also be on death's door. But that's not what Machiavelli says.

What Machiavelli says is: "He told me that he had planned…" The first person breaks in. Our historian cannot veil himself anywhere. He cares too much.

"He told me" — first person — "that he had prepared for everything at the event of his father's death, except the possibility that he himself would also be incapacitated at the moment."

And it's such a magical moment where the veil between the author and the reader breaks for just that moment. And we're like: oh, yes. All of these others he observed from a distance. But Machiavelli was in the room — next to Valentino. I had Valentino's side through this and had the most incredible, life-changing first-person view of this man.

切萨雷·博尔贾的恐怖魅力

So unique and charismatic and terrifying that when you read accounts of him, they range from "this was the most incredible, charismatic leader I've ever met" to "this man was supernaturally charismatic to the degree that he must be literally the Antichrist or an incarnation of the Angel of Death on Earth, because I have no other explanation of how he could be so persuasive and charismatic."

And Machiavelli was in the room. And every so often you just feel that he's still in the spell of this incredible figure at whose side he had the scariest job in the world.

佛罗伦萨的生存策略：献身于征服者

Because Machiavelli's job dealing with Cesare Borgia — it's very clear that the Borgia plan is to conquer the papal states. In the middle of Italy and Tuscany, Florence's dominion is this little notch like a puzzle piece out of the side of the papal states. And anybody with a map looking at it is like: you gotta conquer that. You just have to conquer this. You can't have a kingdom without it. You have to. There is no way to stop it.

So what do you do?

Machiavelli's advice to his polity is: this time we're not going to succeed in persuading this conqueror to pass us by. We can't bribe him into doing something else permanently — but we can buy time. And we can absolutely and abjectly swear to do anything he wants. So we can give him our forces, and we can give him our money, we can pay him and help him conquer the rest of it — and betray our allies. Betray Bologna, which Florence had had a 300-year alliance to defend.

He said: we have to break it. The whole world is broken right now. We have to break every promise and every hereditary alliance we had. We must be at the side of this man. And the only possible survival mechanism is to win from him through loyalty, through support, and through Machiavelli being at his ear whispering forever — Florence is loyal, Florence is loyal.

By that, we buy the boon of Polyphemus, the terrifying promise of the conqueror: "I like you, my guest. I'll eat you last." That's the Republic's only hope.

And that's Machiavelli's job: to stand next to the scariest man who has lived in Europe since Frederick Barbarossa and whisper constantly in his ear, the Florentine Republic will support you and will give your grace anything you ask. Just eat us last.

与《君主论》原则的矛盾？

Doesn't it contradict what you're saying in The Prince about never rise with the help of great powers — for even in success you have empowered somebody who is stronger than you and whose mercy you're at?

I mean, this is not Florence aiming to rise. This is not Florence expecting that it will gain anything by this. This is Florence knowing it will lose. And Machiavelli is very open about the fact: if Alexander had lived another year, Valentino would have finished his conquest and taken Florence at last, and it would have been over.

But popes are mortal. And buying time is sometimes the survival mechanism.

塞尼加利亚的屠杀

And so Machiavelli has this incredible firsthand experience of being with Valentino through all of these decisions. Being with him at the massacre at Senigallia — when the rumor had reached Valentino that some of his people were terrified of him and plotting to overthrow him, and then they were so scared of him they decided to abandon the plot.

And he heard, and he met with them, and told them: I forgive you. It's okay. You've renewed your loyalty to me. You've passed the test. I trust you all as well. And then he invites them to the banquet — and then massacres them all.

The forgiveness is false. The betrayals are punished.

马基雅维利家人的等待

There's this amazing letter that's a couple months afterward where Machiavelli's loved ones are writing from Florence because they've received a letter from him after the massacre at Senigallia. And they say: oh, thank God you're alive. We had no idea — all we heard was that he had massacred a large number of the people who were with him. We didn't know if you were alive.

Because it took months — in the chaos the postal system had completely broken down. It took months for them to get word that Machiavelli was still alive. They didn't know whether he had been caught up in the conspiracy. He easily could have been on a list of names of people the conspirators intended to recruit — and be gone.

So his wife and his loved ones back at home, his children, had to wait months to find out whether he too had been slaughtered. And it felt to them like a miracle that he hadn't.

违反待客之道：但丁式的罪

But it meant that he watched these incredible deeds of: you encounter them, you forgive them, you renew vows of amity — sacred vows taken in the cathedral — and then you slaughter them at dinner, violating the laws of hospitality.

That Dante would say: if you do that, you've committed such a grave sin that you're not just regular damned — a devil comes up out of hell and takes your soul out of your body and inhabits you, and you're actually already in hell even though your body is still alive on earth. Because that's how heinous a sin this is.

And yet, it works. And all the rest of Valentino's men are more loyal to him afterward than ever before, and won't even whisper to each other about dissatisfaction. Because even the faintest whiff of conspiracy might result in death.

为什么瓦伦蒂诺的王国仍然崩塌？

So why does Valentino's kingdom — for which he did everything right — ultimately fall apart?

Because he happens to eat the same thing that gives him food poisoning as his father, and happens to be ill at the wrong moment. And also the puppet that he manages to get in power, Pius III, dies too fast — and then he's outmaneuvered by Julius.

If all those things hadn't gone wrong in a row, the kingdom would have stood, and indeed he would have conquered Florence.

命运与德行：马基雅维利的核心论点

So Machiavelli is constantly reminding us that yes, we have all of these things we can try to do. We can remember it's better to be feared than loved. We can remember not to be hated. We have power over maximum half of what causes outcomes. The other half is always going to be fortune.

Because we look at Machiavelli, we know he's the origin of utilitarian thought, and that he says we need to evaluate people's deeds based on outcome. But he doesn't just say we need to evaluate their deeds based on outcome. He says we need to evaluate their deeds based on what the most probable outcome was before fortune intervened.

罗马墙上的疤痕

And so he says: people look at Valentino Borgia and say, but the Borgias fell. They were feared and then they were hated and then they fell and then their enemies took power — and then chiseled their coats of arms off of every surface in Rome, so that to this day you're walking through Rome and you sit down at a pizzeria and there's a weird scar on the wall — and that scar is where the Borgia bull is no more.

And people want to make the moral of that be: don't do what the Borgias did, they fell.

And Machiavelli's like: no. They did not fall because of their choices. They fell because half of what happens in the world is never in our control. You can do everything right, and it's out of your control. But we have to evaluate what would have happened — and therefore we should imitate them, because everything they did was right.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here, and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
