They thought, "Well, if you go at two-and-a-halves, and you bowl with a good stock delivery to keep them in check, then in between that you bowl a bit wide of the crease, you go and take a bit of time out, go and speak to the fielder and say, 'What we going to have for lunch? What we going to have for dinner tonight?' The batsman's probably thinking, 'What's the new game plan?' And you just change your field, bowl the same delivery."

That kind of stuff is what sometimes, as a young cricketer, we don't talk about enough. Because if you're a young cricketer, you don't see a young 15-year-old or 16-year-old making test debut, but you see them in T20 formats. So you only got to follow what you see. And if you're a young cricketer, you're going to think to yourself, "I want to be a T20 cricketer."

So what could be great for test cricket is — is there a young cricketer? There's a young cricketer, A. Morgan, off Sema — he's only 18. Took a hat-trick. If he can be accelerated and play in the test team — we had Rehan Ahmed just after under-19s, he quickly played for England — more of those stories start happening, then they'll think, "Actually it's possible to play test cricket at a younger age," then T20 cricket.

Host: Yeah. Do we still have a pathways problem — and I say still because we definitely used to. Do we still have a pathways problem in cricket and making it accessible for enough people, enough kids, in this country?

Monty: Well, I think the ECB has recognized that. They're thinking maybe at state school we're not getting enough state school kids — we're getting maybe more private school kids. That's why there's a state school cricket program. There's cricket drones. There's one in Luton, I think Preston, there's going to be one in the West Midlands area. They're opening other areas — one in Bradford as well.

The ECB are recognizing in areas where cricket facilities are not great, they're investing money, creating these programs. There is a slightly disharmony — like they say, if you go to a private school, your coaching is going to be better, your cricket sense is going to be better. When you understand, you're going to speak to a cricket coach, they'll say, "Okay, this is what I need to do." While a state school may not have access to that knowledge. They are trying to enhance that, but there is that gap.

板球的语言、女子板球与全球受众扩张

Host: It's interesting — on the media front, found this as well. A lot of people came up and said it to me: "Oh, I don't understand a lot of the language that people use on cricket. Even if it's on the BBC feed." My wife, who loves cricket — but only really because, you know, as a girl born in the '90s, you didn't get the opportunity to play much — she goes onto BBC Sport and they'll be using all of the cricket language. If you've never played the game, if you've never had the exposure growing up — yeah, we get it, obviously I've been a part of it. But there was just a way of even communicating the game that needs to change. I know that's started to improve. From the media side when you've worked, especially on something like the Hundred — which is now built for this next generation — do you see, even at that level, things starting to shift and trying to make it more accessible, inclusive, understandable for people that haven't had the luxury of growing up in and around cricket environments?

Monty: That's a really interesting point. Coming from a British South Asian background, cricket's massive in India, in Pakistan. Even in England, you follow it, you understand the language. But if you're a neutral fan — let's say a big football fan, or rugby — but you're trying to go into cricket, I think the T20 and the Hundred has helped. We've seen ticket sales rise massively between the age of 25 — we see more people from all different backgrounds getting involved in cricket.

It's helped the female sport massively. The Hundred has been absolutely brilliant for the female cricketers. It's really accelerated their growth. We've got the T20 World Cup happening in England right now. You see the talent — every couple of years, the game is getting faster with the women's game as well. Which is great to see, because sport has always gone quicker and quicker. It's brilliant how the bats — they're getting faster, they're hitting the ball further, fielders are getting quicker. The women's game is accelerating at a really good rate now.

退役后的舞台：媒体、教练、club cricket 与"那一下击中柱头的感觉"

Host: If I said to you, post-cricket now — post-playing — mostly where you enjoy most, where you feel most valuable, where you have the most to add: where is that? Media? Coaching?

Monty: Look, the thing is, in cricket when you're playing for a country, you take a wicket and that buzz is unbelievable. You're trying to replicate that. You're always trying to find that same feeling. That's why sometimes I play a bit of club cricket — if I hit the top of off, even one bail throughout the whole club cricket summer, it makes my summer. That's it. That's all I need. It feels good.

And it's the same thing — what's the next thing that's going to give me the same feeling? That's why I did a course in sports journalism. So hopefully talking about the game from whatever perspective it is — it gives you a buzz. I love working as a commentator, as a pundit, working in the media — which gives me huge satisfaction. Coaching is another area which I would love to go into. It's lovely to help someone and then see them perform and do really well. You still feel connected in their journey. So both areas — I love doing.

"Seven Forms of Income"：第二段事业与组合式人生

Host: You mentioned as well there's a portfolio element to life after sport for a lot of athletes. What you mean by that is it's lots of different things that come together to make this new job — the second innings, as you perfectly described it earlier. Outside of that direct work with cricket, what have you been doing — business front, charitable front, etc.? How's that all packaged up for you?

Monty: So, you need — I was reading a book on finance. Self-help books, we all do, right? Make us all better again. So —

Host: What's the best one?

Monty: Well, I'm still trying to figure that one out.

Host: What's the worst one?

Monty: Oh, yeah. I don't want that to be bottom of the shelf of WH Smith. Why? If I mention that book —

Host: That's the power of the show.

Monty: Exactly. This is the power of this show. As you're going through — Simon Sinek, yeah, exactly — when you're going through Heathrow Airport and it's WH Smith, and I mentioned that book, and they think Monty mentioned it, and it's 50p, it's because of him.

Host: Yeah, exactly. I've dropped it down to the bottom of that shelf.

Monty: So I don't want to mention that book. There's many great books out there to help. But for me — I found that you need seven forms of income if you want to become a millionaire. Let's say I've got a goal — I want to become a millionaire. You need seven forms of income. That comes in: media, playing, coaching, after-dinner speaking, I do TV work, charitable work — which obviously I don't get paid for, but charitable work is great for networking, enhances business opportunities — and creating your own business opportunities. So there you go, I've got seven forms of income already.

That is the challenge for the retired sportsman: you continue to keep yourself busy, but actually having a business — have one or two businesses that is your major cash cow. That is always going to be the challenge for athletes: to develop that.

Host: Did anyone tell you that, or was that something you found out for yourself?

Monty: That's something I found out for myself. The challenge for me in the UK is: is there a business I can develop? Is there someone I can work with on a business that can actually be my longer-term project?

Host: How's it been leveraging the network that you built while playing to help you after your career?

Monty: It's been good. People are very helpful. People want to talk about cricket. I'm a big football fan. I love my boxing. I love all my sports. I love England doing well in all the sports. That's my biggest thing. Now I want England to win the World Cup. That's it. On this show, I'm telling you right now — England are going to win the World Cup.

Host: Do you think?

Monty: Yes, honestly, they're 100% going to win it. They've got all the access, all the resources. There's loads of Arsenal players in that England team right now, and that's going to be the reason they'll win it.

Host: You're going to tell me you're an Arsenal fan now.

Monty: Absolutely. I was there at the parade after 22 years — brought everyone together. I was loving every moment. It was the best feeling in the world.

Host: You're not a Luton fan?

Monty: I am a Luton fan as well.

Host: So you're both?

Monty: Yeah. I had that half-shirt, half Luton, half Arsenal. My loyalties were both. As a football fan, we have irrational behaviors. We have a local team and a Premiership team — a lot of cricket fans or football fans I know disagree with me. They go, "No, you got to have one team." But I speak to them — you have a local team, which is Luton, which I'm even still angry about. On the 93rd minute, they scored, and Steven scored in another game, and they got into the playoffs and Luton didn't. They missed out. But they did well — they won the cup this year against Stockport, which was brilliant.

Host: It must have been perfect for you having Jack Wilshere as manager — an Arsenal legend playing — sorry, managing your other team. That's almost the perfect marriage.

Monty: It is the perfect marriage for me. I wish all the best for Jack Wilshere. I want him to continue to make Luton Town a great club, because he's an Arsenal — played for Arsenal, one of the great Arsenal players. Also, he's going to help my Luton to get back in the Premiership.

Host: Having these different elements to you, these different layers — yes, you're a multiple-format cricketer, but there's also so much else around it that you can now give time to and build on and look to create value in. To some people that is quite scary, but it's also quite exciting, I guess — if you embrace it fully and you go, "What does this now look like for me?"

Monty: Exactly. Like I said, the challenge is: can I make my second innings equally successful as my first, or even beyond that? You're looking for a business opportunity that gives me the same buzz as turning a ball when I played for England. Media does it as well. When I do after-dinner speaking, I drop a few jokes — I love doing that, that's quite good fun. Playing gives me that energy. Media — talking about it — does as well. But the next challenge is: is there a business for me where it gives me the same opportunity, the same buzz, where I can actually take it to the next level?

"粉丝最想问什么" 与 "你最想被问什么"

Host: What do people ask you the most? Is there one thing — you say you're doing an after-dinner speech, or you're doing charity work, or you're doing some media — is there one thing that everyone comes back to? Is it the Ashes?

Monty: Yeah, they talk about my favourite wicket. They talk about my favourite moment in cricket. They'll talk about, "What is it like playing for England?" — all of these kind of things. It is a lot of fun, just to talk about my memories.

Host: Is there anything you'd like to be asked that you're not asked?

Monty: That's a great question. If there's one question I'd like the cricket fans to ask me, is: what did they enjoy about me as a cricketer, and what is it that they liked? That's something for you to ask. But for them to come to me and just say to me — a lot of people say to me, "You gave us a lot of enjoyment, the way you played your cricket, and the way you played it" — which was amazing. But I think that'd be one question.

Host: Can I answer it? Because I'm a fan, right? Look, there you go, I'll give you that answer. The way you played the game is the easy answer. But what it is is: it humanized it. Quite often elite athletes seem very, very far away from what you can achieve, because they won't give you some of the emotion from it. You see a wall, and you see a way of being that is quite blocked off — and that's often the mindset that elite athletes have to get into.

But you did it with a smile. If there was ever a moment to have a bit of fun, you had that fun. You would absolutely dial in when you needed to. But I personally, as a fan, remember watching you, and you know, at that time, cricket — enjoying it, like actually enjoying that interaction. And it would be noticeably different to how I'd feel watching other players. You bridged a lot of the very, very serious landscape of professional sport and made it accessible. You humanized, I think, playing professional cricket.

Monty: I think that's a really good point. Sports people are seen as superheroes, or some people that are distant away — not saying that you're not, of course. I don't know what superpowers I have, but I'm yet to discover. Yeah — big hands.

You look at someone like Lewis Hamilton, Anthony Joshua — you'll think they're so far away. Cristiano Ronaldo — these are like superhuman beings. When I played cricket — I'm not saying I'm on the same bracket as them — but I think the cricket fans felt more of a connection. They thought, "Oh, here's a club cricketer who's playing for England — we can connect with this guy."

Host: And enjoying it.

Monty: And enjoying it.

Host: They weren't afraid to enjoy it in the moment, which most people build their whole careers to get to. You played 50 times for England. It's not like, "Oh, you know, I dropped him for a couple of tests." Half a century of test matches over seven, eight years.

Monty: I wish I played 75. I would have got my MCC membership automatically.

Host: Is that what it is, 75?

Monty: So I missed out on 25. I'm not even on the associate list. I'm going to try and get that however I can.

品牌、赞助商，与数字时代的"无过滤"声音

Monty: It's interesting you talk about what I brought to cricket. I recently had a new bat sponsor up north — Farfire Crest, the bird — and I go to him, "How comes you look to sponsor me?" And he goes, "The reason I want you is because of visibility, because of your brand, your visibility. When we say the name Monty Panesar, it gives us enjoyment, because you bring joy to cricket. Everyone knows who you are, and a lot of people would see that — you'll get eyeballs on my cricket brand. I'd rather give my sponsorship to a cricketer who can bring eyeballs, and is obviously been very lucky to be popular when I played cricket, than someone who's highly skillful but doesn't have the brand or is not a popular cricketer."

That's really interesting — when I had that conversation, it gave me the incentive that actually I need to dial into this and make it my strength. When I'm working on any opportunities — like the media, like the coaching, like businesses — bring this is what I can bring to you guys. Then hopefully people can see the benefits and the value of it.

Host: Well, look — your YouTube channel that you've started is going to be a good way to do that. Because the best thing about those kind of things is that you are your own producer. You can create whatever content you want. No one's telling you how to be or what to do.

Monty: Absolutely. Sports people now — we live in the digital age. Digital journalism is massive at the moment. People want unfiltered, highly opinionated views on things that are happening in sport. The normal cricket fan — the fans are the biggest stakeholders in sport — they want to hear that. They don't want to hear just the same old boring stuff. They want to hear proper in-your-face kind of stuff, highly opinionated, highly judged views — because that's what creates the interest and the traction.

收尾

Host: Look, Monty, it's been amazing having you in to chat. As I said, from watching you for so many years as a fan, you get an impression — and I'm so pleased to see that that impression actually is genuine and real. It's been a lot of fun having a chat with you, and I think you've got some really exciting stuff going forward. But thank you so much for showing an interest, and thank you for wanting to come and join us — because it is a really, really fun thing to do when we get a person that's engaged as you have been today.

Monty: No, thank you so much for the opportunity, for giving me the platform to talk about my journey, about correlation between sports and business, and also my cricketing journey. And hopefully — England win the World Cup.

Host: England win the World Cup.

Monty: And I said it here first, on your podcast.

Host: Said it here first. It'll make a great clip. Thank you.

Monty: Yeah, thank you.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反常识的观察：Monty 的"大手"是一个被忽略的隐喻系统
整段访谈中最容易被一笑而过、却最值得停下来思考的细节，是他反复提到自己的手——比 Tyson Fury 还大的手。表面上这是闲聊，但你把它和他的整段叙事并置看，会发现一种**身体资本（physical capital）向品牌资本（brand capital）的隐秘转译**：
- 年轻时，"大手"是他能从左臂快投转型左臂旋的生物力学前提（biomechanics → 技术）； - 中年时，"大手"成了播客里被反复消费的视觉梗（biomechanics → meme）； - 现在，球拍赞助商 Farfire Crest 看中的不是他的技术指标，而是"你的名字一被说出来，人们就会笑"（meme → eyeballs → money）。
也就是说，他的"大手"经历了**三次资产证券化**：从运动器官，变成技术差异点，再变成可被赞助商定价的注意力资产。运动员退役后的真正难题，不是"再就业"，而是**找到那个能在三种资本之间反复转换的底层资产**。Monty 自己未必完全意识到这一点，但他在用这双手讲一个关于资产形态变化

item.title: [a16z Deep Dives] Why Retention Still Defines Product-Market Fit | Deep Dives with a16z
item.dc:creator: a16z Deep Dives
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：苹果的"无魂设计"恰恰是它的护城河

Josh 在谈到新 Siri 时说了一句容易被忽略的话——"它不需要奉承你，不需要说'你今天看起来真棒'"。这看似平淡，实则是对整个 AI 消费市场的一次精准切割。

当全行业（ChatGPT、Character.ai、Replika）都在争夺"陪伴"和"情感共鸣"这块高地时，苹果反其道而行之，把 Siri 定义为"有灵魂但不假装是人"的工具。这是一个极其昂贵的战略选择——它主动放弃了最容易产生留存和 DAU 的情感粘性维度，转而押注"工具性人格"。

这里藏着一个跨领域的连接：这非常像日本的"職人精神"对比美国硅谷的"产品人格化"。日本匠人做的茶碗不会试图跟你"对话"，但它在你手中的每一次温度反馈都在和你交流。苹果在 AI 时代复活的是这种"道具的克制"——而这恰恰是大多数 AI 创业公司学不来的，因为它们必须靠拟人化来制造留存。

二、被掩盖的悖论：Robin Hood 的"彩票化推荐"揭示了金融产品的游戏化本质

Josh 详细拆解了 Robin Hood 推荐机制的关键——给的不是 10 美元，而是一股随机价值的股票。这个细节被他轻描淡写，但其实是金融产品史上一次极其大胆的认知劫持。

传统 Give 10/Get 10 模式触发的是"经济理性"脑回路（这值不值得我推荐朋友？）；而"给你一股随机股票"触发的是老虎机脑回路——多巴胺的不确定性奖励。Robin Hood 在做的不是降低 CAC，而是把"开户"这个最枯燥的金融行为，包装成了拆盲盒。

跨领域连接：这和Pokémon GO 的"步行孵蛋"机制、Duolingo 的连击系统本质同构——都是把"延迟反馈的严肃行为"用即时性赌博机制重新编码。Robin Hood 的真正发明，不是免佣金交易，而是把金融启蒙游戏化。这也解释了为什么 GameStop 事件会发生在它身上而不是 Schwab——它从一开始就培养了一群把市场当游戏的用户。

三、最容易被忽略的预言：Gen Alpha 会成为"社交顶级捕食者"

对话尾声有一个被一笔带过却极具杀伤力的判断——这一代孩子可能因为 AI 反而重新精通对话艺术。这是反直觉中的反直觉。

主流叙事是："AI 让孩子失去社交能力。" 但 Josh 与主持人捕捉到了一个反向回路：当 AI 让你能安全地、低成本地、无限次地排练困难对话（第一次约会、谈判、道歉、表白），社交能力就从"天赋型技能"变成了"可训练技能"。

这里有一个深刻的跨领域连接：这等同于飞行模拟器对飞行员培训的颠覆。在飞行模拟器出现前，飞行员的核心瓶颈是"真实飞行小时数"——这是高成本、高风险的。模拟器把它变成了可任意重复的廉价输入。结果不是飞行员变弱，而是出现了一批历史上最熟练、最冷静的飞行员。

如果这个类比成立，那意味着：未来真正具有社交统治力的人，不是天生外向的人，而是把 AI 当作"社交飞行模拟器"训练过万小时的人。这对约会、销售、外交、咨询行业都会是一次重新洗牌。一个隐藏的产品机会：为"高风险对话"提供的私人模拟器，可能比任何 productivity tool 都更具留存力。

四、贯穿全片的隐藏主线：留存是一切，但定义已经变了

Josh 反复回到"retention is everything"——但他真正说的是一件更深的事。在 AI 时代，留存的定义从"你今天还来吗"变成了"你愿意为它放弃多少旧习惯"。ChatGPT 不是赢在 DAU，而是赢在它逼着用户主动卸载 Google 习惯。这是行为经济学里所谓的"沉没习惯成本切换"——一旦发生，就是单向门。

所以未来的衡量标准可能不是 retention curve，而是 abandonment curve：你的产品让用户抛弃了多少旧动作？这是一个还没有被 SaaS 仪表板正式命名的指标，但它可能是 AI 消费产品估值的真正锚。

📝 转录文本

Why Retention Still Defines Product-Market Fit

Deep Dives with a16z — 对话 Josh Elman

开场：AI 不该只关于"替代"

"Right now, AI has been so much about productivity, job replacement, work replacement, and I think we have a moment to shift that — which is how do these new tools help you get more out of your own day and your own life and the things you want to do."

主持人： So, it sounds like you're saying retention is still the most important metric. Is that true?

Josh： I mean, I think it's always been.

关于 Apple、ChatGPT 与"实验室会赢一切"的疑问

"You've been a part of really every product cycle, the big ones and the small ones, and now most recently leading product marketing for a lot of the AI efforts at Apple. One of the things I learned a lot at Apple was how do we tell a compelling story to regular people? One of those things that the moment you use it, you're like, 'Oh my god, I can never go back to the old way.' We forget that technology is no longer the underdog. The parts of the products we've made are running the world."

"You know, we used to say real life, but online life is real life now."

"ChatGPT was amazing for a whole bunch of searches that we thought were totally solved through Google. And all of a sudden, you start putting those searches into ChatGPT, you're like, 'This answer is more than 10 times better. This experience is more than 10 times better than Google links back and forth.' And I think that's why ChatGPT one."

"One topic that we discuss a lot is — are the labs just going to win it all? Should we all just go home? And I know it feels like we have a version of this conversation every time there's a new product cycle. What's your view?"

"So I think a funny way to think about this is…"

欢迎 Josh Elman 加入 a16z

主持人： Welcome back to the Horowitz podcast. I'm here with a person that I've been friends with for a long time, admired for a long time, was my investor for a brief time, and now I'm proud to call him partner — Josh Elman, welcome.

Josh： Thanks so much for having me. I'm really excited about this.

主持人： It's going to be awesome. So Josh, everybody probably already knows him, but you've been a part of every important consumer story. I don't know if that's because you've been the catalyzing force behind them, or perhaps you're a good picker.

Josh： Sometimes it's just showing up at the right place at the right time and being on the journeys, but it's been really incredible to get to be part of creating technology that we all use every day in our lives.

在斯坦福立下的"志向"

主持人： I was reading some of the background and it sounds like you sort of set an intention at Stanford to be a part of the intersection of culture and technology — and you've kind of done that.

Josh： It's kind of crazy. My resume had this objective: "to create technology that changes people's lives." As I think back on that, I wish it was even more like "benefits people's lives," "makes people's lives even better." Because changes — technology is kind of neutral. But there's so much that we've done that's just tried to make the world better, more connected, more human, and give us all these new tools and powers that we never could have had before.

技术从"边缘玩物"到"运行世界"

主持人： It's interesting because you came from a time when, when you graduated, technology was these weirdos in the back of the classroom, it always felt like a toy. And now it's something that some of the most important conversations in our society are about technology.

Josh： They're about technology, they're happening through technology. We used to joke that if you got to 10 million users, you were impacting so many people in the world. And then it became 100 million, and then a billion, and then billions every hour. We forget that technology is no longer the underdog. The parts of the products we've made are running the world.

It's like — we used to say "real life," but online life is real life now. I think it is sort of default.

主持人： I think the pandemic proved that for us most of all.

Josh： We kind of maxed out on how much you could actually do in the online life. There were so many things where we would meet over Zoom — family reunions, friend groups, birthdays, dinners. We totally maxed out what that was. And I think what's been really cool as we've come out of that is so much of that has lasted and has made a lot of things more natural. We still like to sometimes connect over video, but now we also are getting much more back in the real world, and technology can do a lot more to bring that together.

Josh 的简历：从 Facebook 到 Apple

主持人： So before we get too deep into it, let me frame up the background. You've been a part of really every product cycle — Facebook, LinkedIn, Twitter (now known as X) in varying roles, TikTok / Musical.ly, Discord, Robinhood, and now most recently leading product marketing for a lot of the AI efforts at Apple.

Josh： When you put that together, it's been an incredible run. But what I just came off with Apple was a real change for me. About 6 years ago, I decided — I'd never worked at a large company, and I thought we were in a place where startups were actually going to struggle against some of the big companies. I was like, let me go try the other side and learn how that works. I got this incredible opportunity to work at Apple. What a fabulous company. I've had the best time working there.

在 Apple 的六年：从 App Store 到 Apple Intelligence

主持人： Information is pretty fluid in our industry, but Apple's famously opaque. What did you change your mind on through the Apple experience? And before that — tee up exactly what you worked on.

Josh： When I joined Apple about 6 years ago, I started in the App Store business — a giant business for Apple, both in discovery through the App Store client and as a whole platform for in-app purchases through games and apps. The question was: how do we actually help people discover more, and how do we help people get more out of their apps?

We had this vision to launch the Games app — something you could go to every day to figure out what to play, what was happening in the games already downloaded on your device, or what your friends were doing. That launched a couple years ago.

But then AI started happening, and I got an opportunity to move over and start up — or restart — the product marketing function around AI for Apple. We had these visions of what personal intelligence was going to be. How could you really bring that to people embedded in the device, where all your content is, all your data?

Siri's been around for a long time. It hadn't been as successful or as well-reputed as people had hoped. And I think there was a huge opportunity with what was happening in Gen AI to rebuild Siri. I got to be part of that over the past two and a half years. It's been a very interesting roller coaster. But I'm so proud with what was just announced two weeks ago at WWDC. I mean, the story of what Siri AI is — I'm using it every day and it's fantastic.

什么叫"个人智能"？

主持人： We've had a lot of intelligence over the last three years, but arguably less personal intelligence. Talk about the personal part. What does that uniquely unlock?

Josh： What's really special with these assistants is not just what they can help you do or navigate in the world and bring world knowledge — but what they can understand about you. And it's not just what you ask it over time, but having access to all your information. When you trust it, your device has your mail, your messages, your calendar, notes, anything you save. Being able to have an assistant that can tap into that and answer questions.

I had this experience about a month ago when I was beta testing this — I was driving to a dinner, and all of a sudden I freaked out and was like, "Oh my god, am I going to the right place?" I hadn't added it to my calendar. I just knew I was meeting a friend for dinner. We had texted about it. I just said, "Navigate me to dinner." And it found that message and got me right there. I was a few blocks off — if I had gone to the wrong restaurant because I just had remembered it wrong…

This morning I was trying to figure out where to park, and someone had texted me a whole bunch of different parking locations. I just was able to go, "Hey, where should I park?" And it goes and finds the information that's there and pulls it together. That's what you want from an assistant.

主持人： Very cool. It feels like it lives in a unique part of the consumer ecosystem — something that doesn't feel directly overlapping with the startup today.

Josh： I think that's right. We've always kind of expected Siri to be great and understand you and understand everything. I'm so glad I can't wait for everyone to get to play with it — but it also reminded me just how many more opportunities there are in the world.

消费级 AI 的现状：被 productivity 主导的市场

主持人： There's been obviously a lot happening around AI, machine intelligence broadly. I'd say a lot of it, at least for startups, has been more B2B oriented than consumer oriented. What's the state of consumer today?

Josh： We're in a really interesting spot. ChatGPT in many ways is a consumer app — one of the biggest consumer apps that's come on like a storm in three and a half short years. Because it's how do we navigate the world, the information we have access to, things we want to do, places we want to go spend our time and energy. How do we connect with other people?

Right now AI has been so much about productivity, job replacement, work replacement. And I think we have a moment to shift that — how do these new tools help you get more out of your own day and your own life and the things you want to do?

It's a massive paradigm shift in interface. We haven't even all processed what it's going to mean when everything can start with natural language. When you get options and you're able to explore across a variety of options right there in a really dynamic interface that gets generated for you. When you have an idea and you just want to create an app or a website or an experience that's very personal to you, and you can just express what you want.

Everybody's talking about how you can create something from scratch — it's amazing, you can code in ways you never could before. But even if something's already coded and working, you can tinker with it and ask it to be customized in the way that you want. I don't think we've unpacked much of this for consumer yet.

为什么社交时代的创新没有在 AI 里重现？

主持人： To wind it back to the social era, we had a lot of entertainment apps, a lot of social apps, a lot of connection apps. We're not seeing that today with AI. Is that an ecosystem problem? Are founders just not interested?

Josh： Part of it is some of us think a lot of those problems had been solved. We had to find ways in the early days of social networking with LinkedIn, Facebook, then Twitter — people didn't have a way to even connect. LinkedIn was like "you're going to put your resume online" — people were like, "What? I would never put where I've worked online — that's only if I'm looking for a job." Facebook was like "Connect to everybody you've met in high school and college, throughout your life."

主持人： Terrifying.

Josh： And we did. And then we figured out how much more fluid that makes the world. How you can get information that isn't just brokered through a few channels, but through the people you care about, learn about, and are interested in. We've seen a lot of the negative implications of that, too. But on the positive side, we're so much more connected to who we are and what we're interested in.

When you think of AI right now, you think of AI as just short-circuiting things you already do. That's what a lot of people are building, but they're not exposing us to new ways to connect, new ways to get out in the world, new ways to solve problems we're trying to solve in our lives. We're going to have to figure out those 10x things that replace some of those habits we've already formed.

"伟人理论"与自我探索

主持人： I think a bunch about this great man / great woman theory of history. It feels like the intersection of technology and us as people has mostly been intermediated through groups, as you said, in social. Now we have a chance to kind of be that great man / great woman via this technology. It's a very different shape — not just productivity, but a different shape of entertainment and exploration as well. What are the sides of yourself that are just totally undiscovered still?

Josh： With AI, you can go down these self journeys. One of the challenges with AI is it can also be very self-reinforcing — if you're going down this very solo journey with a companion app or something, you can get in this mindset that you don't need the rest of the world. The products that can take that energy, that exploration, those groups, that "great man theory," and put us in a place where it's encouraging us to actually do different things and get out in the world and connect with other people — and do things in the background that help us be in a better place to do that — there's a ton of things still unexplored.

Agent 会进我们的群聊吗？

主持人： Do you think we're going to have agents in group chats with us? What is that going to look like? Do we have to establish social norms?

Josh： I ultimately think we're going to have this set of agents or things working on our behalf, or things in the background solving things for us, that we'll talk to just like we talk to regular people — but we'll expect different things from it. We'll expect a deeper understanding. When I give it hints, it'll be much better.

And then I think it will actually help us interact with other people. "Hey, I'm going to go talk to Anish's agent or negotiate this thing, and then we're going to make sure you guys are set up to have this great conversation."

主持人： It's a level of indirection — which has a lot of interesting implications for work. There's so much conflation of "what is the truth" with "what is personal to me." The likes and dislikes and disagreements — even intellectual — often lead to emotionally charged situations. I'm optimistic, or at least hopeful, that agents will diffuse a lot of that, and work can be more about the honest pursuit of truth.

Josh： I hope so too. The best work happens when you've got the right information in front of you. One of the things I learned a lot at Apple was how important the culture of debate was. To get to the best answers, you need to really discuss them. But the only way you can have real debate is to have as much information and thought prepared ahead of time. That was the hard work. Agents will help us be more prepared and ready to have better debates.

Agent 和 App 是替代关系吗？

主持人： Do you think there's a tension between agents and apps? Are agents the final app?

Josh： A lot of people have used this moment to ask: is this the death of apps, because you'll just get to everything through a command line? I'm very much a believer that apps and experiences — being able to visualize and explore information, or spend time in an app consuming content — none of that's going to go away.

Through these agents we'll more quickly get to what we're looking for, more quickly get into an app where everything's set up right. But I definitely think we're going to want to spend more time in rich experiences instead of just back and forth in chat.

主持人： Is chat an intermediary then, or is it an interface that's having this renaissance and it's here to stay?

Josh： It's an incredible starting point, an incredible substrate. Think of human language — how do we have most of our conversations? They are dialogues back and forth. But after we're dialoguing, we then want to explore information and see things in bigger ways.

Look at games — chat's never going to replace a game. Like a chat game like Zork that I used to play when I was a kid…

主持人： Great reference.

Josh： That is very different than a rich immersive experiential game. You're never going to replace that, but chat is a great way to get started and help me think through something before I'm ready to have that experience.

Roblox / Minecraft 一代的期待

主持人： Games is a great way to think of how these two interfaces can reinforce each other. If you're playing a modern RPG, there are these graphic-heavy experiences, but a lot of fun is in talking to the NPCs and exploring the dialogue — all of that should get a lot richer.

Josh： This is a huge area for consumer invention — immersive stories and experiences and new kinds of entertainment that become the beginnings of worlds. We have a generation of kids growing up on Minecraft and Roblox. They're used to playing a game where they controlled the world, where they chose to join the world. Roblox is this incredible co-experience platform.

They're going to expect, as that group grows up, more and more experiences like that — that are rich, that allow you that sense of control, ownership, tinkering, privacy in this bigger world. But the world should be so much richer because of AI, and so much more open-ended.

主持人： So does that mean the Gen Alpha coming out, the Roblox/Minecraft generation, will expect to be able to fork and remix all their utility apps?

Josh： Honestly, I think so. Every piece of software still has to do a lot of its core functionality — not every Gen Alpha is going to start their accounting software from zero, or their travel planner from zero. But once they've got the thing that does most of those functions, they're going to want to tinker with it. The apps that will win in the next generation will give people that sense of ownership at the end.

分发：从病毒传播到创作者经济

主持人： It leads to a natural question — when it's trivial to create anything, how do you discover things? How scarce is attention? Talk about distribution broadly.

Josh： This has been a really interesting wave of the internet — how much of it's powered brand-new distribution methods. We both grew up through the social era where most of it was virality. A lot of techniques and tactics and testing of how to drive more virality — how to get one person so excited they would share it. Sometimes some people over-hacked this with inadvertent address-book invites. That never worked. It was always the intentional viral sharing: "this is so great, I want you to join me."

Then we got so many of those that everybody got jaded — "why are you inviting me to another thing?" — and all of a sudden you're going to stop doing that. So we moved into the search world. Being search-optimized for Google was a massive thing.

What we've seen the past 5 or 10 years that are really the working methods now are the trust we have in these new creator relationships. Sometimes they're true social relationships, sometimes somewhat parasocial. Creators are able to talk about the things they're doing and exploring. You learn to trust that this person isn't just doing it to make money — they're doing it because they're passionate about what they're tinkering with.

That's the new path of discovery. You have to be so good that you're getting those people to talk about it. Sometimes you can pay for those endorsements, but everything only ultimately works if it's true organic word of mouth.

创作者 vs. 传统社交网络

主持人： Let me challenge you a little. There's something intellectually dissatisfying about creators being the next-gen channel versus the traditional networks. In the old days, the value of the Insta network, Facebook network, X network was so powerful. Creators just feels like a weaker form of that.

Josh： I'd argue it's the biggest form of that — because most creators have earned their reputation based on trust. In the olden days, a very famous athlete would get up and endorse a cereal, and you're like, "there's no way that athlete is eating that cereal every day." But now when a creator says "here's my health routine, here's my travel routine, I went to these places, I liked these two but this one wasn't as good" — you learn to trust them.

Discord is another good example — all through influence. But it wasn't mass influencers. Sometimes it was streamers going "we're playing on Discord now." But most of it was the person who gathers everybody for games going, "Hey, I'm trying this new tool, it's way better than what we've been using — everybody jump on it." That one person would bring everybody in their group. Then a few people would go "that was really cool, I also play games with this group." It's not even micro-influencer — it's pure word of mouth that scales all the way down.

AI 原生分发渠道 vs. GEO（生成式优化）

主持人： Do you think that's the channel for AI native consumer products? Or is it going to be something like ChatGPT's apps directory, Codex, or something else?

Josh： I think we're going to be back in the same world where people need this spread, this word of mouth — whether accelerated through creators or just through individuals being so excited. At the same time, you're going to need to figure out GEO — generative optimization — where the agents talk about you, and when someone's trying to solve a problem, the agents will actually do that.

There's huge opportunity in directories, in content for model training or RAG, or ultimately whether these products become really smart and realize that referring things out makes them better. Right now I don't think any of the core assistants have that mentality — that referring people out makes them stronger. But I think that's the biggest opportunity.

主持人： What do you mean "referring people out"?

Josh： When I start trying to solve a problem with ChatGPT or Claude, it does everything it can to solve my problem itself. "Hey, I want to plan a trip." "Hey, I want to figure out a good bottle of wine." What would be really cool is if it said, "Hey, I know you're trying to plan trips — here are some things, and here are the resources to go deeper." They can't own them all. Being smarter about knowing when to hand you off — that'll be great. It'll create a lot of opportunity for those tools, and perhaps those tools are apps.

A lot of them will become apps and their own services. They'll have some chat experience, but a lot more — when you're planning a trip, chat's great to get a list of stuff, but then you've got to turn it into bookings and lots of other details. When you're dealing with a financial plan, it's great to work through problems but you still need to turn it into actual details. Those artifacts will be better managed in other services.

留存仍是最重要的指标

主持人： Are creators sufficient for AI consumer products to get to scale, or do we need an AI-native distribution channel?

Josh： I think the short answer is there's a lot of ways to get to scale today. People can get to 10 million, 50 million users in ways you never could before. All that really matters is if you get to them, do they actually use you? Do you stick? Do you become part of their lives?

I'm less worried about finding distribution methods for awareness as much as: what are the products that are actually going to move our habits and change us? Part of being an AI product today is it has to be so much better than the products were before.

ChatGPT was amazing for a whole bunch of searches that we thought were totally solved through Google. All of a sudden you start putting those searches into ChatGPT — "this answer is more than 10 times better, this experience is more than 10 times better than Google links back and forth." That's why ChatGPT blew up.

For every experience consumers have today, with these new possibilities of building things differently, presenting them differently, experiencing them differently — the moment you use it, you're like, "Oh my god, I can never go back to the old way."

主持人： So it sounds like you're saying retention is still the most important metric. Is that true?

Josh： It's always been this idea of — do you really use the product? If you can get me to try something… what's really cool about this era is the willingness to try is bigger than it's always been. That's part of what got me so excited to come back to the startup side and venture side. We can actually get people to try new things. But trying new things only matters if people stick and bring it into their lives in a way they're so excited to tell the next people.

Everybody asks "how do you get to this many users so fast?" The answer is: don't. Get to a smaller number who have moved their lives over to your product, who have truly found something new they will not want to go back from. Once you find that, then it becomes how do we repeat that?

Robinhood 案例：愿景与小步聚焦

主持人： There's some non-intuitive wisdom in that — like Paul Graham's whole thing about monopolizing a really small market being better than being diffuse across a big market. Andrew talks about the way to build a big network is to build a bunch of small networks. But we're so over-trained on this "go big, billion users" thing.

Josh： You have to be able to think in both at the same time. The best founders I've gotten to work with figured out how to have the vision of what is billion-user scale and why is the world different when everybody's using it — but then go, "I'm not going to get there today. Today I've just got to get to this, win this area, convince this group to become passionate."

Robinhood is an incredible example. They had a vision to democratize finance for everybody — imagine anybody in the world who didn't formally have access to the stock market, to the type of investing products other people had access to, being able to give that to everybody. But that didn't mean overnight needing every single person able to do it in their retirement account. It started very small — if you are actively thinking about wanting to invest, or are actively already trading, we should be a better product for you. Then over time, how can we earn the right to expand the platform to everybody else? But not launch with 30 products today — that would have confused everybody and landed no one.

Robinhood 的获客魔法：股票推荐机制

主持人： I'm glad you mentioned Robinhood — there are two companies we talk about as exceptions in consumer lore from a distribution perspective: Robinhood and TikTok. You've been involved in both. The way we tell the story for Robinhood is that they did do significant paid acquisition, but because of this very brilliant mechanic — give a stock, get a stock — you had a non-inflationary CAC versus being in an auction with Facebook and Google. So they were able to scale even though it was paid.

Josh： Robinhood didn't start paid. They started with a product they thought was just a better way to invest. Let's remove the cost of commission — back then it cost five bucks or more a trade with the other big platforms. They said, "we're going to figure out how to give it away for free. We know there are other ways we can monetize." They found several mechanisms through the ecosystem.

The key was it was all word of mouth at the beginning. They were on Reddit — there were Reddit communities really excited about it. Anybody just word-of-mouth on mobile saying "I kind of want to invest in the stock market — this app is great." And it had beautiful design. One thing you needed when dealing with people's money was trust. Beautiful design showed people cared and put a lot into it. That really helped Robinhood have that initial credibility.

Over time, figuring out "okay, how do we grow from here?" — they tried a lot of things. Definitely tried some paid ads. They worked. Free trading was a message that worked for people already in a trading mindset, but if you weren't, it wasn't.

But the referral program was really magic. There were a lot of referral programs at the time — Airbnb, Uber had "give 10, get 10." Those are nice because you have a little discount, but it's a dollar amount. You're like, "is it worth 10 bucks? Am I trading off my friend for 10 bucks?" Robinhood — "here's 10 bucks to invest" — what do I even invest in for 10 bucks? You couldn't even buy a share.

So the team came up with this mechanic: give and get a share — an actual share — and used the fact that the share was a lottery. Sometimes it would be a $2 stock, sometimes a $100 stock. Enough people got the $100 stock that they raved about it. People who got the $2 stock said, "oh, I have a whole share of stock — I can sell that, I have $2 in my account I didn't have before." Robinhood's ability to balance that made it a very cost-effective mechanism.

When you're doing paid ads to bring in people already susceptible to the message, then bringing in this referral program where people share and advocate it — referral is not quite as pure as word of mouth, but it's close. I'm only going to bother somebody if I at least believe in it, or the incentive is so good. It became this powerful mechanism. The brand kept building. Design got better. One day you're like, "whoa, this is no longer the little upstart financial thing — every other company is dropping commissions because they now see the threat." Schwab did it, Ameritrade did it, Schwab bought TD Ameritrade. The brokerages got transformed — all from this little plucky "let's democratize finance, but get the people we can early."

主持人： It demonstrates the power of a founder company even being post-public — Vlad is doing AI trading stuff. He's leaned the most into it.

Josh： I'm just blown away by what they've done since I've been gone. I left at the end of 2019. It's turned into this incredible multi-platform — no longer just trading. Retirement, credit card, banking components. Vlad has done an incredible job navigating the seas. They went through bumpy times too — crazy downtimes in the stock market, GameStop felt like it might be existential for the brand. But they came back to their core values: great design, great product, trust for the user, something people are proud to talk about and share. They've really been able to win people back and grow a ton.

TikTok / Musical.ly 的故事

主持人： Okay, so we've established Robinhood was a referral program. Now talk about Musical.ly / TikTok. Not everybody may know the connection. They're the other great exception — anytime we say "paid doesn't really work at scale," people say "but TikTok."

Josh： This is a crazy multi-arc story. Musical.ly was a small team in China trying to build for the Western markets. They were actually starting trying to build an education app — hadn't really worked. They had seen some fun things happening around lip-syncing, and decided they'd build a little lip-sync app.

They put it on the App Store, called it "lip sync," and were seeing spikes every week. Alex, the founder of Musical.ly (and who ran a lot of TikTok), told me they were so confused. They finally found out the spikes on Thursday night were because of when Lip Sync Battle aired on MTV. Lip Sync Battle would run, people would go type "lip sync" into the App Store, download Musical.ly, and start creating lip-syncs.

Musical.ly designed their product in two really smart ways. First, it was really easy to take your video and post it elsewhere. You could create a great lip-sync video and post it to Instagram. Second, they curated a feed of the great lip-sync videos. When you'd open the app, you'd swipe through, add some friends, and create your video. They might post it on Instagram.

That loop was: create something you couldn't create anywhere else, then post it out to Instagram and your friends. You'd start following your friends, see what they had posted alongside other really cool stuff being curated.

They found out there were a bunch of B+ stars on Instagram who weren't quite getting enough credibility — only posting pictures (Instagram video was just coming out at the time). They were like, "let me use Musical.ly to create some cool content for my Instagram feed." And on Musical.ly, they became the number one stars, the A-listers, because they were getting featured in the feed.

Now you had this loop: influencers posting tons of stuff on Instagram, people seeing Musical.ly on Instagram, having a better experience in the feed, talking about it with friends. It all started to compound. The other secret was there was always a little Musical.ly bug at the bottom of the Instagram video. You could download the video — unheard of at the time. Create the video in Musical.ly, download it, upload it to Instagram, and it would keep the Musical.ly bug. People weren't cutting that out. It created this great loop.

The company got really big — tens and tens of millions of users daily, hundreds had tried it. Then they started thinking whether to keep going independently, raise more, or sell. Got an incredible offer from ByteDance. ByteDance had a product, Douyin, that was very large in China — they had modeled some after Musical.ly, had their own invention, had a great AI team. They were like, "we can help Musical.ly scale even more worldwide." So they bought Musical.ly.

They spent about six months tinkering, rebuilding the product, moving Musical.ly and Douyin into a shared codebase. Then they relaunched it with ads, with dollars — because to get from here to where we want to go, we need to pour a lot more into it. They were probably one of the biggest spenders on Facebook, Twitter, every social media to get people to download TikTok at that time.

But the product loop was so good that everybody retained. You'd sign up, and with a couple swipes you were deeply personalized. Your friends already on Musical.ly were coming back trying new things. It was really energizing — the whole loop worked. It still took them pouring a lot of money in to get distribution, but the retention really worked.

I joke at the same time, Quibi was pouring similar amounts of money to get people to download Quibi — which didn't totally work.

主持人： Didn't work as well.

Josh： So is the lesson — if you have a highly retained working loop, you can do paid and it's okay?

Josh： I have no problem with people doing paid if they understand it. Paid to bring in people who will become very good retained users and spread the word can be a great catalyst. What you can't do is spend a lot of money to burn 10 people to find the one who actually stuck — and think that scales. That only gets more expensive.

消费者付费意愿与推理成本

主持人： Adjacent to this is a debate we've had: it's amazing that consumers are willing to pay for software in a big way. Top ChatGPT SKU is $250 a month. Top Grok SKU is $300 a month. Gemini Ultra I think is $300. Consumers are paying for software. On the other hand, because there's no zero marginal cost of distribution — inference costs money — you can't really build a mass market free product without a massive balance sheet. Is it better, worse? Is it just changing the shape of startups?

Josh： This willingness to pay and necessity to pay is definitely shifting. 15-20 years ago everything was shifting to the cloud, and the startup cost became much less. You didn't have to buy a bunch of servers and rack them. Sure, serving cost a little money in bandwidth and compute, but relatively low for what you were getting.

Now if you're running tons of inference, you need your customers willing to pay for it. They are. The ability to pay in apps is so much easier than it's ever been. Getting payments is easy if you're providing value.

I think it hurts companies that need massive network effects — because part of the benefit of massive network effects was free distribution to everybody. I haven't totally figured out what the balance is, but that's what I'm excited to work through over the next few years with various startups.

主持人： It seems like one thing that could really change that is if iPhones get really powerful GPUs.

Josh： We're going to be figuring out, with the models you can run on iPhones or laptops, how to push more of the less-complex inference onto device to lower the cost. There's no reason that everything you're ever trying to do — refining a title, rewriting a paragraph, answering basic questions — can't use models that are incredibly powerful on the device. Then you only send the more complex stuff up. From both enterprise and consumer sides, really smart founders will optimize that.

主持人： I know Karpathy has done a bunch of work, a few folks have shown you can actually train and run models on 386s and 486s. There's a lot of good old-fashioned engineering that will push more and more to the edge.

实验室会赢一切吗？

主持人： One topic we discuss a lot: are the labs just going to win it all? Should we all just go home? It feels like we have a version of this conversation every product cycle. What's your view?

Josh： A funny way to think about this is — 20 years ago, everybody was like, "won't Google just do it?" Then Google could do everything. Certainly Google has expanded in a bunch of categories. But there's a ton of startups that have grown and become massive companies since then.

15 years ago, 2010 era, everybody was like, "well, isn't Apple just going to do it all? Mobile apps will all be their own apps." We had this great world of mobile-enabled apps and social apps we could have never imagined.

About 10 years ago, I kind of started thinking that too — I wasn't sure if you could truly build another consumer startup from scratch in these networks that are now 1 billion plus users. It's part of why I actually left venture to go build again. I went to Robinhood, which led me over to Apple. I said, "gosh, I'm not sure if you can build something big enough in pure consumer areas."

I'm not sure if I was wrong. ChatGPT obviously found it. I had invested in Discord. We had just sold Musical.ly. Those were going to make it through. I joined Robinhood — that one's going to make it through. But starting from scratch, you have to get enough momentum to get to that billion-user scale. I wasn't so sure. If I didn't really believe I could find that path in venture, I was going to work on them instead. Part of why I went to Apple was to learn and work at the biggest scale and try to deliver value to everybody's pocket.

But right now, I think the world is so open again. The main chatbots, the main assistants, are going to do a lot for us — we're going to rely on them for a lot of things. But we're going to expect everything to work like them. And we're going to rewire so much else of what we do in life — our services, the way we get things done — that the opportunities to fill all those areas of our lives, help us connect, help us get information, help us explore, are not just going to go to "there's only two products we ever use."

创业公司能做但大公司做不了的事

主持人： I totally agree. There are ways startups can play that incumbents and labs can't. Companion is such a great example — it touches on everything that every committee at a big tech company doesn't want you to do. There's disagreement, probabilistic behavior, sexuality — all these ways you can interact with a product that are very human. At really big scale, it's hard to ship things like that.

Josh： Having been part of one of the biggest companies, learning to ship things that matter and make the world better is kind of the fundamental value of what we do — and the fundamental value of what you can do at that scale. But when I think about companion products and entertainment and how AI can take us on these journeys — it's incredible how much different it can be than everything we've had in the past. We've never had an experience like that.

On the other hand, it's a high level of responsibility to be very careful — with kids, with safety, with sexuality, with letting people who might be going through mental challenges feel they're getting something served through a product that isn't bringing them back to humanity or helping them get the help they need. We have to find that balance.

But once you accept that's the baseline — there is so much to explore that not every product wants to be. Lots of products don't want to be your best friend. If you've used the new Siri — it's incredible, but it's very much trying to help you get things done. It's designed to help you get things done with the experiences and content you already have, what it knows about you, what it can access in the world. It doesn't need to be sycophantic. It doesn't need to say "you look great today" and create that personality.

But there are other experiences where you might want opinions on a wardrobe and how you dress, give you tips, treat you a little bit more in that human relationship. That's a very different product.

不同 AI 产品的不同灵魂

主持人： If you unbundle the two — there's a product that's just about getting things done, almost has no soul by design. We need that. But then there are products that might mirror things we do in the rest of the world. I might join a volleyball team because I'm looking for friends, and volleyball is a level of indirection to building new adult male friendships. There will also be companion products that look like that.

Josh： I agree 100%. There are so many that can help us do more of that and explore the world, do things with other people in the background, figure out what makes sense for you. And I'd argue even the "get things done" has a soul — but a very different soul. It's not trying to replace any human relationships. None of this really is — it's all trying to augment relationships. It's not trying to make you perceive it in some sort of human way. It's trying to really be a tool. That's what we are as technologists — we're tool makers. We make things, and they can be net neutral, and it's our job to infuse humanity into them.

主持人： I love that. It does feel like this is a technology to explore our humanity — and we've never had that before. We've had a lot of technology to extend our intellect. That's let us do incredible things — SpaceX this week is the best example. But we really haven't had any technology that's helped us be a little more self-reflective. Social products are fascinating, but exploring us as groups instead of us as individuals.

Josh： That's a really profound way to think about what we can do with this new technology. So much of it is helping you be better. I've seen these great little apps helping people learn to date by practicing first-date conversations. It sounds funny — we're both older and not in that lifestyle right now — but if you're nervous about something, being able to practice is incredible. I haven't done one of these in a long time. I have to admit, I did use a little bit of going back and forth to ChatGPT to just practice talking and getting back into this mindset. That's how these things help us be better.

Gen Alpha：最强的社交一代？

主持人： It would just be so funny and ironic if the result of all this technology is that Gen Alpha — they're just the most incredible social apex predators. The art of conversation is back. It's like the 1950s.

Josh： That would be amazing. We always forget — but that's the generation they've grown up with. They've grown up fully online, fully mobile. We've seen some downsides. A lot of things are happening right now to even prevent social media for people under certain ages — mostly because we haven't all done the work to make these as safe and additive as possible. On the other hand, this generation is going to know how to adapt and get the most out of this technology, hopefully in better service of others. Hopefully look at examples of what's gone right in the world that they can live up to — and what's gone wrong that they can say it's our turn to go fix.

主持人： It's fascinating — this class graduating right now is the first class that grew up, had ChatGPT for all of college. What I think ChatGPT or broad AI lets you do is what they call "the greats" in college — studying ancient Roman, ancient Greek, everything related to it, an individualistic very abstract intellectual exploration of things just for the sake of it. That's a lot of what the technology lets you do. So I hope they are as enthusiastic about it as we are.

Josh： I really hope so. One thing that's really heartwarming for me when I talk to kids is when they tell me, "Hey, I don't use it to do my work because honestly I just know that's not what I'm going to get graded on — I might get caught or something else. But what I use it for is to help me make my work better." The fact that you can turn to ChatGPT when you've done some writing and go, "How do I improve this?" — that's such a powerful tool to have in your arsenal.

I've been using new Siri on my Mac — you write something and they go, "How's this sound? How could I spice this up? How could I get my point across better?" The tips really help you go, "okay, that's a really good tip — I'm going to put that into what I'm writing." When you use it that way, these tools are very additive as opposed to replacement.

Eugenia 的洞见：花时间 vs. 省时间

主持人： Somebody we both know, Eugenia — you might have introduced me to her, or at least whispered a good word on my behalf. Thank you for that. We're so proud to work with her. She said this thing that was so profound: look, people are not trying to save time. They're trying to spend time.

Josh： I love that.

主持人： The entire original sin mistake we're all making in assessing these companies is that the average consumer doesn't know what to do with the blinking cursor. They actually need to have things pushed to them so they can explore, and not have to be so high agency.

Josh： This idea of "time well spent" is exactly the right method of what consumers want. This is why building for consumers is so different than building for enterprises — you can't just start me with something and "I've got a problem, I'll dig into it, give me a blank cursor."

What are the ways you want to best spend time? There can always be a lowest common denominator — more scrolling, more anything, just keep going infinitely, just keep pushing this button in this game and see what happens. You can pull people and lull people into that. But the best products can be smarter and actually take us from idling away time to spending it better.

You want to say to an AI, "Hey, I'm a little bored," or "I need some ideas of what to do" — and have it spark you, give you motivation, encourage you to go do things. Whether it's get out in the world, or do something new, or think about something, or be entertained — it doesn't always have to be work or learning. It can just be fun, but still things that add value, where you reflect afterwards and you're like, "I'm really glad I did that."

创业方向请求

主持人： Maybe to bring us home — give us your kind of request for startups. What are the areas you're personally most interested in? What's underexplored?

Josh： Coming out of having worked inside Apple for a long time, and at Robinhood, and coming back to venture — I'm thinking a lot about what is this next wave of all the ways we connect as people, spend time well, whether that helps us travel better, manage our finances better, manage our health better, get things done, learn how to do our how-tos, get through that list. All these ways we can use this new technology to help us get through our lives, spend it really well, and connect with other people.

Pretty open-ended — because so much right now is built for productivity and enterprise: "I can make this team more effective, here's 20 workflows, here's 30 prompts that help everybody." I'm like, what are the things that make me so happy to go, "I'm using this every day and you should too — and I can't wait till we all are, because it makes it better for everyone."

Josh 在 a16z 的角色

主持人： Take us through what you're going to work on here at a16z.

Josh： I am so excited to be back in the startup world and get this opportunity to help Andreessen Horowitz portfolio companies figure out their way through this consumer landscape — figure out how to grow, tell their stories, build their products. And then I'm really excited to help all the future Andreessen Horowitz portfolio companies — let's figure out who they are, what they're doing, how I can help them, how we can help them figure out their paths. And when it's the right time, make sure they come in and we as a team really fund them.

主持人： Talk about, for a moment, what are the specific challenges you've worked through with companies? Is it about distribution? About M&A? What are some of the ways?

Josh： The stuff I love helping with most is thinking about the product and the product loop. How do you onboard people to get to that retained experience? What do you need to do to get people all the way up the ladder? How do you let them bring other people into that network? What does a core product do for them every day? And how do you evolve a single-use product into multiple uses and scale that as a product platform?

So helping people right in the heart of what that product and product strategy is — but also happy to help talk about how you organize your product team and growth team. It's completely changed in this new era. Things are more different for a product management function and an engineering function in the past six to seven months than the past 30 years of my career. It's incredible how much has changed — and navigating that, knowing what worked in the past but translating that to modern times.

Then obviously helping companies figure out their own path, whether that is M&A. There was a period earlier this year where I had a couple different friends going through M&A processes — I was talking to them multiple times a week, helping them think through the options, how to set up right for every meeting.

And lastly is how to tell your story. The one thing I've gotten this incredible masterclass on, getting to work at Apple, has been right in the heart of: how do we tell a compelling story to regular people? How do we take this world of AI and intelligence and distill it into products that people would want to use and bring into their lives every day? I'm super proud of what the team put together at this most recent WWDC and how we've told the story of Apple Intelligence and Siri AI. I can't wait to bring more of those lessons to companies as well.

结语

主持人： Incredible. How can people get a hold of you?

Josh： They'll be able to email me at jelman@a16z.com, find me through all the social medias, find me through a friend. I'm really excited to help.

主持人： I love it. Josh, welcome to the team.

Josh： Thank you. Excited to be here.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：苹果的"无魂设计"恰恰是它的护城河
Josh 在谈到新 Siri 时说了一句容易被忽略的话——"它不需要奉承你，不需要说'你今天看起来真棒'"。这看似平淡，实则是对整个 AI 消费市场的一次精准切割。
当全行业（ChatGPT、Character.ai、Replika）都在争夺"陪伴"和"情感共鸣"这块高地时，苹果反其道而行之，把 Siri 定义为**"有灵魂但不假装是人"的工具**。这是一个极其昂贵的战略选择——它主动放弃了最容易产生留存和 DAU 的情感粘性维度，转而押注"工具性人格"。
这里藏着一个跨领域的连接：这非常像**日本的"職人精神"对比美国硅谷的"产品人格化"**。日本匠人做的茶碗不会试图跟你"对话"，但它在你手中的每一次温度反馈都在和你交流。苹果在 AI 时代复活的是这种"道具的克制"——而这恰恰是大多数 AI 创业公司学不来的，因为它们必须靠拟人化来制造留存。
## 二、被掩盖的悖论：Robin Hood 的"彩票化推荐"揭示了金融产品的游戏化本质
Josh 详细拆解了 Robin Hood 推荐机制的关键——**给的不是 10

item.title: [Invest Like The Best] Investing a $120 Billion Balance Sheet with No Outside Investors
item.dc:creator: Invest Like The Best
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：保险公司是"反预测"的赌徒

大多数人以为保险业的核心是"精算未来"——用概率模型预测什么时候会出事。但 Vlad 抛出了一句颠覆性的话："我们不预测未来，我们为未来的所有可能性做准备"。这不是文字游戏，而是哲学层级的差异。

预测者押注于某个单点，所以一旦错了就崩盘；准备者押注于整个分布，所以无论发生什么都能存活。这其实和达里奥的"全天候"、塔勒布的"反脆弱"是同一种生物。但更隐蔽的洞察是：当一家公司放弃预测，它反而获得了最强的进攻能力——因为它不需要等待"对"的时机，而是任何时机都能下场。Liberty Mutual 的 1200 亿不是在等风口，而是把自己变成了"风口的反向收割机"。

二、跨领域连接：保险浮存金 ≈ Y Combinator 的批次结构 ≈ 大学的终身教职

你乍看之下，保险、风投、学术毫不相干。但它们共享一个稀缺资源：结构性免于即时反馈的资本/时间。

  • 巴菲特的浮存金 = 不必每季度向 LP 汇报的钱
  • YC 的批次制度 = 让创业者在 3 个月内只对一群同伴负责，而不对市场负责
  • 终身教职 = 让学者不必每年发顶刊也能存活

Vlad 透露了一个更深的机制："透明度换取自主权——没有透明度，就没有自主权"。这条法则在三个领域里都成立。永续资本不是"自由"，它是用持续的透明度赎买来的耐心。这反过来揭示了一个让人不安的真相：许多对冲基金宣称的"长期主义"其实是赎买不起的耐心，他们没有付透明度的钱，所以也没有真正的自主权。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：可乐颂悖论——稀缺塑造灵魂，丰裕塑造想象力

Vlad 用 6 岁时在苏联排队买面包的故事，和纽约联合广场一千种可颂的对比，表达的不只是"市场经济好"。更深的洞察在于：稀缺不仅约束你能买什么，更约束你能想象什么。在苏联，"面包就是面包"——这不是物质问题，是认知问题。当一个人没见过可颂的一千种形态，他甚至无法形成"还可以再迭代"的念头。

把这个思维迁移到投资上，就出现了一个惊人的镜像：Vlad 投资框架里最强的部分（"先问要什么 exposure，再问怎么获取"），本质上是在给自己制造可颂式的丰裕。绝大多数 LP 只有一种"工具"（基金投资），就像苏联人只有一种"面包"。当工具集贫瘠，连"我应该想要什么"都被工具反向定义了。这是组织设计层面的"croissant economics"——你的工具集决定了你能想象的投资宇宙。

四、彩蛋洞察：AI 让"折现模型"失效，市场尚未消化

最被低估的一句话是 Vlad 谈估值的部分——"未来变得不可见时，所有倍数是否都应该下调？"。这不是宏观问题，是认识论问题。

历史上的估值下调要么因为利率、要么因为衰退——都是"环境变量"。但 AI 引入的是结构性的存活率不确定：你的现金牛 10 年后可能根本不存在。这意味着——这是真正的盲点——长久期信用利差应该结构性走陡，但今天的信用市场几乎没有定价这件事。Vlad 在很温和地暗示一个万亿级的错位机会：30 年期的 Salesforce 债券，定价像在 2015 年，但风险像在 2030 年。

📝 转录文本

Investing a $120 Billion Balance Sheet with No Outside Investors

开场：保险业的独特位置

Insurance is one of these industries that serves so many different purposes. Where we sit in the economy is quite a unique place. We're not in the business of predicting the future. We're in the business being prepared for all its eventualities.

The market's changing. The world's changing. We need to have the liquidity to always react. We need to be flexible, but at the same time have permanence in stay of some of these businesses because that's what makes you a good partner.

You will likely have trillion dollar companies in 2030 that currently don't exist. And you probably have trillion dollar companies or many hundred billion dollar companies that will not exist. We're starting to see that, right?

一、平台介绍：1200 亿美元的独特结构

Vlad, this is going to be a fascinating conversation given that you sit in one of the most interesting investment seats probably in the world. I think to give people context, the right place to start is for you just to describe the platform — how much money you manage, how it's managed, why that platform is unique and different. The seat itself is so interesting to me, and then we'll go into all the things that you've learned sitting in the seat and building the platform. But first, just ground us in: what is the thing? How big is it? How does it work?

What is the thing? The thing is the balance sheet of one of the largest insurance companies in the world — one of the most diversified insurance companies in the world, Liberty Mutual Group, which has, I would say, two primary insurance businesses that ultimately feed the investment platform.

First is the one that probably most are familiar with — the Liberty Jingle — which is our personalized business, one of the largest in the US. That, of course, is the home and auto component of our business.

And then there is the business that is much more global in nature and really serves a sophisticated set of companies, brokers, partners providing commercial and specialty insurance across many domains of business.

Those two insurance businesses ultimately feed an investment platform that takes the reserves as well as the surplus capital of Liberty Mutual Group, and invests for the benefit of our balance sheet, ultimately for the benefit of our policyholders, so that we can ensure that our promises are always met and have the financial strength behind them.

So we are about $120 billion in capital. And what's interesting about our platform is that it allows us an incredibly unique way of behaving as investors — which is to say we are focused not on any form of third-party capital, which has lots of benefits but also lots of challenges when you manage money. And it allows us to think about investing from a long-term perspective, and it allows us to do the right thing, not the expedient thing, and it allows us to maintain what I would describe as investment hygiene. That is one of the most difficult things to do when you're managing other people's money.

The other part that's really quite unique is the ecosystem that we're in, which allows us to grow our capital base in the service of our policyholders. We are not driven by shareholders whose priority is return on capital in the form of dividends and buybacks. That's not part of our structure, and it allows us to again think about making decisions that are the right decisions, not expedient decisions.

二、保险浮存金的力量：与巴菲特模式的对话

So maybe explain one level more why the insurance idea is so powerful. Very famously, Buffett built a big chunk of Berkshire's success on this idea that if you have a sort of insurance part of your business, you control this float — he would call it float — and that this magic access to capital creates this permanence that allows you to do things others can't do. Maybe build that bridge between the Buffett way of thinking and how it feels to actually operate this thing.

What really is interesting to me is how insurance is one of these industries that serves so many different purposes. The way I like to think about it: one side of our business — and one side of our balance sheet — is all about protecting and syndicating that risk, allowing risk to be taken by the people in the world.

And so we have a very appropriate way we think about that at Liberty. We say we want people and businesses to embrace today and confidently pursue tomorrow. That's what the insurance part of our business does.

When we sell those promises, when we take in those premiums, we then move them to the other side of the balance sheet and we do something else that's really interesting for the economy and society. We invest that float, as Buffett would say — but ultimately invest our policyholders' premiums in order to grow the economy, support the economy, invest in critical infrastructure, fund entrepreneurs, create jobs.

Where we sit in the economy is quite a unique place. We do things on both sides of our business that allow us to protect and create the foundation of commerce, and at the same time grow the economy. That's a unique spot.

三、1200 亿的拆解：储备金、成长信用、成长股权

So coming back to this unique combo — you're providing the value through insurance, hedging people's risk one way to think about it — that creates this pool of capital, $120 billion, that you can then (at least some portion of it, we'll get into that) invest and support the growth of the economy, creation of jobs.

Break down the 120 — how much of that is tightly controlled and has to be a certain way because it's heavily regulated. How much of that is more open? And then for the open portion, how do you think about how to allocate it? It's a lot of money. It's one of the bigger investment platforms in the US. So it's a challenge. That's a lot of money to put out. Something that's going to move the needle for you needs to be pretty big. I'm curious how you think about that.

So 120 is a snapshot. I think that number will be bigger next time we talk a couple years from now. But let's talk about the 120.

I would say you could probably think of it as roughly 70 to 75 billion of that is reserves. So that could be described as tightly managed. Again going back to this notion — of course you want to make sure that whatever happens in the investment portfolio you will always be in position to fulfill that sacred promise of writing an insurance policy to your policyholder.

But I would say even there, I think we have quite a unique approach. We are not just buying investment grade bonds, putting them in the drawer, and waiting for that coupon to come once a quarter and then ultimately maturity. That could be a sleepy, boring way — frankly the way this type of capital pool was managed historically. We're quite innovative. We do lots of different things that allow us to be a liquidity provider into that marketplace. But that's about $75 billion.

I would say you could think about the rest as broken out between what we describe as growth credit and growth equity. Those two pools of capital are growing as a function of our surplus. And to your point, they are a way we can really be a full-service kind of capital provider into all parts of the economy.

Growth Credit

What we've done with our credit business is taken an approach — not of public-private, which currently of course gets lots of headlines — but really what is our levered corporate credit business, and we've put those parts together. So our public credit, high yield, and leveraged loans business sits with our capital solutions business, sits with our direct lending business, and with our partnership structure that's focused on credit. All of them sit together, have one platform, one reporting structure, because we believe the expertise frankly is what's important.

And you'll do direct deals, you'll do manager allocations, you'll do big partnerships — whatever?

100%. The way I like to describe that: people very often start with a product — direct lending, or high yield public, or whatever. I think we asked the question: what exposure do we want in the totality of our business? When you ask that question, the next question is: assuming you could figure it out and have the ability to build that portfolio of risks, portfolio of exposures — what's the best way for me to get those risks?
