Continuous LoRA：开源训练栈

Swyx：Yeah, let's go through more of the core IP, because I don't want to cut you short. You were just getting into some good stuff with SDPO. You have like this "5 days of trajectory," which is also a very, very strong launch — I think one of the strongest I've ever seen, you know, in this space. Yeah, keep going. Like don't let me — [laughter]

Ronak：Yeah, yeah. Um, I guess I can share one more as well. And yeah, it was super fun. Like we've just been doing so much across research and product, and so it's really awesome to be able to showcase that to the world in our 5 days of trajectory.

So I can share one more as well — I mean, our goal at Trajectory is to really empower every single company to get to the world of continual learning. It's such an exciting unlock when you can just put a product out there to a couple hundred users, and now the intelligence just grows over time.

传统训练 vs 持续训练

And so one of the other exciting things we did is open-sourcing a training stack for continual learning. And so this is in conjunction with SkyRL — Berkeley's SkyRL lab — and Anyscale as well.

And so basically the high level here is: up until now with training, it's very much linear. Like if you've ever kicked off a run with Slurm or one of these normal training clusters, it's like you kick off a job, it starts to spin up a bunch of resources, spin up the GPUs in order to do the sampler, do the training, and then you run the entire life cycle of the training job, and then spin it down. This is normally how training works.

The problem is, with continual learning, you don't really have like training explicitly starting and stopping. You're probably running concurrent jobs at the same time. And so the normal training paradigm starts to break apart. This is also very similar to the thesis like Thinking Machines — a few of these other distributed LoRA sort of companies' infrastructure has had.

Our contribution is to make this open source for every company.

并发训练池

One of the things here is — like, okay, so this is the normal life cycle of a job: setup, you do a bunch of sampling, training, and then cleanup. What Continuous LoRA does is say, "Hey, instead of a bunch of linear jobs like this, I can actually stack things together, have one dedicated pool for training and one dedicated pool for sampling," and then now I can start to put all of these pieces together and run jobs in parallel.

And so the results are really cool. We ran this on several different scale-up of experiments, and you can see that across the board, even as you scale up experiments — so like two concurrent jobs, we're able to cut the wall-clock time in half. As this goes up to four jobs, eight jobs, we're able to run concurrency really well.

Swyx：How come — how come eight goes up?

Ronak：I can also share it here. Let me walk through here. So —

Swyx：Okay.

Ronak：Okay. So the way this works is — okay, so up here is like serial training run, right? Like you're doing a bunch of sampling rollouts whatever. But if you have a fixed set of GPUs, then you have to run the job sequentially after another. With Continual LoRA, so it is actually slower per training job — like if you just care about, "I just want run one to finish as fast as possible," then you should be doing it completely serially, because all the resets are going towards that.

But with continual learning, what you care about is just, "Let me have all of these runs learning on the job." It also might be that data is coming in not quite sequentially like this, but maybe in batches, right? You get new data from production, now you need to have run four suddenly take up more resources. So it turns into a lot more of a dynamic workload.

And you can start to see here, like eight concurrent runs, for example, takes about the same time as sequentially three jobs would take. And so this also scales out — like we didn't fully push the bounds of this in this blog post. We're currently scaling this up to like 16 concurrent runs, and scaling up to — this was also on smaller models as well — like scaling this up to like 235B's.

And the really cool part is, obviously, this doesn't degrade performance whatsoever. It's still the same algorithms. We're able to see the same gains with any sort of normal model.

And we wanted to also make this very plug-and-play. So we have some kind of instructions here of just how to get started using it in SkyRL, which is really exciting.

操作系统视角的训练

Swyx：Yeah. It's really funny — as an operating systems fan — like that you see very similar like scheduler-type problems: starvation, preemptive scheduling, all these kinds of terms. Like you just take a normal OS class, you like then you start applying it to every problem domain like this.

Ronak：Yeah, yeah. Exactly. I think it's almost like training up until now has been a very research problem, and I think people have attacked it from a research-y perspective where you don't care about all of these distributed-systems innovations.

And suddenly now training is becoming more production, right? Like the end goal of Trajectory is that every company can be kicking off their own training runs, continuously learning, updating. And that also requires a training stack that is built with a systems-level or engineering kind of mindset. And so this is just kind of the beginning, but I think there's so many different insights from distributed computing that can be applied to here.

Act Two：可观测性与控制权交给客户

Swyx：Got it. By the time this comes — oh, you will have sort of finished your 5 days. Any other sort of overall tech bets or directions that you want to hint the audience towards?

Ronak：Yeah, totally. So where we're at with the product right now is we're able to take all the kind of noisy information from a company, all the valuable signal of what people are doing in a product, be able to train the model and then re-upload that, and then have that observability, the evals, all of those things.

But right now it's still kind of like these single components of the stack, and a lot of these things — like we are kind of like managing or looking at kicking off the training runs. The next step after this is — one, like, what we think of as Act Two of the company is: I want to make this legible to the customer. So just like the right observability tools, the right abstraction levels for evals.

And then second, give them control. So like I want to have the customer say — like a PM that's managing an agent product — should be able to say, "Hey, okay, here's where my agent is really good, here are the areas where it's still failing, and now all I have to do is just modify the model, modify all of the pieces, and suddenly tomorrow I wake up and I have a smarter model that I can see in production." All of those things should be in control of the customer. So that's the next phase.

Act Three：服务 Fortune 500

And then after that, the most exciting part is — right now we're working with these AI-native companies I mentioned: Clay, Harvey, Decagon, Rogo. Soon we'll be working with the kind of tech incumbents — you can imagine like the Airtables, Notion, those sort of companies. The stage after that is Fortune 500.

And in order to get there, we need to get to true continual learning, that I can just have just the observability layer, right? Like I'm observing, let's say in Walmart, what are users doing, what are all of the manual processes? And then start to say, "Hey, okay, maybe a model isn't even possible today if you were to just use an off-the-shelf sort of model, but I can now observe exactly what users are doing. I can start to see the patterns, build out an agent, a harness, a model that all works perfectly for that workflow."

And now you can imagine any sort of company — their front-using product should just dynamically be learning from their users and constantly updating.

不只是模型：harness、skills、memory

In order to get here as well, tactically, we're starting with the models. But very soon I want to be also improving the harness, improving skills, doing maybe even the memory layer as well. I think all of those in conjunction are the continual learning solution.

Swyx：Yeah, I know you mentioned that at the beginning as well. And I think all of those are super important, and also the interplay between the two. Like no one really knows what it means to optimize the harness and the model together, right? And I think there's very interesting research there and what that looks like.

Ronak：So these are all very exciting areas that we're like super stoked to get into.

是否只聚焦在 Coding？

Swyx：Is it worth focusing on coding alone as a verifiable domain? Or are you unopinionated because you got legal, you got the other stuff?

Ronak：Yeah. So for us, I mean, I think that just like we saw in coding for the last 2 years, I think that's going to happen for every single company and every single domain. So our thesis was basically: okay, coding is moving super fast, let's accelerate the rest of these industries to first just get to where coding is, but then after that truly transform all of them with continual learning. So that's why we're starting with also companies in different verticals to also build our platform so that it's agnostic to whatever domain you're in.

招聘与展望

Swyx：Okay, cool. Like very ambitious. I think I finally get it more after talking to you, and hopefully people listening along can get a sense of the scope of the vision as well. Where should people point to for trying stuff out? Um, you know, what are you looking for in terms of hiring, anything like that?

Ronak：Yeah, yeah, for sure. No, so we're going to be growing really fast and it's exciting to see that. I would say honestly the main place where all of the research world lives on is Twitter, and so following along on there.

And I want people to also engage and be excited about what we're building. I felt like when we were at Windsurf again — like doing, just seeing how developers were using coding agents, and being able to be really in tune with that — it was super important. And for us that's the same way: like I want to just be in tune with how customers are using our product, and how continual learning research is kind of scaling up, and us being able to at least contribute some innovations to that field.

And we're going to be — I think the two areas for us are obviously research and growing that team, and product as well. We want to be really robustifying our platform, scaling it up to not just five customers but 15, 20, 50 very soon. So yeah, that's kind of the next frontiers.

收尾

Swyx：You got five very good customers in there that are extremely good logos for a lot of people. Yeah, I'm excited to hear more and see more, you know, and people can check you out for sure. I think I've also invited you to my conference, AIE, where we are doing a continual learning track. I was very cautious about doing it. I was like, "I don't know if the field is ready." But with you guys and some of the other folks also working on continual learning, I think it's time. Even though we don't exactly know what the end products will look like, I think there's enough test cases where, like, yeah, people are trying to put this in production already.

Ronak：Yeah. I think it now is the time of continual learning, and so it's super exciting. I'm very excited to be at the fair.

Swyx：Yeah, yeah. All right. Congrats. Amazing launch — seriously, like so well executed. I'm actually like very, very impressed, as like someone on the marketing side, right? Like —

Ronak：I'm like, man, that means a lot coming from you. Yeah. [laughter]

Swyx：I'm like, okay, like this is the bar now for like how to launch a products lab, you know? So yeah, congrats, and excited to see more.

Ronak：Thank you so much. Yeah, thanks for taking the time to chat. This is super fun.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：他们卖的不是模型，而是"产品的新陈代谢"
大多数人把 Trajectory 理解为"帮企业做 RL 微调的平台"，但仔细听 Ronak 的叙述会发现一个隐藏的范式转移——**他真正在做的，是把"软件"这个静态名词，变成"代谢"这个动词**。
注意他那句话："你昨天用的模型，明天会犯同样的错误。"这其实是在指控整个软件工业一百年来的根本缺陷：**所有产品都是"标本"，不是"生命体"**。Word 文档不会因为你用得多就变得更懂你，Excel 不会因为千万会计师纠正过它就学会财务直觉。所有 SaaS 都是"冻干食品"。
Trajectory 真正的产品是一种**新的物质形态**——会自我修复、自我增殖的软件代谢系统。这就解释了为什么他放弃数百万美元收购金：他不是在创业，他是在押注一个尚未被命名的新工业革命的基本单元。
## 二、跨领域连接：从操作系统到生物学，他们正在重新发明"细胞"
Ronak 自己也提到了一个很有趣的关联——他们的 Continuous LoRA 看起来像"操作系统调度问题"（饥饿、抢占、并发池）。但我想推得更远一点：
**真正的类

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] Why People Loved a Murdering Tyrant - Ada Palmer
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、"暴君受欢迎"的悖论：正义的真空比暴政更可怕

人们以为博尔吉亚被爱戴是斯德哥尔摩综合症的早期版本，但真相更冷峻——当地人不是爱上了刽子手，而是第一次尝到了"无关系的判决"的滋味。这里隐藏着一个反直觉的政治学定律：人民对"程序中立"的渴望，往往强于对"统治者出身"的在意。一个外来者之所以能给出公平裁决，正是因为他对本地的恩怨网络一无所知——他的"无知"反而成了"中立"的保证。

这与现代企业里"空降CEO"现象惊人地同构：内部派系斗争激烈的公司，员工常常更欢迎一个对所有人都同样冷漠的外来者，因为只有他才不会偏袒任何一条旧的派系链。陌生即正义——这是一个让人不安的洞见。

二、马基雅维利的"自由"定义：把自由从"权利清单"还原为"程序存在"

我们今天理解的自由通常是"我能做什么"的列表（言论、投票、迁徙……）。但马基雅维利给出的定义更原始也更锋利：只要存在一个人，能在街上指着你说"杀了他"并且立刻被执行，你就是奴隶——无论你拥有多少其他权利。

这个标准之残酷在于：它和"判决是否公正"完全脱钩。一个不公正但有程序的体制，比一个公正但无程序的体制更"自由"。佛罗伦萨人冒死捍卫的"libertas"，并不是他们自己的参政权——他们根本无权参政——他们捍卫的是"老板的老板"参加共和议会的权利。

这里有一个跨领域的回响：现代密码学里的"零知识证明"和分布式系统里的"拜占庭容错"，本质上都在做同一件事——让信任来自协议而非来自人。马基雅维利早在500年前就指出，自由的本质不是"碰上一个好人"，而是"不需要碰运气"。一个再仁慈的暴君，其继任者也是赌博；而一套再糟糕的程序，至少是可以预期的。

三、最意外的观察："内部征服者"为何比"外部征服者"温和——文化资产作为人质机制

为什么美第奇家族夺权后佛罗伦萨仍是佛罗伦萨，而外来征服者会毁掉一座城？通常解释是"乡情"，但真正的机制更冷酷：美第奇之所以保护佛罗伦萨，是因为这座城是他们的资产负债表。他们的权力价值锚定在这座城的文化、建筑、人脉、商业网络上——摧毁佛罗伦萨等于摧毁自己的市值。

这个逻辑在今天有一个出人意料的孪生兄弟：风险投资人为什么不会摧毁自己投资的初创公司，而恶意收购者却经常拆解掉一家百年企业？ 区别不在于善恶，而在于"出口策略"是否与标的物的存活绑定。内部征服者必须"持有"，外部征服者可以"清算"。

更深一层的悖论是：正因为美第奇不必让人害怕（他们已经"是"佛罗伦萨人），他们反而不需要施暴；正因为外来征服者必须让人害怕（他们随时可能被赶走），他们必须先摧毁一些东西来证明自己。温和不是道德的产物，而是退出成本的产物。

这也解释了一个反直觉现象：最长期的统治者往往最温和，不是因为时间软化了他们，而是因为时间证明了——他们已经把自己变成了被统治物的一部分。

📝 转录文本

一、博尔吉亚的悖论：为什么人民爱戴一个屠杀者

You know, one thing that everyone is surprised by is that when Valentino Borgia — this is that when Cesare Borgia ("Valentino" is much more what he's called in the period) — uh, when Cesare Borgia conquers these cities in central Italy and he goes in and he massacres the ruling family and he works hard to kill every member of them that he can, so that there isn't a potential rival claimant to come displace him. And he implements neutral justice.

Because he and his cronies have no side in that city. They aren't connected with one group of families against another. And when [snorts] they implement justice, they do so neutrally because they aren't interested in the local backstory of factions.

And as a result, to everyone's surprise, he moves into a city, he massacres the rulers, he implements an authoritarian regime, and he's incredibly popular and beloved by the people. And everyone says, "Why are they liking this man? He is a cruel, murdering tyrant." And the answer is: "For the first time in generations, they have had something close to fair justice."

Mhm.

二、派系正义 vs. 中立正义：木匠之子的案例

Meaning, it used to be that there was one faction in power and there was another faction out of power. Which meant, in our scenario where a carpenter's son gets drunk and kills someone in a broken brawl:

  • If that carpenter's son is the carpenter of the power that's in power, then there will be no justice and there will be no consequences for this murder — maybe the smallest of fines.
  • And if that carpenter works for the families that are out of power, then throw the book at him, he'll be executed for that death.

And there will be no fair justice. The outcome of the sentence will be entirely who's in power and out of power, and not the fairness of the case.

But when both of those ruling families have been wiped out, and an outside power is here, and a homicide takes place — the neutral judge hears this neutrally and gives the same answer regardless of whose family's carpenter that is.

And the people, who have lived in generations of "there is justice for some and injustice for others," suddenly having equitable justice are delighted by this and find that wrongs are finally being punished. The people that they've resented and hated for so long, who were in power, are finally being punished for the crimes that they commit.

And this makes Valentino's conquering and violent regime incredibly popular with the everyday people of these cities, who are therefore willing to sign up for his armies and help defend his conquests and keep them in power and man his fortresses.

So Machiavelli and others are startled by this. They had expected that if a conqueror moves in, massacres the rulers of a city, everyone in the city will hate and fear that conqueror. But if the conqueror is feared and not hated — because he wiped them out, but then was fair toward the people — then it works.

三、为什么佛罗伦萨陷落如此可怕？马基雅维利的"自由"定义

So, why would it have been so bad if Valentino took over Florence and he had survived? He would have massacred maybe the ruling regime at the time, the republic, but — I don't know what Machiavelli is especially concerned about — but like the cultural treasures of Florence and everything.

So, again, in the Discourses, I think the cultural treasures of Florence would potentially have been okay. Um, there's two answers to that.

第一个答案：自由 = 程序的存在

One of them is: Machiavelli is very adamant that if you live such that there is somebody who can have you summarily executed — he can walk by you in the street and point at you and say, "Him. Kill him." — and it happens, then you are not free.

Um, in his vocabulary in the text, if you live in a state where there is an arbitrary power who can have you put to death, you are a slave. And if instead you live in a system where there must be a trial, and there must be a process, and this must be examined and public — right? — if there is a system, then you have liberty.

That system may be unfair. It may be biased. It may be, in Machiavelli's case, the very system that tortured and exiled him. But there was a system, and he considers that difference to be enormously important.

So if Valentino conquers Florence, it's not going to be that system anymore. There will be a man who can walk down the street and point at a Florentine citizen and say, "Kill him." And they will kill him.

And will that tyrant be fair? Maybe. Will that tyrant exercise his power well? Perhaps. Will his successor be worse or better than him? We don't know. We can't predict. It's a monarchy. It's vulnerable to good successors and bad successors. But the people of Florence are not free if there exists a man who can say, "Execute him."

四、Libertas 旗帜下的悖论：为底层无权者捍卫上层的特权

That meant a lot to Machiavelli. And it meant a lot to the Florentine people. And it's kind of hard for us to see how few liberties and how little franchise they had, and yet how much they cared.

Right? Florentines are constantly willing to go into the street and risk their lives flying the banner that says libertas across it — liberty, right? And the banner libertas is the coat of arms of the Signoria, the Senate, which is selected from the 1% super-mega elite —

[laughter]

— tiny minority of the city that is eligible to be in government. They aren't rioting to defend their right to participate in the republic. They're rioting to defend their bosses' bosses' right to be in the republic.

And yet they care so deeply about it, and they consider it fundamentally different from the situation in which there is a man who can walk down the street and point at you and say, "Him. Kill him." Right? And that tradition of liberty means a lot, and would be gone even if the most beneficent tyrant in the world took the city.

So that's half of the answer.

五、内部征服者 vs. 外部征服者：美第奇为何"温和"

Can I ask about that?

Sure, real quick.

So, when Lorenzo de' Piero de' Medici takes over, is he not that guy?

So, that's the second half of the answer. There is a huge difference between when the conqueror is from your city, loves your city, and wants to take care of your city — and when the conqueror is from the outside.

Because when the Medici take over Florence, they want Florence, and they want Florence to be Florence. And they want all of its beauty and all of its treasures to still exist and be theirs. They would never consider razing important parts of it to the ground. They would never consider threatening the Florentines with, "We will destroy your city walls, or we will destroy your cathedral if you rebel."

Any outsider would.

Yeah.

So Florence looks more like Florence under a Medici duke than Milan looks like Republican Milan under a Visconti or Sforza duke — than Ferrara, which has no remnants of its republic, does under the Dukes d'Este, who can do anything they want, including murderously gouging each other's eyes out, and the city will never take one step against them.

So Machiavelli is aware that Florence, if it has to fall, falling to the Medici is gentlest — uh, most volatile, perhaps, because they aren't going to be feared as much as an outside conqueror would be feared — but certainly gentlest, and that you preserve some important rights when you're conquered from inside that you don't when you're conquered from [outside].

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

## 一、"暴君受欢迎"的悖论：正义的真空比暴政更可怕
人们以为博尔吉亚被爱戴是斯德哥尔摩综合症的早期版本，但真相更冷峻——**当地人不是爱上了刽子手，而是第一次尝到了"无关系的判决"的滋味**。这里隐藏着一个反直觉的政治学定律：**人民对"程序中立"的渴望，往往强于对"统治者出身"的在意**。一个外来者之所以能给出公平裁决，正是因为他对本地的恩怨网络一无所知——他的"无知"反而成了"中立"的保证。

我们今天理解的自由通常是"我能做什么"的列表（言论、投票、迁徙……）。但马基雅维利给出的定义更原始也更锋利：**只要存在一个人，能在街上指着你说"杀了他"并且立刻被执行，你就是奴隶——无论你拥有多少其他权利**。

item.title: [Business of Sport] Is SailGP One of Sport’s Most Under-Appreciated Assets?
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、SailGP 真正卖的不是"帆船比赛"，而是"可被报销的高净值社交场"

表面看，这是一档想成为"水上 F1"的新兴体育联盟；但如果你把它的商业模型抽出来看，会发现它本质是一个伪装成体育资产的 B2B 关系经济平台。Mark Lasry 们愿意花 3500 万、4500 万、6000 万美元买一支队伍，并不是赌转播权未来翻倍，而是因为——正如节目里那位投资人坦言——"我可以在一个周末做八九位数的生意"。

这就解释了一个反直觉的现象：为什么 SailGP 的奖金池只有区区 1280 万美元（节目主持人也忍不住说"挺寒酸"），而队伍估值却以每年 30–50% 的速度上涨？因为奖金是给运动员的，估值是给"通往迪拜、里约、百慕大、香港的高端关系入场券"定价的。这是一种典型的"二级价值定价"——资产的价格脱离了它的一级现金流（赛事收入），转而锚定在它能撬动的体外资本流（客户关系、生意撮合、品牌曝光）。

让人意外的地方在于：这其实跟高尔夫俱乐部会籍、达沃斯论坛门票、苏富比 VIP 通行证是同一种资产类别——它们的公开估值看起来都"不合理"，因为定价的真正变量是"在场者的净资产总和"，而不是产品本身。SailGP 是第一个把这种"会籍逻辑"硬塞进体育联盟外壳里的产品。

二、它做对了一件被女足、LIV 高尔夫、XFL 都做错的事：先承认自己小

节目里那段对女足的对照非常锋利，但还可以推得更远。绝大多数新兴体育联盟死于"伪装成熟"的诱惑——一上来就发出与 NFL/英超对标的薪资、奖金、转播标价，然后在三年内被自己定下的高水位线淹死（XFL、AAF、LIV 早期、Saudi Pro League 的某些俱乐部都属此类）。

SailGP 反其道而行： - 队伍起价 500–1000 万美元，故意留出 5–10 倍的升值空间给早期投资人； - 奖金"寒酸"，但因此不会强迫现金流去追赶虚高承诺； - 1000 万美元的成本帽 + 中央技术研发池，从第一天就把"军备竞赛"这个 F1 用了 70 年才学会的教训内化了。

这里隐藏着一个跨领域的连接：SailGP 的资本结构其实更像一支早期 SaaS 公司的 Cap Table，而不是传统体育联盟——低初始估值、有意保留稀释空间、用每一轮新队伍销售来"重新定价"整个联盟（最新一队 6000 万 → 暗示存量队伍同步升值）。这是把硅谷的 priced round 机制嫁接到了体育所有权上。Larry Ellison 不是偶然出现的金主，他带来的是 Oracle 式的资本节奏感。

三、最被低估的洞见：SailGP 在赌"部落（tribe）"将取代"规模（scale）"成为体育资产的新计价基准

节目里 Giles Morgan 那句话是整集最有分量的暗线："总人数没那么重要，重要的是你的部落有多清晰、消费力有多集中。"

把这句话放到更大的视野下看，你会发现 SailGP 在押注一个正在悄悄成型的范式转移：

旧范式（广播时代） 新范式（碎片化时代）
资产价值 ≈ 触达人口 × CPM 资产价值 ≈ 核心受众净资产 × 接触深度
转播权是主菜 转播权是"蛋糕上的樱桃"（节目原话）
怕小众 主动选择小众，但要"垂直纯净"

这与近两年看似无关的几个现象形成了同一条暗线： - Substack/Patreon 模型——1000 个真粉胜过 100 万游客； - 奢侈品行业的"逆增长"策略——爱马仕主动限制产能以维护稀缺； - 私募信贷的兴起——绕开公开市场，直接在富人圈层完成交易闭环。

SailGP 的本质，是把这套"绕开大众市场、直接在高净值毛细血管里循环"的逻辑，第一次完整地复制到了体育所有权层面。

真正出人意料的结论：如果这套模式成立，未来 5 年我们可能会看到一批"反 F1"的新联盟出现——它们不追求 Netflix 纪录片爆款，不追求十亿观众，而是刻意把受众压缩到几十万人，但每个人都是 LP、CEO 或主权基金经理。马球、电竞中的高端表演赛、Padel 的精英巡回赛，都可能复刻 SailGP 的剧本。

而那时回头看，SailGP 不会被记住为"最成功的帆船联盟"，而会被记住为第一个把"部落主义"完整证券化的体育产品。

📝 转录文本

开场：一个被忽视的体育资产

Firstly, we love looking at new sports.

— Not only new sports, is it? — It's a new sports for the breakdown. — Yes. A new sports for us. For us. — Absolutely. We love it when we touch on a sport probably for the first time is how I should have phrased that.

SailGP, however, is something that I've had a relatively good insight in for the last couple of years, not least because I've interviewed the managing director. And there's been a huge amount in the kind of investment side where, you know, you've seen opportunities come about for investing in some of the franchises and the teams, and therefore having a lot of kind of research and behind-the-scenes look at where this business has really thrived and where there's some opportunity there.

— A conversation that we had some time ago. And by some time ago, I mean right when we started this podcast, when I was sort of listing out the various things that didn't make any sense to me whatsoever about the sports industry. And I had somewhere on my list SailGP. And you said, "No, you're not understanding it. You don't get it." And you then lined out to me at the time why actually you think this is going to make sense. So, ever since then I've had a little asterisk in the back of my mind saying that when the summer months come and when SailGP really comes into European focus, that I want to pick your brains on why this actually is a sports asset which is going to be a valuable thing going forward.

价值在哪里：一级价值 vs. 二级价值

— Well, I remember exactly when it was. And I think the reason I said it wasn't necessarily because I have an undying belief that the league itself is a brilliant product and a great commercial opportunity, which it's doing well and it's doing really good things. What I do believe is that it's a very good example of where value can sit for investors. And when we're continually talking about investing in products in sport or assets in sport, we very often look at the primary value. I.e.

— Yeah.

— This —

— TV rights.

— makes X amount of money. It is therefore worth three times its revenue.

— Yeah.

— That's not where a lot of sport gets its value, rightly or wrongly. This market trades in slightly different ways. A SailGP entity, right, well, I'll give the context in a second, but a SailGP entity has a power because of the product, because of the luxury element to this.

产品本身：水上的 F1

You go and race in some of the greatest cities in the world. I wrote some of the races down this year. You look at where they go. They go to Perth, Rio, Hong Kong, Abu Dhabi, New York, Bermuda, and Portsmouth. Where, you know, who wouldn't want to go to those places in the world? You go and sit for a weekend two-day events. Generally, these are — you go and watch some of the most incredible technology in sport. The hydrofoils that the teams race are absolute kind of state-of-the-art technology-driven machines. They can go up to 100 km an hour on the water. I mean, they don't really float. They fly. That's what people say. They fly through that water.

— I've seen it. Yeah.

— And what you create is this kind of — it's Formula 1 on water, right? And that's really what Russell Coutts and Larry Ellison did when they founded this organization. Russell Coutts, legendary New Zealand sailor, three-time America's Cup winner, gold medal winner at the Olympics, and Larry Ellison, the founder of Oracle. They wanted to create this element of competition, commerciality, luxury around the sport.

帆船运动的"碎片化"过去

Because up until that point, really, sailing had been a slightly dislocated sport in as much as it gets every 4 years a lot of prominence at the Olympics. And then every now and again a lot of prominence with the America's Cup, which is a series of races which enables the holder of the America's Cup to be taken on by various challenges.

— Yeah.

— And then you've got other sort of particular regattas like in our case the Cowes, you know, the Royal Regatta at Cowes on the Isle of Wight every August. And there are various other regattas like — I've been going to a phenomenal event in Germany called the Kiel Regatta, which is one of their big social events in Germany every single year. But these are all a bit sort of one-off here and there. There hasn't been anything to bind it all together.

— Yeah.

— And it's a little bit actually — we're talking about F1. You know, you're going back to the old days of F1 where there were all sorts of Grand Prix in all sorts of different parts of the world, sometimes even happening on the same weekend as each other. With some drivers going here, some drivers going there. So, it looks to me that what SailGP is trying to do is trying to codify and put structure around the sport which up until now has been dislocated. Is that fair?

Russell Coutts 与 Larry Ellison 的起源

— Well, if you look at the reasons Russell Coutts really wanted to set up at the beginning, it was a frustration that the America's Cup kind of had an inability to reimagine the event as commercially sustainable and then — and really most importantly — an entertainment property.

— Yeah.

— And when he had the idea — so Team USA, the America's Cup had Oracle as a backer.

— Yeah.

— And therefore, when the idea — I think to create a breakaway — what's the most important thing you need in a new league? You need financing.

— Yeah.

— And Larry Ellison, just for context for those of you that don't know, his current net worth is 247 billion dollars. So, if ever you have a man capable of making your dreams come true and creating a new league, which as we know from other discussions we've had in sports, which have been very capital intensive.

— And he'd been involved in yachting for a long time, Larry Ellison. I mean, going back decades and decades.

二级价值：周末里的八九位数生意

— He had. And when you're looking at this and you're going, "Right, okay, we want to create that entertainment property. We need to create this amazing atmosphere where technology is right at the heart of it and it's quick and it's exciting and it's short enough to capture the attention but long enough to get the value of a weekend." So, two-day events in these beautiful cities.

I talked about where the secondary value is. People coming into these events, right? You go and buy a team. And you get an opportunity to go to these beautiful cities. Imagine how much business you can do taking some of these people to it. It's the age-old, "oh, you know, you can do business on the course" but in a modern environment.

And I had people saying, "Yeah, well, you know, you may think that 35, 40 million dollars, 50 million dollars for this team is madness based on the commercial performance of these teams right now. But if I tell you I can do eight, nine figures worth of deals single weekends because I have an asset which is a unique experience to take people to, then that is a pretty strong proposition for me to go and make that investment." Secondary value.

— Yeah.

基础数据：成立时间、队伍数量、估值轨迹

— Can I say something about basics? There'll be some stuff here that you know which I think that the listeners — and in some cases me — would really value knowing. First: when was SailGP set up?

— 2019, I think.

— Right. So it's actually not new.

— Sorry, 2018.

— Okay.

— Even if —

— So same applies.

— Yeah. So it's really got going post COVID. How many franchises are there? How many teams are there?

— There are 13.

— There are 13 teams, and those teams have been sold by the central league effectively —

— Yeah.

— to new investors. You're saying at 30 to 40 million is those been sort of prices?

— Yeah. So what you had originally was the teams were branded as countries.

— Right.

— Had a nationalistic element to this, creating natural competition and also natural fandom. You would have —

— Did that — has that worked?

— I think it has, because [gasps] what you were able to do at the beginning is create an early attention that gave people a purpose and a reason to watch it. Because, hey, guess what? Even if I don't really care about this new league, I care about my country. And if I like sailing and my country is a part of it and I get some of the sailors that I'd associate with because they got talent in that were well recognized —

— And were they pegged to the countries, so to speak?

— So mostly, yes, but there have been occasions —

— Enough — there was enough rooting in countries that it wasn't a total sort of farce, right?

— But really at the beginning, the first couple of years, you had teams going for 5 to 10 million dollars.

— Yeah.

— Okay. So should we say a reasonable recognition —

— Yeah.

— of the early stages of a tournament, a league, an event —

— Yeah.

— with a price that was justifiable.

— Yeah. Mark Lasry then paid 35 million dollars in 2023 — or his investment firm did — for the US team. So in 2023, you'd got it up. So let's say 2018, 19, 20, you were 5 to 10 million. 2023 at 35 million. The Italian team in 2024 went for 45 million dollars. The latest team sale was 60 million dollars this year, and the next iteration they expect to be 70. I mean, if you listen to them, really what they're saying is it's going to be 100 million dollar assets relatively quickly.

与女足的对照：循序渐进 vs. 一步登天

— And what I love about this — if you remember the conversation we had a couple of weeks ago about women's professional football, which has spawned a lot of debate. We can probably do a bonus episode purely on the various views that have been expressed online about that discussion we had about professional women's football in this country.

But my fundamental criticism of it was not that there shouldn't be elite women's football, and not even that elite women's footballers should not be paid. My point was that by effectively predetermining that they were going to start at a certain very high level and then massively undershooting on the revenue required and the proof points required to justify that very high level, everything's had to be chopped back from there.

Whereas what it looks like to me from here is they've done this exactly right. They've started off at a low level. They've allowed those early teams to get in quite cheaply, but then be proof points for how this really works and why this is then going to work further. You've then got an upswing of value where the franchise is becoming worth more. That then creates momentum and belief, and that means that you then start dragging in even more people.

成本帽：从第一天就解决了 F1 用 70 年才学会的问题

— So there's a point I really want to drill in on here. We come back to Formula 1. What — apart from Drive to Survive — what made Formula 1 investable? Cost controls. This had cost controls from day one.

— That's really interesting.

— It had cost controls for two reasons. Firstly, every boat is the same.

— Right. When you say they're the same, are they exactly the same or essentially the same?

— For — again, I don't go too deep into my understanding of every boat. The build of these boats has to be the same. They have to be the same boats. I.e. you cannot go and spend another 100 million pounds trying to create the better boat than your opponent.

— So your R&D costs —

— Your costs are going to be much lower. And what SailGP do is they put money into a technology hub which is continually —

— on behalf of the whole series.

— on behalf of the series. Yeah. So any developments that are made comes centrally from funding. That goes into like an innovation lab if you want to call it. And the innovation lab will be working behind the scenes to try and improve the boats across the board. But it means individual owners don't come into this thinking, "Hey, guess what? If I throw another 50 to 100 million at doing the boat, I'm going to win." They can't.

There is also a $10 million cost cap in place. So the top performing teams, you know, they're approaching $10 million. Actually, there's quite a few of them that are doing $10 million just in commercial revenue alone now. So you're starting to see real value. You're starting to see break-even hit in the series. You're starting to see profit occasionally being shaken off in the series.

收入与投资人：明星 + 顶级品牌

In really a short space of time — as you said, if we say that COVID was quite a difficult time for anyone operating a global event series — let's judge them post-COVID. You're talking about the development of a league, and $200 million was generated in 2025, which was a 66% year-on-year increase. So there are some really strong elements in here that I think point towards this being a very interesting proposition of new emerging IP being leveraged to create some value.

And that's shown in how they've gone about attracting investors into it. So if you look at some of the people that have gotten involved in some of these buys, it's brilliant. You got Ryan Reynolds and Hugh Jackman who invested in the Australian team in 2025. Anne Hathaway was part of the Team Italy. Sebastian Vettel bought into Germany. Kylian Mbappé bought into the French team.

And commercially, the brands — we're talking about Formula 1 again. Like Rolex, DP World, Emirates, Accor, KPMG, Fever. Top quality brands. Exactly who you would want as a premium piece. What these races are also doing is they're creating about $230 million of combined value for the regional hosts. Because this is now seen as a really great kind of — it functions as premium tourism.

有人在看吗？转播现状

— It does. Here's a trickier question.

— [clears throat]

— Does anyone actually watch this stuff?

— So, do you know what — broadcast is quite interesting, as is live. So a key part also is onshore racing. They call it onshore racing, i.e. to go to the shore. Quite a lot of sailing can sometimes be far out at sea and you can't create the atmosphere on the coastline. This has to be relatively close so that they can create the stadium atmosphere in sailing.

But broadcast also is an interesting one because — so CBS are the prime broadcaster for it and have been since inception, and it's been going well and they've just renewed for another couple of years. But they're under a time-buy arrangement. And so really SailGP is still like they're sort of buying their way onto the platform and they're not yet at the stage where you've got networks bidding for these rights. They're still in the process of proving this has value as a viewer —

— Yeah.

— density. But, you know what, Charlie? SailGP in Spain last year set a record for the most viewed event with 23 million viewers across 200 territories. They average about 18 million viewers.

— Still.

— So the numbers are solid, right? There's something to build on. I think if you're looking at the overall proposition, there's enough in there from a value perspective where there are people coming in here recognizing value — even if it's not how we generally judge value in sports — to say, "Do you know what? I want to keep being associated with this because product is good. It's fast. It's entertaining."

不做大众，做精准：捕获 alpha

— When you say it's entertaining, those TV audience numbers are half-decent, by the way. Right? So you know, therefore those people find it entertaining. So therefore de facto it is entertaining to a degree. But it strikes me that there's something which is almost cleverer than that going on, which is basically to say:

"Okay, as a sport we are never going to be a mass market sport. Therefore what we do need to do is absolutely capture our alpha within the premium market. We've got to package something here which the premium market can really buy into — both in terms of the very rich parts of the premium market buying franchises; the next bit down from that having some luxury tourism around it; the next bit down from that turning up to the odd race in their home territory but also buying into the brands who are sponsoring it. And actually, if all we do is get all of that absolutely spot on, we've got a really decent proposition. Now, if eventually we can end up getting enough TV audience to drive some TV rights money as well — great, but we're not based off that, because that's currently not even in the business plan."

— Well, as I said, you've got teams here. We look at how teams in other sports generate revenue.

— Yeah.

— We know that over-reliance on broadcast is an increasingly concerning element of any business model.

— Right.

— This is basically looking at organizations that can create enough sponsorship to break even. Just sponsorship.

"部落（Tribe）"理论：Giles Morgan 的洞见

— Yeah. I just want to say actually — interesting about Charlie — you and I had a meeting last week with Giles Morgan, the former head of sponsorship for HSBC and Howden Insurance subsequently, who did the whole British Lions sponsorship.

— Yeah.

— And Giles is now a consultant really advising the market on how to make yourself attractive to sponsors. And the thing which he is obsessed by is tribe. Right? This tribe is so clear. This tribe is so clear. And what Giles says is that the total numbers you're doing is less important than absolute clarity and spending power around who your tribe is.

And it seems to me that what this is, is an absolutely brilliant commercial proposition. And if anything happens on the TV right side, that's the cream and the cherry on the cake. But the cake is having something which really hangs together for the big brands.

— And look, Ben Ainslie actually teed it up very nicely. I saw it in the statement. So Ben Ainslie is head of Team GB SailGP, which is actually the Emirates GB racing, and the defending champions. They won last year for the first time. And he just said: "Sailing has never had a commercially successful league or series of events before. The key is continuity, racing around the globe, that builds over time."

插曲：Ben Ainslie 与 INEOS 的争端

— Speaking about Ben Ainslie, probably a story for another day, but you know —

— He's having some challenges with INEOS.

— He has a very significant current dispute against Jim Ratcliffe and INEOS.

— INEOS?

— I have.

— It's quite an interesting one.

— It is an interesting one.

— that INEOS's claims are actually on the boats.

— Yes.

— And their rights over these highly expensive — I think they were up to like 180 million was spent on some of the development of some of them. Yeah. But Ben Ainslie now has got an umbrella company really. So that includes the America's Cup team for GB. It includes the SailGP team. Another indication of INEOS's relatively rapid and dramatic retreat from a lot of their sporting assets.

— With the exception of that staking Man United, of course.

— Of course.

— Or Ratcliffe himself is taking Man United. But of course we could do another one. To be honest with you, that dispute between Sir Ben Ainslie and Sir Jim Ratcliffe — two of the most prominent British success stories of the last 20, 30 years in totally different fields — would be a really interesting bonus segment for another day.

奖金池：刻意保持"寒酸"

— No, it's not. And I just want to focus — we've been talking a lot about prize money and I missed it out. You know, just interesting to see what they've actually been putting up for some of the teams. So, the winner of each — there's 13 of these events that go on throughout the season. 13 teams, 13 events. The winner of each takes $400,000. The winner of the championship final at the end of the season is $2 million, and the total prize pool is $12.8 million.

— Yeah. So they've created a nice financial pool of —

— It's quite modest, isn't it?

— It's modest.

— It's quite modest.

— And again, I like that. That prize pool is reflective of where the series is right now. We can give you something, guys, but we're not going to pretend that we are F1.

— Yeah. Effectively, what women's professional football's done is it's tried to pretend that it is men's professional football before it's got revenues which are anything like it.

— It's taken elements of Formula 1 and it's looking at how it can apply it in a different environment, and it's recognized that there are lots of things in sailing that will also speak to a similar demographic that Formula 1 has managed to — as you said — tribe.

— Yeah.

— Tribe. So I was — I'm keen to talk about it. You brought it up as well, because again, it's very easy to be continually critical of a lot of things that come up. However, while this is absolutely not perfect — and trust me, there's plenty of questions, as you said you could develop and have around the longer term financial viability of this as it grows in scales and as prices go up — it's working right now by the looks of it to an extent where you're seeing development, you're seeing interest increase, and you're seeing fans pay attention.

The challenge is: can you really continue to bring people that don't know much about sailing, that new fan base — and that, because that was one of the aims of —

— Yeah. Even if you've got 247 billion dollars.

收尾：去哪儿看？

— So, yeah. Go and watch some of the events. It's on now. They're pretty much on —

— And how do we find it if we're in this country? What's the best way to find it?

— Do that to me.

— [laughter]

— Just saying.

— [gasps]

— Do you want to find out?

— It's on TNT Sports or Discovery Plus or HBO or whatever the hell it's called now. Who knows? But no, TNT have the rights for it in the UK platform.

— Let's all head down to Portsmouth this summer.

— Head down to Portsmouth, or if you want, you know, you can go to Rio or —

— Yeah, well, we could do, but I'm not sure if the breakdown expense card yet reaches that far, Charlie.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、SailGP 真正卖的不是"帆船比赛"，而是"可被报销的高净值社交场"
表面看，这是一档想成为"水上 F1"的新兴体育联盟；但如果你把它的商业模型抽出来看，会发现它本质是一个**伪装成体育资产的 B2B 关系经济平台**。Mark Lasry 们愿意花 3500 万、4500 万、6000 万美元买一支队伍，并不是赌转播权未来翻倍，而是因为——正如节目里那位投资人坦言——**"我可以在一个周末做八九位数的生意"**。
这就解释了一个反直觉的现象：为什么 SailGP 的奖金池只有区区 1280 万美元（节目主持人也忍不住说"挺寒酸"），而队伍估值却以每年 30–50% 的速度上涨？因为奖金是给运动员的，估值是给"通往迪拜、里约、百慕大、香港的高端关系入场券"定价的。这是一种典型的**"二级价值定价"**——资产的价格脱离了它的一级现金流（赛事收入），转而锚定在它能撬动的体外资本流（客户关系、生意撮合、品牌曝光）。
**让人意外的地方在于**：这其实跟高尔夫俱乐部会籍、达沃斯论坛门票、苏富比 VIP 通行证是同一种资产类别——它们的公开估值看起来都"不合理"，因为定价的真

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] How Florence Afforded the Renaissance
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被颠倒的因果：艺术不是战争的"剩余物"，而是战争的"替代品"

大多数人读这段历史时，会自动套用一个直觉框架——"先有经济剩余，才有文化繁荣"。但这段对话里埋着一个反直觉的颠倒：佛罗伦萨砸钱搞艺术，恰恰是因为它在军事上根本打不过法国。换句话说，米开朗基罗与波提切利不是富裕的副产品，而是一种"非对称防御武器"。

这让佛罗伦萨的艺术预算本质上更接近今天小国的"软实力国防开支"——爱沙尼亚的数字外交、卡塔尔的半岛电视台、新加坡的国立大学奖学金。它们的共同逻辑是：当硬实力曲线斜率为负，你必须把预算转移到一条对手算不清账的曲线上去。文艺复兴因此可以被重新定义为：一场被军事弱势倒逼出来的"维度跃迁"。

二、跨领域连接：富布赖特项目 = 美第奇家族的图书馆

对话里那个最锋利的类比——"美国国防开支里单位美元效益最高的是富布赖特项目"——值得展开。富布赖特派一个研究生去开罗喝咖啡，比派一个航母战斗群进波斯湾更能稳定关系。美第奇花相当于今天 3000 万美元给孙子建图书馆，从财务角度看是奢侈，从博弈论看却是降低未来战争概率的期权费。

把这条逻辑横向拉到今天，你会发现一个意外的对应：OpenAI 把 GPT 免费开放给开发者、英伟达把 CUDA 课程塞进全球高校、字节给 TikTok 创作者发补贴——本质都是同一种"美第奇式外交"。表面是文化输出/生态建设，底层是"用对方算不清的货币结账，把硬碰硬的成本转嫁出去"。文艺复兴的真正遗产，也许不是某幅画，而是这套"用美学做地缘政治套利"的操作手册。

三、被忽视的认知颠倒：在那个时代，"复古"才是"高科技"

这是整段对话里最容易滑过去、却最值得停下来的一句——"For them, cutting-edge technology is imitating the past."

我们这代人内置了一个几乎不可见的预设：未来 = 进步 = 高科技。这个预设新到只有约 250 年历史（启蒙运动之后才稳定下来）。在文艺复兴人的认知里，时间箭头是反向的——黄金时代在身后，所谓"前进"就是想办法追回罗马。布鲁内莱斯基造大穹顶，在当时人眼中不是"创新"，而是"我们终于重新学会了古人会的事"。

这里有个让人后背发凉的跨域连接：今天的 AI 圈正在悄悄回到这种"backwards is forwards"的心智结构。许多前沿研究者半开玩笑地说"我们在重新发现 1980 年代的连接主义"、"Transformer 其实是 Schmidhuber 90 年代想法的复活"、"RLHF 是行为主义心理学的回归"。当一个领域开始把"回到某个被遗忘的源头"当作突破方向时，它在文明结构上就进入了"佛罗伦萨时刻"——不是因为没有未来，而是因为未来太不确定，于是过去成了最可靠的指南针。

一个让人意外的推论：判断一个文明是处于上升期还是焦虑期，看它的"前沿"指向未来还是指向过去。佛罗伦萨指向罗马，所以它在补课；硅谷如果开始系统性地指向 80 年代的 AI 寒冬前夜，那它可能也正在经历某种"高科技的怀旧"——而这未必是坏事，因为正是这种"向后看的向前走"，催生了人类最密集的一次创造力爆发。

📝 转录文本

引子：繁荣与战乱的悖论

Obviously, we remember this period of producing all these great cultural artifacts, all these amazing buildings, all this art.

Yeah.

And then we're talking about the precariousness of the prince, the constant wars, how they're literally fighting all the time. How is there this surplus that is available for all these different projects?

You write in your book about how the older Lorenzo de Medici spends what would be today, because of the expense of, you know, building libraries and buying books, what would be today $30 million to build a library to educate his grandsons. So, how is there all this surplus available for education and arts and so forth in a period where everybody's fighting everybody, and if you lose a war, your city will get, if not raised, at least the ruling faction will get killed?

第一重答案：金融与羊毛带来的巨额财富

So, half of that answer is finance is incredibly profitable.

Mhm.

And if you're the banking center, the amount of money that is flowing in is staggering. Uh, big wool, the big industry for Florence, is also incredibly, incredibly wealthy. So, in the same way that Harold Ford becomes incredibly rich in a period when a suit of clothing is something you save up for like buying a car, and everybody needs one. Uh, you can get very rich that way. So, A, there's lots of money.

第二重答案：艺术即外交——比战争更便宜的防御

But, B, do you remember how it's often said that the biggest impact per dollar for US defense spending is the Fulbright program?

Mhm.

Because diplomacy is cheaper than war. And sending a, you know, bright-eyed, bushy-tailed young graduate student out to a country to enthuse about its culture and make connections and make everyone feel positively does a lot more to avoid conflict — and also get help in conflict — than the same amount of spending on the actual army does. Right? And that dollar for dollar, diplomacy is cheaper than war.

They're using the art to do diplomacy. And so, in one sense, if you're not doing the art, you would have to spend more on the war. It's not that the art is being made from a surplus of the war. It's, "Oh, no, we can't afford enough armies to actually defend us against France, even if we spent every penny we have on armies. It would not defend us against France. But, we sure can spend it on painting fleur-de-lis all over our seat of government and creating beautiful, expensive gifts for the king of France."

So, the one the king of France comes, he will feel like we are friends and we are giving him all of this cultural output. And if we fought him, we would lose. But, if we play the culture victory game, that's cheaper.

Yeah.

And we can try to win.

法国使节眼中的佛罗伦萨：那是"高科技"

We talked about this last time, the experience of what it must have been like for a French diplomat to arrive at Florence and look at these people he considered to be, I don't know, no no nothing and nothings — were not even descended from the Caesars and so forth — and they're producing all this stuff.

When one goes to visit Florence now, the interest is in part because these are historical artifacts, right? Because somebody made them 500 years ago. But, if you're seeing them at the time, this would be something either you thought only the Romans could have done that we can't do anymore, or something that even the Romans couldn't have done.

Right. I mean, they're high-tech then. They're like when we look at an incredibly impressive skyscraper that's taller and more precarious and amazing than a past skyscraper.

I think that is a sort of underrated aspect of what it must have been like to

Yeah.

be a foreign power evaluating Florence at the time.

终极颠倒：在那个时代，"向后看"就是"向前走"

Yeah. Yeah, we have to remind ourselves that these are high-tech achievements as well as historic achievements.

And also, that this is a period in which backwards is forwards — which is to say, this is not a period that, like us, thinks of the future as where potential is, and that humanity might get better and better over time. The of humanity is recapturing Rome.

Right.

Uh, backwards is forwards. If we can get more and more like that, that'll be better. That's what we aspire to. And they do debate, can we surpass the Romans? Can we make things even better than the Romans? But it's an if, it's a debate. It's not a "definitely of course."

For us, it's a "definitely of course." We're moving forward. We're trying to build bigger and more impressive things. Even for people who are cynical about progress, people will say, yeah, we will be more powerful in the future. We'll be able to do more. We may use it to stab ourself in the foot, but we will be more powerful in future.

And for them, it's very much, will we ever be as powerful as the Romans were? We don't know. We can debate it. We hope so. We aspire to it. Will there be another Pax Romana? Will there be another universal peace someday? Will we ever achieve that again?

So, when we look at something that is like Florence's cathedral or Florence's neoclassical buildings, you know, we look at it and we know they're imitating the past. And so, we don't think of it as cutting-edge technology. But for them, cutting-edge technology is imitating the past.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、被颠倒的因果：艺术不是战争的"剩余物"，而是战争的"替代品"
大多数人读这段历史时，会自动套用一个直觉框架——"先有经济剩余，才有文化繁荣"。但这段对话里埋着一个反直觉的颠倒：**佛罗伦萨砸钱搞艺术，恰恰是因为它在军事上根本打不过法国**。换句话说，米开朗基罗与波提切利不是富裕的副产品，而是一种"非对称防御武器"。
这让佛罗伦萨的艺术预算本质上更接近今天小国的"软实力国防开支"——爱沙尼亚的数字外交、卡塔尔的半岛电视台、新加坡的国立大学奖学金。它们的共同逻辑是：**当硬实力曲线斜率为负，你必须把预算转移到一条对手算不清账的曲线上去**。文艺复兴因此可以被重新定义为：一场被军事弱势倒逼出来的"维度跃迁"。
## 二、跨领域连接：富布赖特项目 = 美第奇家族的图书馆
对话里那个最锋利的类比——"美国国防开支里单位美元效益最高的是富布赖特项目"——值得展开。富布赖特派一个研究生去开罗喝咖啡，比派一个航母战斗群进波斯湾更能稳定关系。美第奇花相当于今天 3000 万美元给孙子建图书馆，从财务角度看是奢侈，从博弈论看却是**降低未来战争概率的期权费**。
把这条逻辑横向拉到今天

item.title: [Mercatus Center] Sajjid Chinoy on Whether India Faces another 1991 Moment
item.dc:creator: Mercatus Center
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：印度不是"供给受限"，而是"需求受限"——这颠覆了三十年的政策本能

Chinoy 抛出了一个几乎被所有印度宏观叙事忽视的判断：印度经济的核心病灶已经从"供给约束"翻转为"需求约束"，但整个政策机器（包括话语、媒体、产业政策）仍在按"供给短缺"的旧剧本运转。

制造业产能利用率自 2012 年起卡在 74-75% 一线，十多年没有突破临界值。这意味着 PLI、基建狂飙、减税降准——所有"释放供给"的工具——都在解决一个已经不存在的问题。这是一种典型的"将军总在打上一场战争"的政策路径依赖。

更惊人的隐藏推论是：如果约束确实是需求侧，那么印度过去十年最被推崇的"宏观稳定"成就（低赤字、低通胀、克制刺激），在某种意义上亲手压制了自己的私人投资周期。这不是说宏观稳定错了，而是它的成功制造了下一阶段的新困境——一个"成功者的诅咒"。

二、跨领域连接：卢比、安全带与"波斯尼亚式"汇率政策的隐喻

Chinoy 把卢比比作"安全带"是这场访谈中最优雅的洞见，但它的真正威力在于跨领域映射——这是控制论里"缓冲变量"的思想：在一个由多变量耦合的系统里，必须有一个变量被允许大幅波动，才能保护其他更重要的变量保持稳定。

印度精英对"强势卢比"的执念，本质上和中国央妈早期对人民币的执念、阿根廷历次的固定汇率崩盘、1997 年泰铢的最后挣扎是同一种心理结构——把汇率视为"民族尊严的代理变量"而非"减震器"。这背后藏着一个少有人指出的政治经济学事实：强势汇率是一种隐形的、累退的再分配机制——它补贴了进口消费者（多为城市中产）、海外留学家庭和外汇债务企业，代价由出口商和未就业的年轻人承担。

把它和荷兰病的镜像（油价下跌作为"反向资源诅咒"）拼在一起，你会看到印度过去十年其实经历了一场"无声的去工业化压力"——只是它伪装成了"宏观稳定的胜利"。

三、最被忽视的系统性悖论：印度正在用"现金转移支付的胜利"换取"国家能力的退化"

这是访谈里最具爆炸性但被一带而过的观察：JAM（Jandhan-Aadhaar-Mobile）三位一体的成功，正在反噬印度的财政纪律。

逻辑链条是这样的：技术让"按一个按钮，24 小时内现金到账"成为可能 → 转移支付的交易成本归零 → 邦与邦之间陷入"竞争性民粹"（State A 给 X，State B 必须给 X+Δ）→ 邦级赤字从 2.5% 飙到 3.3% → 挤出了真正具有长期乘数的资本支出与人力资本投资。

这是一个"科技效率提升导致制度退化"的经典范例——和社交媒体降低信息成本反而恶化公共讨论质量、算法推荐降低搜索成本反而窄化认知是同构的。技术消除摩擦的同时，也消除了"决策成本"这个原本起着过滤作用的制度防火墙。

跨领域连接：这与Mancur Olson 的"分利集团"理论 + Acemoglu 的"窄走廊"假说 形成了惊人的互文——印度正在以"包容性政治"的名义，建造一座自我消耗型的福利—选举永动机。最危险的不是任何单项支出，而是"时间不一致性"：转移支付的政治收益在今天兑现，被掏空的健康、教育、基建恶果要 10-15 年后才显形——而那时执政的将是另一拨人。

最反常的预测：印度真正的"1991 时刻"可能不会以国际收支危机的形式到来，而是以邦级财政的静默崩塌——某个大邦因债务利息+养老金+转移支付吞噬 80% 以上支出而失去执行能力。届时"改革"的内容将不再是 1991 式的"对外开放"，而是中央与各邦之间的债务责任重新谈判——一种印度版的"财政联邦主义重构"，类似 1980 年代美国的 New Federalism，但触发条件痛苦得多。

📝 转录文本

Sajjid Chinoy 谈印度是否面临又一次"1991 时刻"

开场介绍

Shruti Rajagopalan：Welcome to Ideas of India where we examine the academic ideas that can propel India forward. My name is Shruti Rajagopalan and I'm a senior research fellow at the Mercatus Center at George Mason University.

Today my guest is Sajjid Chinoy, who is the managing director and chief India economist at JP Morgan. He's also currently serving as a part-time member of the Economic Advisory Council to the Prime Minister of India. Sajjid previously served on the advisory council to India's 15th Finance Commission and has also served on a number of expert committees of the Reserve Bank of India.

We talked about India's current balance of payments and capital account challenges, the constraints holding back private investment, why employment and exports have become first-order policy priorities, the rupee depreciation, shrinking FDI, and much more.

For a full transcript of this conversation, including helpful links of all the references mentioned, click the link in the show notes or visit mercatus.org/podcast.

Hi Sajjid, welcome to the show. It's such a pleasure to have you here.

Sajjid Chinoy: It's great to be here. Thank you.

一、当下的全球冲击与印度经济现状

Shruti：So we're recording in the last week of May where the world is a little bit crazy. The world economy is turbulent. The Indian economy has got a lot of challenges that it hasn't faced in many years, maybe even in decades.

The current crisis is that the rupee is well above 95 to the dollar — or well below 95 to the dollar depending on how we say this. Fuel prices are rising and will only rise further because we're almost at the end of our inventory and stockpile. The import bill is rising again, same because of the West Asian crisis.

And in a more macro sense, FDI has nearly stalled in India over a longer term, maybe like a decade-long trend. Private capital investment has been not picking up. Same with domestic demand. And things look like they're only going to get worse because of the global uncertainty and also the increase in input costs.

So I don't think it's a 1991-style balance of payments crisis, but that phrase is getting invoked in every other newspaper business column right now. Before we catastrophize over where we are and where things are going, I just wanted to get a sense from you on how you view the Indian economy right now in the last week of May. You're my go-to expert whenever I think about how to take stock of the Indian economy.

Sajjid: Thank you, Shruti. So as you rightly point out, these are unprecedented times in some levels. People talk about this being the largest energy shock ever. At last count, about 14 million barrels were offstream — that's almost 15% of global energy supply. It's not just crude as we've discussed. It's crude, fertilizers, helium, all of that.

What's different about this episode is that in the past when there have been oil shocks, there was never a concern about availability or shortages. It was a question of price. As an importer, there was a terms-of-trade shock and we know what the familiar playbook is. What makes this more pernicious is you also worry that if the trade doesn't open soon, there'll be shortages which then invoke non-linearities.

增长：从疫情前的疲弱到新驱动力的浮现

But let's step back and ask ourselves where the economy was heading into this event. Maybe we'll break this down into growth, inflation and the external sector, if that's okay.

For growth, let's just step back and paint a bigger picture. India kind of entered COVID on a bit of a weak wicket because if you remember, growth was already slowing precipitously in 2019 below 4%. Then you had the growth shock and that kind of was the trough. Then you got new growth drivers that were emerging in the pandemic.

The first was clean balance sheets. We had last seen those back in 2011-2012. The whole decade we were grappling with the twin balance sheet problem. To policymakers' credit, they stuck at it. We first recognized those non-performing assets, then we had a way to resolve them through the bankruptcy code, then we recapitalized public sector banks. So 10 years later, as we were in the pandemic, the good news was bank balance sheets were clean again. Net NPAs were the lowest since 2011, and corporate balance sheets were also healthy — leverage was very low, profits were picking up, cash levels were high.

So the good news is that you had these new growth drivers emerging. I'll talk about four of them:

  1. Twin balance sheets had been cleaned up and credit growth was picking up.
  2. A big public investment push by the government. This was a strategic choice — to focus on infrastructure rather than cash transfers like some other economies had. To the government's credit, it defied some of the skepticism both on state capacity as well as fiscal space. We've seen altogether — central, state, PSU — public investment pick up by about 1% of GDP.
  3. A huge pick-up in service exports. The focus was so much in 2020 about China-plus-one and whether India would benefit from manufacturing. But the real revelation was it was all on the service export side with the advent of global capability centers.
  4. The less appreciated one — the real estate cycle had finally begun to pick up. For 10 years real estate was dormant. Then demand begins to pick up. Real rates were negative in the pandemic for the first couple of years. Demand picks up, inventories come down, new construction picks up.

So the good news was that after the pressure points of 2019 and 2020, some of the healing began in 2021-22 as these four relatively new growth drivers came together. The hope was that this would finally crowd in gross fixed capital formation, or private investment — something we've been waiting for for the last 12-15 years.

私人资本支出为何没有起来：约束已从供给侧转为需求侧

But that did not happen. You enter 2024, you end 2024 where you still haven't seen private investment pick up. And it was then clear that some of the pace at which public investment was moving was not sustainable. You could not have public investment grow 20%-30% a year — both because fiscal constraints began to develop (public debt had moved up post-pandemic; both the numerator and the denominator were responsible) and absorptive capacity constraints were kicking in.

By the end of 2024, there's a recognition that public investment will have to slow to more normal levels, grow at 8-10% a year, and we had not seen private investment pick up.

Maybe we'll address that right up front. Why has private capex not picked up?

In my mind, I don't think there's much of a mystery here. We are kind of conditioned to thinking of the Indian economy as being a supply-constrained economy. In the 80s, 90s and 2000s, infrastructure was a supply [constraint], banking capacity was a supply [constraint] and credit became a binding constraint for MSMEs, agricultural production was a supply [constraint].

But I would argue that in the last 15 years, we've worked to alleviate some of these supply constraints. Instead, the binding constraint has become a demand constraint.

If you think about private investment, what you will notice is capacity utilization for manufacturing has averaged about 74 or 75% since 2012. Now if you're in a world in which you've got a lot of Chinese excess capacity — which is the new phenomenon post-COVID, and China is exporting that excess capacity around the world, to emerging markets, to Asia, to India. India's imports from China are almost 4% of GDP, the trade deficit — then you can understand why Indian entrepreneurs will be risk-averse. Your existing utilization rates have not moved up to critical thresholds, you're struggling to compete with cheap Chinese imports, and then you throw in the fact that we've had so many shocks — a COVID shock in 2020, a Russia-Ukraine shock in 2022, a tariff shock in 2025 under President Trump, and now you've got an oil shock.

So my short point is that what we found at the end of 2024 — for which I was not particularly surprised — is that gross fixed capital formation had not picked up because, while there were some drivers of demand, these were not all firing in unison. Central capex was moving but state capex was patchy; urban consumption was strong but rural consumption was uneven; service exports were strong but goods exports had lagged.

2025 年的"最后一推"与 C+I+G+(X-N) 等式

Then we enter 2025, and what the government does is one final push — they had by then recognized that demand is the constraint. There was one final cyclical push:

  • Direct tax cuts in the budget in February
  • GST cuts in September
  • The RBI cut interest rates by 150 basis points
  • Regulatory easing to act as a force multiplier
  • Just 2% inflation last fiscal year, so purchasing power picked up
  • A strong monsoon

The hope was that all these cyclical factors together — these six factors — would be the straw that broke the camel's back. As luck would have it, you began to see some tick up in consumption in November, December, January, February. Post-GST the auto sector was doing particularly well. End of February, the IPA tariffs were reversed by the Supreme Court — India goes from having the highest tariff rate in the world to having a rate closer to 10% like everybody else.

Just when you thought that maybe this is the year in which all of this comes together and there's enough demand visibility for private capex to occur, you get this large terms-of-trade shock from the [West Asian conflict].

From a growth perspective, Shruti, that's where we are. New drivers have emerged post-pandemic, but the heavy lifting has been done by public investment, and the fact is private investment is still awaiting sustained demand visibility to invest.

I'll end with one final point. I hate to invoke equations, but think of GDP as being consumption plus investment plus government spending plus exports — C + I + G + (X − N). In I we will just keep private investment. In G we lump both government spending and government investment.

G has done the heavy lifting for fiscal reasons that we'll discuss. G has to step back. You want I to fire. I being private investment, which is endogenous, needs C to pick up and/or X to pick up. You need both these drivers to be firing to crowd in private investment.

二、邦级财政、转移支付与挤出效应

Shruti: So I'll also zero in on the reasons for aggregate demand not picking up, especially on the state side. India's public debt-to-GDP is about 75% —

Sajjid: It's actually 85 now.

Shruti: 85 now. Okay, so I'm already a little bit behind. The center-plus-state deficit is between 7 to 8% now. We have almost 60% of the state government expenditures going towards payrolls, pensions and welfare transfers. And welfare transfers have really ballooned — this is the post-GO, post-Aadhaar, post-Jandhan-account-transfer kind of frictionless transfer system and neverending election cycles.

Now this has two kinds of impacts in different directions. One is these transfers should have boosted demand — that was part of the reason, other than trying to win elections and support different interest groups. That hasn't quite shown up in the numbers, at least not consistently. But the other side is it crowds out state-government-level capex, and that obviously has an impact on private capex in those states because all capex gets crowded out.

So far, given the trend, it seems like that seems to be the dominant pull factor — pulling us away from capital expenditures in a way that even welfare transfers are not able to boost aggregate demand to encourage private capex. Is that a good way of thinking about it, or are there other things going on at the state level?

Sajjid: That's a great starting point. Some of this should not be a surprise because if you think about a lot of theoretical work that's been done on fiscal multipliers, there's no doubt that in the medium-term and long-term, capex multipliers are much larger than transfer payments. It's not a good trade-off to be cutting public investment for short-term demand stimulus by giving more cash transfers. And we're finding that out.

Remember what's happened at the state level is also that deficits have widened. So this is not just a compositional issue — of course, there's a compositional issue where with more cash transfer, there's less space for public investment, health, education — but fiscal deficits have widened. States used to be around 2.5-2.6% of GDP. They're now at 3.3% of GDP. This higher borrowing by the states has meant that bond yields have moved up, spreads of states have moved up, which then affects the whole panoply of interest rates in the system. So that at the margin has an impact on private investment decisions.

就业才是消费的真正引擎

On the aggregate demand question, come back to first principles. All of this can temporarily help. Between 2013 and 2019, consumer leverage went up a lot — this was the advent of the NBFCs, the non-bank finance companies. Indian households had low leverage and they kind of levered up. Then you had interest rate cuts last year. You've had welfare payments. All of this helps at the margin. But it ultimately comes down to household income prospects, which come down to quality of employment.

Policymakers have tried a lot, but the fact is, in terms of the quality of employment, we haven't had the kind of dramatic structural transformation that we had hoped for. This is not true just of India — we've seen in other emerging markets that COVID reversed some of the structural transformation. Too many people were still in agriculture and not enough in goods and services.

For me, that's the key to long-term consumption. Everything else is a short-term fix. When we get quality of employment, labor productivity, wages — then consumption will be strong and sustained because households are going to smooth over temporary support. You have to go back to a permanent income shock to make consumption more sustainable.

三、政策优先级的迁移：从宏观稳定到就业、出口与FDI

Shruti: So one way to read your work from say 2016 to 2019-2020 was your arguments about how India's first-order task was to institutionalize macro stability — avoid premature stimulus, have policy certainty and policy credibility in terms of having a clear pathway towards reforms like GST, bankruptcy law, MPC, and so on.

When I read your work over the last 2-3 years, it seems like your to-do list has changed. Now the first-order task is employment, consumption, FDI, exports and private capex, maybe exactly in that order. What has changed in your diagnosis? Is it that the binding constraints have changed, or is it that we've just solved the old macro stability problems?

Sajjid: It's a great question. I think you nailed it — it is the binding constraints. We don't even have to go 30 years ago. We just have to go 13 years ago to 2013, because we had this period between 2010 and 2013 post-global financial crisis where growth was very strong — 9-10% — but it was unsustainably strong because it was happening with very large fiscal stimulus, real rates were negative, [feeding] into a large current account [deficit]. Some of us were worried that at some point this would unravel, and that's what happened in the taper tantrum.

The morning after the taper tantrum, some of us were concerned to say that we need to recognize as a country that macroeconomic stability is the foundation for sustained growth — there is no trade-off either in the near-term or in the medium-term for sure.

宏观稳定的制度化：通胀目标制与债务锚

To policymakers' credit, right from 2013 — right from the year of the taper tantrum — over the last 13 years what's been very encouraging is an institutionalization of macroeconomic stability. It started with the inflation targeting framework. I was fortunate to be on the committee —

Shruti: Part of a lot of these institutional changes yourself, so you should take some of the credit.

Sajjid: Oh no, no credit. But it was very nice to see that this happened very quickly. The taper tantrum happens from June to August. By September a committee is set up — and I was lucky to be on that — to think of a new monetary policy framework. By January our report is out arguing that India needs a nominal anchor: we need to go from WPI inflation to CPI inflation, we need to have a target and a band, we need to have a monetary policy committee. De facto that gets implemented a month later in February 2014, and then the government notifies it the next year. Here we are 13 years later — almost everybody's going to say this has been an unambiguous success.

When I talk about institutionalization, what I mean is — think of what happened the last couple of months. An oil price shock occurs. We're just in the early stages of that. And already commentators are saying, "Oh my god, inflation's going to go towards 6%. Will the RBI be raising rates?" Whether the RBI raises rates or doesn't is less important. What is really heartening is that the framework is immediate recall to everybody. It's been embedded in everybody that the central bank is serious about inflation targeting. There's a band, and all the governors over the last 14-15 years have reinforced that.

Similarly in fiscal policy. From 2016 — the NK Singh committee report on debt management — we now have an anchor in debt. There have been lots of shocks in between, but we now all recognize, we talk in those terms, that public debt is 85%. Post-pandemic, I don't think any level of debt is sacrosanct; what's more is debt dynamics — as long as debt is not on this inexorably increasing path, you can stabilize debt first and bring it down. So the fact that we have this framework on debt — the center has said we want to bring central debt down. There are a lot of questions about state debt that we should get into.

成功的代价：成为自己成功的受害者

These are all extremely encouraging signs and we've begun to see the fruits of this. Inflation has been low and contained. Inflation expectations are far more anchored. One would argue risk premia on government bonds has fallen over the last decade after this.

So the reason that other priorities have emerged is because we're a victim of our own success that prioritized macro stability. And we've had a series of shocks — COVID was not any small shock. So the employment imperative has just become more urgent because we fixed the macro stability problem.

There's one other thing — sorry, I'm digressing — underneath all of this has been the fact that manufacturing and now services are getting so automated. If you look at India's capital-labor ratio, you look at the Annual Survey of Industrial —

Shruti: Bananas for a country that is so labor rich.

Sajjid: Exactly. From 2005, there's this very sharp increase in capital-labor ratios — which is to say that for every unit of output, we're using more machines and fewer people. Look at our export basket: pharmaceuticals, engineering goods, capital goods. The labor-intensive stuff — textiles, leather, gems and jewelry — have reduced in share.

It's a combination of all this. We've solved the macro stability problem. The employment problem is coming to the fore because of capital intensity around the world, because COVID has done damage to all emerging markets, and now it's become a binding constraint. Only when we get strong and sustained employment will we get the consumption that will drive the private investment. So it's become a binding constraint.

四、补贴、福利转移与财政架构的重塑

Shruti: I don't know if some of these reforms would come under micro-level reforms or macro-level reforms. For instance, fertilizer subsidy, fuel subsidy and energy subsidy that we give — like free water, free electricity to farmers. These don't sound like macro stability issues, but when we add it all up — all your agricultural subsidies — it's like 2% of GDP. It's crazy how large that number is.

But the second part is also that it distorts prices in a way that ricochets through the rest of the economy because these are factor markets or very crucial inputs to pretty much everything else. So is there a reason that this reconfiguring of government spending — which I would think is still a very macro big-picture question — has just not come within the macro spending priorities? Or is it just so politically difficult that the technocrats have tinkered with whatever they can tinker?

Sajjid: That's a great question. There are so many elements to what you've asked.

One is how we think about fiscal policy more generally at the macro level. The first order was just to take large deficits, ballooning deficits, and bring them to a level where debt will stabilize — that was the first task, which we're on right now.

Then you get to the more important task: I think we have to re-examine the entire fiscal architecture.

一个新世界：弹性吸震型经济

First and foremost, let's first get our heads around that the old world is gone. In just the last 6 years, you've had a pandemic, you've had two wars, you've had an AI issue. How do we become a more shock-absorbent economy? Central to that is always retaining fiscal space to be able to buffer some of these shocks.

That is going to mean on the revenue side we have to raise more resources, because the only way to meet our expenditure priorities and bring debt down — the only way to square that circle — is to raise more revenue. That's a whole different debate. We don't want to do this in a regressive manner, so we don't want indirect taxes to do the heavy lifting; we want direct taxes. Which means we need a broader base.

Shruti: Need a much broader base, number one.

Sajjid: And we also have to think strategically on non-tax revenues. There are lots of government assets that we own — is there a way to monetize those assets smartly over time to raise revenues? I think of the revenue side as creating the resources that then give you fiscal space to handle shocks.

支出侧：重新分配以投资人力资本

The expenditure side has to do with the reallocation of expenditures, as you pointed out. In this new world, we also have to be very cognizant that human capital augmentation is so critical that we need more resources going towards health spending. They may not be sufficient conditions; they are necessary conditions. Quality also matters. But we need more money on health and education, on the green transition, on creating smarter safety nets — and that is going to mean that you have to redirect resources.

My guess is the same as yours — that some expenditures from a political economy perspective are easier to reallocate than others. But yes, at some point we have to look at these subsidies from two perspectives: 1. How much fiscal space do they open up? 2. Even if it's fiscally neutral, can we replace a product subsidy with a cash transfer? Because the former distorts product markets and the latter doesn't.

These are difficult questions, but ones we'll have to eventually confront.

福利转移与财政纪律的深层联动

Shruti: I want to push a little bit more to unpack the link between the quality of the welfare transfer or the government spending and the macro stability questions.

There are two ways to interpret what has happened. One is, of course, we're so happy that we've managed to get some kind of guidelines for deficits, for inflation targeting, now for debt, and so on. But that means there is a limit on how much government can spend. One would have hoped that the first lot of spending goes towards the highest multipliers — which is capex and then health and human capital and education. But that has not been the case.

So now what we have is — we are within the limits, but that has distorted the quality of spending. With earlier governments maybe even 15-17 years ago, they were just spending too much, but they were spending on all sides of the welfare state — they were giving the subsidies, but they were also investing in capital expenditure, also investing in social safety nets through NREGA and so on.

Now we have every interest group, as Devesh Kapur and Arvind Subramanian have said, "suckling at the Indian state." So we have every interest group doing that, but you have a lid on it, which means they never quite get to the good expenditure. Are these two things just a lot more linked than the policy wonks are giving credit to?

Sajjid: They're absolutely linked, because there is no free lunch anymore. Now fiscal spending has become a zero-sum game precisely for the reasons you mentioned. There's one way to break that cycle — and the way to break it is more revenues. If you can do more on tax-to-GDP, on non-tax-to-GDP, then you can spend more on everything without changing your fiscal deficit and therefore your debt dynamics.

These things are inextricably linked. If you do a better job on revenue mobilization, there's more for everything. But holding that constant, the expenditure side becomes a zero-sum game. And if you want to spend more on education and health, as we must, then it has to come from somewhere.

I'll make one more point. At the central level, what's happened is encouragingly public investment has picked up a lot, but non-interest revenue expenditures are down to levels that we haven't seen before. So there's not much more flab to cut on the bone. Therefore, if you need to do more spending on the green transition or health and education, hard decisions on subsidies will eventually have to confront us.

邦级的"竞争性民粹"陷阱
