On the state level, what's been worrying is — electronic cash transfers — the transaction costs are gone away. You press a button and people get cash in 24 hours and 48 hours. And then you get into a competitive populism: if State A did X, then it's incumbent on State B to do X plus delta, and the delta is often quite large.

So we need to evolve a national consensus on this, because there's a time-inconsistency problem from a political perspective. The cash transfers happen today, the election is tomorrow. But the foregone impact of that on health, on education, on public investment at the state levels — this shows up 5, 10, 15 years later. So there is a real urgency that needs to be addressed at the state level.

The last thing I'll say is that the fact that state deficits have widened so much has partially undermined the center's consolidation. So despite central consolidation, the fact is the combined deficit this year is upwards of 7%. In peacetime, you can't [be] having a deficit above 7%. What happens when there's another shock like we're experiencing right now?

MSP、化肥补贴与通胀的隐性结构

Shruti: One of the other crazy bits that we spend on is something like MSPs. Suddenly we might be walking into a big supply-side shock — rising fuel prices and therefore rising inflation. Now MSP also contributes towards that. So what is a good way to think about some of these input subsidies that are meant to keep prices high? They hurt you both on the government spending side and the quality of government spending, but they're also going to hurt you now very soon on how we think about foodgrain prices, given that there is all this other rampant inflation coming our way.

Sajjid: In fact, in a paper we did, Shruti — I think it was Prachi and me back in 2015 — on what is responsible for India's sharp disinflation that time. There was a new monetary policy framework, but we also had lower food prices. What did we find? That MSPs were actually key on two fronts — not only for contributing to inflation, but there was an embedded persistence in inflation that MSPs caused, because when inflation was high, the inputs into MSP determination meant that inflation became more persistent.

For all of these reasons — these are easy for me to say from a political economy perspective; much more challenging — but in a perfect world, you want to move everything to cash transfers. You want to be providing support, but you don't want to be distorting prices, misallocating resources, and then creating this import dependence. Fertilizers are a huge import — it makes us more vulnerable.

The good news is we've been very restrained on MSP increases for the most part —

Shruti: Except recently.

Sajjid: Except recently. But this is not unrelated to the inflation target — because when the central bank makes a public commitment to inflation, it [becomes] incentive-compatible for the fiscal authority to play along, recognizing that if MSPs were to be extraordinarily high and that pushes inflation up, then the RBI would have to offset that by tighter monetary policy.

But you're right. Holding that apart, tough decisions on subsidies await the fiscal. But I would say it's not just subsidies. We need to rethink the entire fiscal architecture. What level of debt do we want to reach? What burden-sharing will happen between the center and the states? Central debt is coming down; state debt has been rising on a monotonic path. If you're going to have an overall public debt target, who has to do the heavy lifting — center or states? That's not an easy federal question to answer. And then the question of what are our new spending priorities — where will that fiscal space come from? There's a whole architecture question on fiscal that extends beyond just subsidies.

危机作为改革窗口

Shruti: The reason I wanted to zero in specifically on fertilizer MSP — it feels like this is that crisis that maybe we should make use of, because the fertilizer bill is just shooting up. Is this the time to kill the fertilizer subsidy? And the war has given us a great opportunity and yet another — not balance of payments crisis — but balance of payments problem.

Sajjid: External events open up political space. It is the best time to do it from an economist perspective. Of course, politicians may have a different view, and we saw this in 2022. In 2022 as well, the fertilizer subsidy ballooned. And in a way, the trade-offs that we spoke about will be in full display in the coming year because if the fertilizer subsidy bill is higher, then you've got uncomfortable trade-offs: do you want the fiscal deficit to widen beyond what you had budgeted, which then has implications for debt dynamics, interest rates, reputational costs? Or do you keep the fiscal deficit within target, but then you make hard decisions about cutting public investment? Both those trade-offs will come to the fore in a year like this.

五、卢比、减震器与汇率政策的政治经济学

Shruti: With the rupee depreciating, our import bill is going up. So both on fertilizer and fuel, we're going to feel the pinch very soon.

You have been a very vocal supporter of letting the rupee depreciate and find the appropriate level for various reasons. You held this view right now in your column a few days ago. You held this view in November last year with all the Trump tariffs piling on. You held this view in 2019.

What is it about the Indian establishment that has this obsession with a strong rupee? Is that just an optics thing? Is it maybe like a very self-serving thing — that the IAS officers have to pay college fees in dollars and want the rupee to be stronger? What exactly is going on?

Sajjid: Thank you, Shruti. First and foremost, I'm very happy that in the last 15-16-18 months, we've let the rupee depreciate. It has depreciated almost 14 or 15% in real effective exchange rate terms. So I'm glad that that Rubicon has been crossed.

It's not just the establishment — there's skepticism among market commentators, private sector industrialists. And there's a belief that, "Oh, we are an importing country, so when the rupee depreciates, our imports become more expensive in rupee terms, this is going to be inflationary." And there's an overriding skepticism about exchange rate elasticities — that if the exchange rate were to depreciate, you're getting inflation but you're not going to do much on exports and imports.

The burden of proof is on us. A paper that I wrote for the IPF in 2018 — RBI has done some similar work — clearly shows that when you control for these things carefully, the real effective exchange rate clearly has an impact, holding other things constant, over time on export competitiveness.

What you see sooner — and the part that's less appreciated — is the import part. On the one hand we're complaining that Chinese imports are flooding India, and then until the beginning of this year, the real effective exchange rate vis-à-vis China — India versus China in bilateral terms — had strengthened by 10%. That's a fancy way of saying in real terms at the beginning of 2026, before the recent depreciation, what — 10% cheaper than they were 5 years ago. China already has this advantage in terms of cost, and we give them another 10% cost.

卢比作为安全带的隐喻

So it's incumbent upon some of us — there is skepticism across the board — to prove that... I keep saying the rupee is like a shock absorber in the following sense. When you're driving a car, think of the rupee as your seat belt. When you brake, suddenly something has to take the shock.

Shruti: What a seat belt you are.

Sajjid: The variables we should care about are employment, growth, inflation. The rupee is just an enabling mechanism. Rupee by itself should not be attracting so much attention. If it absorbs a shock, other domestic variables that matter to us don't have to move as much.

But I'm glad to see that in the latest episode that thinking has taken over. One quick caveat to my own thinking — one needs to be careful about speed.

Shruti: It shouldn't spiral such that then foreign inflows start rethinking their India strategy and things like that.

Sajjid: That's the balance you need to get right. In my last column I was saying we definitely want the rupee to depreciate, but if you put too much burden on the rupee being the only adjustment mechanism for the balance of payments, then you risk such a rapid depreciation that this becomes self-fulfilling. Already you've begun to see the last few weeks that importers, corporates, foreigners have begun to hedge their stocks — stock of FDI, stock of ECB, stock of FPI. And once you go down that path, then you get into a self-fulfilling loop: you hedge your stock, that puts more pressure on the rupee, the more the rupee weakens, the more the desire to hedge. We want to break that cycle. We want rupee depreciation, but not too rapidly so as to unanchor expectations in the rupee market.

荷兰病、油价与均衡汇率

Shruti: When I was reading all your columns to prepare for this conversation — all the more recent ones where you're calling for rethinking rupee depreciation in slow stages — it reminded me of one of your older papers with Toshi Jain on the Dutch disease.

The Dutch disease, for those unfamiliar, comes from the windfall that the Netherlands got in the 60s because they discovered more natural gas and energy resources. But what happened immediately was a big inflow of foreign exchange, which made the guilder — those years before they had moved to the unified currency — stronger and it made Dutch manufacturing less competitive. One booming sector effectively strangled their other competitive sectors.

In this paper, you make a pretty elegant argument where you talk about the fall in oil prices and therefore the import bill being the big windfall gain equivalent for India, and that led to the rupee appreciating in real terms, which also coincided with a decrease in its export competitiveness.

What is a good way to think about that given the situation we're in right now? Now we're seeing the exact reverse of what happened in your paper — the import bill rise, the rupee depreciate. Which factor is going to have more impact? Are we going to become more competitive in our exports because the rupee has depreciated close to 15%, or is it going to be harder because the fuel bill is going up and the overall cost of production is going to go up?

Sajjid: There's no doubt that this is all controlled for when you measure these things. As long as there's some domestic value content, your exports become more competitive. Even if the import content is 70%, yes, that 70% moves in line with exchange rate. But on the export side, the full 100% is moving in line with exchange rate. So the larger the domestic value content, the greater the competitive advantage on exports from a weaker rupee. To be fair, these things have attenuated over time, but it's still economically and statistically significant.

A weaker real effective exchange rate over time will help not just export competitiveness but make imports more expensive and therefore help domestic substitutes on the import front. We tend to ignore that part.

Shruti: On that, the elasticities that you find were not very reassuring in terms of spurring domestic supply chains, right?

Sajjid: Yes — on the import front. Different studies find different estimates. Some other studies find the opposite of what we found — that the import elasticity is larger than the export elasticity.

Shruti: Oh wow. Okay.

Sajjid: Exactly. And that moves faster because imported goods get more expensive immediately. And then to the extent that there are domestic substitutes, those tend to expand. So there are different ways in which business happens, but it's fair to say prices matter. In India, studies that look at this carefully find that when you do get exchange rate depreciation, that compresses the current account.

Now the larger point — we need to think a little bit more from first principles. The equilibrium unobserved real effective exchange rate is a function of many things. When you get a positive terms-of-trade shock through lower oil prices, your equilibrium rate itself should rise. When capital flows increase, your equilibrium rate should rise.

Part of this is to understand where the real effective exchange rate is vis-à-vis equilibrium. One of the reasons I think we should let the rupee weaken is because by all accounts — with FDI slowing in the last 3 years and India experiencing a large negative terms-of-trade shock — both of these would argue for a much weaker real effective exchange rate. So those that say, "Oh, the RBI's index was 100 three years ago and now it's 92, [which] means the rupee is undervalued by 8%" are presenting some static real effective exchange rate. This is a dynamic concept that changes when exogenous shocks happen, and I would argue this equilibrium rate is much weaker, and we are facilitating that adjustment towards the new equilibrium.

六、FDI、资本流动与"拉力"经济学

Shruti: You talked about FDI. Your most recent JP Morgan report along with your co-authors has talked a lot about capital flows. Capital flows have shrunk from 2.6% of GDP between 2015 and 2019, to about 1.4% in 2024, to virtually nothing in 2025.

Another finding that you have in the paper which is related to this is that in the last 15 years, net FDI to India is inversely correlated — and very strongly so — with US Treasury yields. FDI has been slowing down. A lot of it is outside our control. But there are things which are within our control, because Vietnam has not had a similar plummeting of FDI — they've done pretty well despite the same global situation.

What is a good way to think about the factors that India needs to focus on, in the very short run and the slightly longer run, on how to bring in FDI inflows with the rupee where it is?

Sajjid: The way to think about FDI is that FDI flows because there are both push factors and pull factors. The push factors are global. When global interest rates went to zero in the pandemic, money flowed to all emerging markets. But then you lose control of your own destiny — because when global factors change, FDI can dry up like we've seen the last three years.

What's more important is to focus on pull factors — what is it that the country can do to come across as attractive, and FDI gravitates towards that. What we show in the paper is that there's one clear episode where India had a strong pull factor — between 2005 and 2010. What happened then? You had this big private investment cycle, and so the quantum of FDI that we attracted could not be explained by where US rates were. That was India's pull FDI.

After 2010, as you pointed out, it's been more push FDI. It should be no surprise because — as we've just discussed — we haven't seen private investment pick up. Vietnam, in contrast, is all appears to be pull FDI, because Vietnam's FDI has been almost flat as a pancake at 4.5% of GDP and invariant to global financial conditions.

The lesson is very clear. What do we do to make India a more attractive destination? It all boils down to starting a fixed investment cycle. My impression is that FDI is complementary to domestic investment. When domestic private investment starts, it tends to catalyze FDI.

经常账户的"血压理论"

This matters because in the past we always talked about India's sustainable current account deficit being 2.5% of GDP, but that in a way was because we were attracting 2.5% of capital flows. If your capital flows reduces, then your sustainable current account deficit reduces. That has implications on what your investment rate will have to be, because ultimately the current account is nothing but the investment-minus-savings rate.

As an emerging market at our level of per capita income, we don't want a current account which is half a percent of GDP. We want a current account which is 2.5% of GDP, because that's telling us that the investment rate will be higher and potential growth is higher. It's like blood pressure. You don't want blood pressure to be too high, but blood pressure that's too low is equally worrisome.

Shruti: And right now our current account deficit is too low.

Sajjid: It is too low. It's been 0.5% of GDP the last year and 0.8% of GDP the last 3 years. That's another sign about investment. If you think of the current account as being investment-savings gap — that is the sum of the public investment-savings gap (which is the fiscal deficit) and private investment-surplus savings (savings minus investment) — with a combined deficit of above 7% of GDP, your current account is only half a percent of GDP. It's telling you that private corporates have too much saving and are investing much too little. All of this, whichever way you cut it, comes back to: what do you need to do to boost private investment?

Press Note 3 与中国 FDI 的悖论

Shruti: I have a couple of different questions on FDI, but I'll start with Press Note 3 — which happened for multiple reasons: pandemic, Galwan. It didn't start out as an anti-China FDI policy, but it effectively became one. It was all Indian-bordering states, and it wasn't saying no to FDI from bordering states; it was that we'll look at it and you need a permit — like the good old license-permit-raj world. So we kind of did with FDI what we've done with quality control orders, or what we did in the 1980s with the rest of the economy.

Do you think this plummeting has in large part been because of this? Because the one country that does have the ability to invest in India in fairly large proportions is China, and we kind of shot ourselves in the foot with Press Note 3, which we've now amended. You've been talking for many months about relaxing the constraint on China and actually inviting more Chinese FDI. Is that the reason that things went so bad so quickly for FDI?

Sajjid: I don't think so actually. Now prospectively opening up investment to China will help — and I'd argue for that for different reasons, for risk management reasons — because China now is looking for outbound FDI and you're seeing this all across Asia. But I don't think it's the primary reason. If that was the case, then you would see that in 2019 FDI would have gapped down. Instead, this happened in 2020 — instead the opposite happened, FDI went up in 2020.

Shruti: But that's because the US yields just dropped.

Sajjid: My sense is at the margin every bit helps. So I think prospectively — and the reason I'm saying this is because it's not just FDI, yes we want more FDI, but it is for risk management.

Chokepoint 脆弱性

One of the things we've realized in this last six months is, quite apart from energy dependence, it is chokepoint vulnerability. A lot of our LPG comes from the Strait of Hormuz. A lot of our gas comes from Qatar. We need to diversify that. We should be cognizant of the vulnerabilities we have with China — 70% of our APIs come from China, and by some estimates for antibiotics it's 90%.

Shruti: 90%. Yeah.

Sajjid: Polysilicon, which is used for solar panels — almost all of it comes from China. Lithium ion batteries, all of it comes from [China]. My argument is that allowing Chinese FDI does two things: (a) It helps you with FDI as a source of capital to finance the current account. (b) You take away the prospect of imports being weaponized. You've got that FDI in India — a domestic company in a joint venture with a Chinese company — and you're getting some domestic production capabilities. Value is being created onshore, jobs are being created onshore. You become less vulnerable to those imports suddenly being shut down.

For all of these reasons, we need to think carefully and try and get more Chinese FDI into India.

FII 与机制不确定性

Shruti: You've been talking about how the more recent pressure — which is so weird for those of us who've been looking at this over the last few decades — is capital-account-led, and net FDI has dried up because of high yields, all the other pull factors not working out. But can you think about a similar push-and-pull framework when it comes to foreign institutional and portfolio inflows? Some of the outflow is obviously global — US and geopolitical risk — but some of it may also be India-specific, like weak earnings across the board. There's a fair bit of regulatory uncertainty — SEBI changing its mind on its derivatives framework. Do we have a sense of what kind of market we wish to be for institutional investors, and are we consistent with that, or is there just too much regime uncertainty causing outflows on that margin?

Sajjid: I think regime uncertainty has been in India forever. We've always had that. And we've had a situation where we've had strong portfolio flows come in even back then — 10, 20, 25 years ago. I think what markets are looking for — in a world in which there are compelling pull factors now — look at Asia: you can see what's happening in Taiwan, in Korea, in Singapore, in Malaysia — they're all intrinsic and a part of the AI supply chain. Look at Latin America — equity flows are going there because they're benefiting from commodity exports.

What we need to look at is: what is the India-specific pull factor?

这是不是另一个"1991 时刻"？

When you talk about 1991 — my sense is this is not 1991 in terms of a balance of payments problem, because conditions are very different. But this should be a 1991 moment in jumpstarting reforms. The reforms of the 1990s have run their course. Yes, we've seen some new reforms in recent years — the bankruptcy law, GST, labor laws last year, we're getting some China-plus-one — but what we need to do is, what is the next set of measures that's going to improve India's structural competitiveness?

I want to emphasize structural competitiveness. This is the right moment in time because I think of global value chains as being a club with closed membership. For many years membership was closed. Now because of what's happened in China-plus-one, multinational companies are looking around to say "I want a hedge away from China." And the old hedge, which used to be ASEAN economies, is not deemed to be as much a hedge — because after the transshipment risk under the Trump administration, getting a hedge from China means actually leaving ASEAN as well. If you go through Vietnam, there's always the risk of you being accused of transshipping goods through Vietnam and attracting high US tariffs.

This is an opportune moment because firms are looking for other destinations, and this is when we need to think of this as being a 1991 moment to push forward the next set of reforms — on factor market reforms, on deregulation, on import tariffs, on QCOs, on an export push. India's compelling pull story — when that happens, whether it's FDI or FII, India will get it. Earnings are simply a symptom. [If you] have private investment, [then] earnings will reflect that.

Shruti: So they all go procyclically. If one thing picks up slowly, all of them will start picking up with a lag. And all of this has to be done at the state level — so it should technically be more doable on an experimental basis. All the structural reforms you talked about, most of them are state problems.

Sajjid: Not only more doable — this is where comparative federalism matters. It's good to see that states are actively competing with each other, and in fact when the next dollar of investment goes to State A, there's visible disappointment and disenchantment in State B. That's healthy competition. Reforms are more at the state level, more horizontal, but this also takes more time. I think the center will need to enable an ecosystem where this can happen rapidly.

七、出口主导型增长：唯一可行的高增长路径

Shruti: One of the areas where you have been consistent now for like 25 years — not just the last 5-10 years — is on exports being the growth story. On this, most of our colleagues who were on our side have changed their mind, some of them more recently, just because the tide has turned. Five years ago it was about Dani Rodrik and premature de-industrialization — that old globalized world has just ended. More recently it's about resilience and geopolitical shocks.

Your argument is actually very consistent: that it's not going to build resilience, and export-led growth is really the only way out and the only way to build resilience — and having industrial policy, import substitution, these things are actually eventually going to hurt your export competitiveness, which means they will hurt your growth and therefore lead to lower resilience.

What is the part of the trade-off that other people are not seeing?

Sajjid: We're preaching to the converted over here, you and me. But let's just stipulate some facts because it sometimes needs repetition.

If India needs to get to $15,000 per capita by 2047, which the prime minister laid out —

Shruti: It's a really great goal.

Sajjid: Great goal. Doable. We need to get there. What does that mean? Let's break it down. In per capita dollar growth terms, this means we have to grow at 8% — 7.9 to be exact. The last decade, we've grown at 6%, 5.9. So over the next 20 years, we've got to up our dollar per capita growth rates by two percentage points.

Only 13 economies in the world the last 100 years have grown at 7 or 8% for this length of time. They all had one thing in common: lots of global engagement, lots of exports. Why? Because the global market is just that much bigger. And for all the reasons you and I have discussed — at certain economic scale, [exports] force a disciplining mechanism. Think of our whole IT sector — just look at the productivity growth in the Infosyses and the Wipros, the IT sector. Again, because they were globally exposed, they had to be world-class.

India's own economic history — when we had very high growth in the first decade of the millennium — people don't fully appreciate this: it was export-led growth. Exports grew in real terms at 16% a year between 2003 and 2011. Because exports were the driver of growth, investment (which is endogenous) responded to that — capacities expanded and investment grew at 9.7%. Consumption growth was less than 6%. India's burst of miracle growth happened because of exports.

We should first mandate that there is no example in history of 7% growth for two decades — which is our aspiration — which is not based on global engagement. The question is, how do you get that global engagement in this economic environment?

即便潮水不涨，份额仍可上升

We've seen that even in the last 3-4 years — yes, the global environment has become more vitiated. But our service exports, for example — confidently global. When you're globally competitive, it doesn't matter how fast the pie is growing.

On the goods front, we're still less than 2% of global market share, which is at this point an advantage. Even if the pie is not growing — yes, a rising tide lifts all boats, that's easy — but even if the tide is not rising, if my share is only 2% —

Shruti: It has nowhere to go but up.

Sajjid: Nowhere to go but up — 3% or 4%. The difference is that in early export growth, when the rising tide lifts all boats, you don't have to do very much. But now the importance of improving structural competitiveness is even higher. To get that 1.7% or 2% manufacturing share to go to 4%, we have to increase market share — and increasing market share means doing better than the next guy, which means you have to become more competitive.

Lerner 对称性：进口关税即出口税

I'll take two quick points because it's a very important question. As you know, in graduate school the first class of trade — Anger, whom you and I know well — first point is an import tariff — there's a Lerner symmetry theorem — an import tariff is the same as an export tax. When we think about import tariffs, we have to be very careful to understand that's as pernicious as an export tax. And worse, a non-tariff barrier on the import side is even worse because they're opaque and intransparent.

The good news is, it's not all gloom and doom. In the last two years, I sense a perceptible shift in India. We're signing more free trade agreements. The 50% tariff last year kind of gave us a boost. We've begun to bring import tariffs down — we have much more to go on that front. We've begun to look at the stock of QCOs (quality control orders) — we need to do more on that front. But we need to be mindful that if you're pursuing an export[-led] growth, we have to rationalize the import side.

韧性 ≠ 复制整条供应链

The last thing I'll say is — in my mind, there is no trade-off between resilience and export-led growth. Resilience means identifying the weakest point of your supply chain. So you don't have polysilicon or lithium ion batteries or LPG — solve for that.

Resilience doesn't mean replicating the entire supply chain at home and import substitution. We should make a big distinction between resilience — which is to say look for the weakest link of the chain and solve for that, either build buffers at home, diversify your imports, look for substitutes — it does not mean we have to go back to autarky and make ourselves [self-sufficient].

I don't see any tension between becoming a more resilient economy — yes, for certain highly sensitive areas we're going to build buffers, we have multiple imports to be placed from. There'll be a fiscal cost, there'll be a dollar cost, there'll be an opportunity cost — that's just a cost of avoiding shocks. It does not mean that we regress into autarky.

滑坡谬误的边界

Shruti: To play devil's advocate — the trouble is we have one very, very, very large and dominant player who is dominating virtually every supply chain, and we don't know what that chokepoint is until they hold it over our head. No one thought rare earths and lithium were an important part of any supply chain until China made the crazy move. Only more recently, as solar has become a larger and larger portion of our entire energy production, have we realized how important Chinese imports are in that space. Same with pharmaceuticals during COVID.

One way of thinking about it: are they on the opposite side of this trade-off relative to you and me? Because now they are worried that every single thing produced in China — and China produces pretty much everything — is just that much cheaper, and depending on what the geopolitics are, they can hold it over our head. So we need to build some resilience in every area, if not autarky. But the process of building resilience means some protective barriers, which means we get somewhere closer and closer to very high trade barriers, if not autarky.

Sajjid: I can't speak for them, but this is a slippery slope I worry about.

Shruti: Yeah, it's the slippery slope I worry about too.

Sajjid: If you go down a path — how much of the supply chain do we want to replicate? Therefore, I began by saying that if you think about risk management at the macro level:

  1. On highly sensitive areas, fearing a global cut-off, you build buffers. China has built buffers on agricultural commodities. So in some areas you can say "very sensitive goods, build buffers."
  2. Wherever you can, diversify. We are now buying more LPG from Australia — we could have been doing that before the crisis. We're buying more LNG from the US.
  3. Hedge prices. Mexico exports oil — it does the famous Hacienda hedge in financial markets. It pays 0.1 or 0.2% of GDP and buys put options to take over price risk. India could have been doing that for the last decade.

Shruti: We don't like markets, so we don't do that.

Sajjid: And for the China stuff, you're right — China is a monopolist, there is no way to diversify that risk. Therefore, if we can get more FDI from [China] —

Shruti: FDI, especially in those areas, encourage them more.

Sajjid: That becomes a hedge. It's a combination of building buffers, getting more FDI from China in the areas where there are monopolies, hedging prices, and diversifying imports. If you do this, you can take care of most if not all eventualities. We don't need to be replicating the entire supply chain at all.

八、劳动力、自动化与"让劳动重新成为有吸引力的生产要素"

Shruti: The second part of trade liberalization, which again 15-20 years ago was a big topic of discussion even when people were pro-trade, was on labor elasticities. You've written about this. You have a paper with Devashish Mitra and Pravin Krishna on Turkey, and then I think Devashish Mitra, Rana Hasan, and Ramaswamy did one for India. The message is either mixed or not good — trade liberalization has definitely increased pressure on labor markets, especially in India at the subnational levels.

These papers were written 15 years ago. The trouble is now the capital-labor ratio is even worse. There is much more automation. The pressure on labor is even higher. If I were a betting sort, I would bet it's worse news now. That's no longer a hot-button topic the way it used to be 15 years ago. But is that another big problem we need to worry about when you and I are still extremely pro-trade and liberalization, even in the current climate when we're trying to bring manufacturing up — but not just manufacturing, jobs growth in manufacturing?

Sajjid: That's a great question. Firstly, in our paper we actually found a different result — but of course it was quite special — that we found that trade liberalization in Turkey (there was a good experiment there) did not increase labor demand elasticities despite [liberalization]. That was unique to Turkey. It probably increases it in India's case.

But the broader issue is the following. We must appreciate what trade does and what it doesn't do. Trade increases the size of the pie. That efficiency gain we've seen in the world in the last 30-40 years — efficiencies have gone up, millions of people have been pulled in emerging markets out of poverty, [trade] created jobs. The size of the pie goes up.

There are clearly distributional consequences from trade. The US has seen this — the whole [hollowing] out of the Midwest, [jobs moving] to emerging markets. When you do trade, the size of the pie goes up, but this is going to skew bargaining power of capital versus labor, make certain factors of production more vulnerable. And this is where public policy has to step in — to buffer the impact, to say we want a larger pie, and the distribution of that pie, public policy has to step in to offset or mitigate.

让劳动力与资本竞争

This leads to a broader question — I worried about how India's capital-labor ratio has steepened so much. This is before AI and what's going to happen on services. We have a lot of global capability centers — worry that if AI continues at this pace, what does it do to jobs there?

The broader question is: how do we make labor a more attractive factor of production? That's just another way of saying — capital is going to become more productive forever more, that's inexorable. How do you give labor a better chance to compete with capital?

Shruti: And that's about skilling and education and building the human capital, health —

Sajjid: Really boring, but it's never been more urgent. Education, skilling, health, mid-career training, all of that stuff. And welfare nets where labor gets left behind.

Part of this issue of trade elasticities is yes, there will be winners and losers. We know that from trade. The winners outsource the losers, but the losers tend to be concentrated; the winners are more diffused — consumers gain. How does public policy compensate the loser? That has never been more important.

Quite apart from technology — there's this debate in the US about what caused some of the job losses: was it really China joining WTO or was it technological progress over time? Today it's trade, tomorrow it's going to be technology. But the question is: what can we do to give labor a better chance to compete with capital? That's the fundamental challenge that we face at this moment in time. It's not a lazy one.

九、蒙代尔三难、资本流动与货币政策独立性

Shruti: Before I let you go — one last question on where you think we are in India on the Mundellian trilemma. Now the world is a little bit different because now this is a capital account issue, not so much a current account one — but maybe that's part of the bigger problem.

You have on one side a question of inflows. You want to have the rupee managed — or rather, the rupee is managed by the RBI, it's not determined by the market. And we want to determine monetary policy and keep inflation low, which we've been remarkably successful at doing in the last decade.

Now you've kind of switched corners within that triangle — like India has never been in any particular corner, we kind of manage all three in some way — but you've switched corners because now you're like, "We need more FDI, so we need to think about capital inflows and we need to think about how we manage the rupee." Do you worry at all about what that does for independence in monetary policy and stability in monetary policy, and how we managed it so far?

Sajjid: It's a great question, and I think actually that's one area where we've actually gained success. Let me explain why.

Firstly, there's no doubt that we need more capital flows into India. We need more FDI for technological transfer, to finance our current account, for higher investment rates. We can't abandon that — the pace of capital account liberalization we can debate, the direction should not.

Now, the [Mundellian] trilemma presumes that you only have one instrument. But in the practical world of central banks, you actually have more than one instrument. The RBI has shown that, in the last few years, we've actually used interest rates and liquidity for domestic growth-inflation dynamics — interest rates have been independent of where the Fed has [been] — and we manage the external sector by sterilized intervention.

I'm not sure that we're stuck in the trilemma. The best evidence of this is if you look at India's interest rate differential with the US. There used to be a time where we were 300-400 basis points higher than the US, and we could only cut when the Fed cut, or when the Fed hiked, India was forced to hike. We've broken out of that syndrome a long time ago.

For the last 6-7 years, our interest rate differential has narrowed very sharply. India's interest rates today are what — 150-175 basis points above the Fed. The fact that the interest rate differential has narrowed is the best sign of monetary policy independence. Because monetary policy has not become hostage to the Fed. When the Fed does move, the exchange rate comes under pressure, but the RBI has multiple instruments on that front to ensure that those exchange rate pressures do not mean automatic hiking of interest rates.

To give you an example — over the last year, from 2025 onwards, we've cut much more aggressively than the Fed has. And yes, there was some exchange rate depreciation.

Let me put it simply. We've allowed in the last year and a half more exchange rate flexibility, which has meant that you've broken out of the trilemma on capital flows and monetary policy independence. And even when there is a trade-off, there is more than one instrument. If you use sterilized intervention, central banks within some degrees of margin can do both — they can manage the exchange rate and they can keep monetary policy independent.

That said, I think we should be very conscious that exchange rate should be managed not only when there are large shocks like this, but on a more day-to-day, week-to-week, month-on-month basis — because then markets develop.

Shruti: Yeah. You just smooth it out and markets will develop.

Sajjid: Right now we're worried that, "Oh, everyone's going to start hedging." But when there's exchange rate stability — too much stability — it dissuades people from hedging. You want to be in a situation where people hedge in the normal course of events, and then there's no moral hazard on the exchange rate to remain stable. So I don't think that's the most acute dilemma we face. We've found a way to manage that. Yes, should we [allow] more exchange rate movement? Yes — it unshackles us even more.

Shruti: Are you at all worried that the kind of capital inflows you wish to attract is going to make the management of the other two a little bit harder, especially on the monetary policy side?

Sajjid: Not really. We want to get more FDI. Let's say we're successful in getting an FDI. What happens in that case is that the RBI will simply absorb that in terms of higher reserves. The first task is going to be, once this pressure point boils over and capital flows return — let's hope that's sooner — we will need to rebuild reserves. You don't want the exchange rate to take the full adjustment.

But even in the case let's say the exchange rate does appreciate, that's not where the pressure point is, because in that case that's disinflationary. Letting capital flows come in and letting the exchange rate do the work is disinflationary, which means it takes the pressure off the RBI. The problem happens in the other direction —

Shruti: The other direction, yeah —

Sajjid: When capital flows out and the exchange rate is under pressure, sometimes emerging markets want to raise interest rates to prevent that from happening. And that in a way is not in sync with your domestic growth-inflation dynamics — monetary policy gets stuck defending the rupee. The good news is we haven't seen that in India the last couple of years. In fact, the whole idea of inflation targeting was: monetary policy should not be defending the rupee; it should focus on growth-inflation dynamics, and let the rupee be determined by external fundamentals.

Shruti: On the other hand, if we start opening up our markets such that we get more capital inflows, that also opens the door to have more capital outflow. So the downside that you were talking about — isn't that just a natural consequence? It'll be cyclical one day, right? So at some point we'll end up having to manage this.

Sajjid: And the way to manage that is twofold. When you've got a large chunk of reserves, you let the exchange rate be the first shock absorber. When that exchange rate movement gets disruptive, then you intervene with reserves to slow the pace of depreciation. This is a well-oiled playbook for this.

Shruti: And we have the reserves for it also.

Sajjid: Then you have reserves. When capital flows in, they tend to be very procyclical. At some point capital flows out. When capital flows out, so be it — let the exchange rate depreciate like it is now. And if that depreciation gets too disruptive, then you use your foreign exchange reserves to slow that depreciation down.

But you don't use interest rates. Successive governors have ensured that doesn't happen. We've not raised interest rates because of rupee depreciation. Interest rates will move when that rupee depreciation translates into higher core inflation and your inflation mandate gets threatened. Then you use interest rates as a second round.

十、收尾：三个银线与"危机即改革窗口"

Shruti: Is that coming our way, by the way, just because of the fuel price shock that is approaching us? We thought this war will be over in two or three weeks, and now it's just gone on and we're getting into June. So is this where we're headed in some way?

Sajjid: We've spoken about all the challenges. Let's end on some positives here.

三个正面起点

Let me give you three positives.

First, we began the pandemic with an economy that was slowing sharply, on a weak [wicket]. In contrast, we've entered this episode with a cyclical upswing. So we've got a good starting point on growth.

Second, the other good starting point is on inflation. The last [reading] was only at 2%. And core inflation is still running at 2.2%.

Shruti: Yeah, but that's because of good inventory management, right? Anytime in the next few weeks, are we looking at a serious oil shock?

Sajjid: A couple of things. The good news — not good news, but the inevitable outcome — is oil prices have begun to move up; retail prices of petrol and diesel have begun to move. It's very important because you want prices to reflect their opportunity cost so you get the right response. That will push up headline inflation. Will it push up core inflation, which is when central banks get concerned, is an open question.

But I would argue, Shruti — one thing we haven't talked about is India is still 5% below its pre-pandemic potential path, and that has meant there's still slack in the economy. Also, Chinese imports have exerted lots of disinflationary forces. You marry both those things and that tells you why in the last two years, despite growth being resilient, India's core inflation has averaged only 2-3% — because there still is slack, and there is the Chinese disinflationary impact. In that environment, you need to see very sharp and sustained input price increases for second-round effects to be elevated and sustained. So I'm less worried about where inflation is headed; I'm more worried about the balance of payments.

The good news is that growth was strong coming in, we can take a bit of a hit this year. Inflation was benign — so there's some ways to go before the RBI gets worried.

The third piece of good news, which may be temporary, is that we began by discussing shortages. The worry in March was that we will see widespread shortages by the month of May if the [Strait] doesn't open. The good news is that we've been able to source more crude and more energy from around the world.

Shruti: Yeah, and we've diversified —

Sajjid: Diversified. LPG is still a problem. But at least for now, these are the three silver linings. Of course, if this continues for a while, all of this will come under question.

把危机当作改革的政治窗口

Shruti: And irrespective of what's going on with the West Asian crisis or even the broader geopolitical trade stuff, we just need to get the house in order when it comes to reforms. We need to get our factor markets in order, we need to remove distortionary prices for all these inputs like fertilizer. I mean, a crisis is a good time to do it, but anytime would be a good time to do that stuff.

Sajjid: The problems are well known, the [solutions] are well known. The hope is that a crisis like this opens up the political space to push on some of [them]. We saw that last year — the 50% tariff came. I think it pushed us to do the GST stuff that was being negotiated for a long while, but it focused the minds to do it quickly. We got something on a reform. We began discussing the two deregulation committees. We began think[ing] of import tariffs and QCOs coming down. So we got some of that momentum started. We should hope —

Shruti: That we just keep building on it.

Sajjid: When the firefighting from this episode is over, we use this to say, "Listen, the world is changing in undesirable ways. For India to compete and be attractive and get pull FDI and pull flows, we need to [make] some of the hard decisions that we've been faced with for many decades."

Shruti: Hopefully you and I will be in business and have lots to talk about in terms of reforms. Thank you so much for doing this. This was such a pleasure.

Sajjid: Always a pleasure, Shruti. Thank you so much.

Ideas of India is produced by the Mercatus Center at George Mason University. If you enjoy this podcast, please subscribe on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, or your favorite podcast app. Help us grow by giving us a rating and leaving a review. Follow us on Twitter @srajagopalan and @ideasofindia. Also check out our initiative commemorating 30 years of India's market reforms at the1991project.com.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：印度不是"供给受限"，而是"需求受限"——这颠覆了三十年的政策本能
Chinoy 抛出了一个几乎被所有印度宏观叙事忽视的判断：**印度经济的核心病灶已经从"供给约束"翻转为"需求约束"**，但整个政策机器（包括话语、媒体、产业政策）仍在按"供给短缺"的旧剧本运转。
制造业产能利用率自 2012 年起卡在 74-75% 一线，十多年没有突破临界值。这意味着 PLI、基建狂飙、减税降准——所有"释放供给"的工具——都在解决一个**已经不存在的问题**。这是一种典型的"将军总在打上一场战争"的政策路径依赖。
更惊人的隐藏推论是：如果约束确实是需求侧，那么印度过去十年最被推崇的"宏观稳定"成就（低赤字、低通胀、克制刺激），在某种意义上**亲手压制了自己的私人投资周期**。这不是说宏观稳定错了，而是它的成功制造了下一阶段的新困境——一个"成功者的诅咒"。
## 二、跨领域连接：卢比、安全带与"波斯尼亚式"汇率政策的隐喻
Chinoy 把卢比比作"安全带"是这场访谈中最优雅的洞见，但它的真正威力在于跨领域映射——这是**控制论里"缓冲变量"的思想**：在一个由多

item.title: [Business of Sport] What does Burnley’s £35M Compensation Win Mean for English Football?
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最被低估的细节：这场胜诉的真正"武器"是一个数据科学家，不是律师

大多数评论把焦点放在"俱乐部之间能不能互告"的法律新颖性上，但真正改变游戏规则的，是 Burnley 用 10 万次蒙特卡洛模拟 来"反事实地重写"那个赛季。这意味着英格兰足球的争议解决，正在悄悄地从法理论证（legal reasoning）转向概率论证（probabilistic reasoning）。

这是一个让人意外的转折：仲裁庭实质上接受了"如果……那么……"这种反事实因果（counterfactual causality）作为索赔基础——这种方法论在反垄断诉讼里早已成熟（美国法院在评估市场操纵造成的损失时常用），在保险精算和气候诉讼里也是核心工具，但在足球世界里这是第一次被"判赔 2600 万英镑"地确认。

跨领域连接：足球俱乐部之间的"损失机会（loss of chance）"诉讼，本质上和烟草集体诉讼、气候变化归因诉讼走的是同一条认识论路径——用概率模型把"假如世界另一种走向"货币化。Burnley 这次胜诉，其实是把足球拽进了"计量法律学（econometric jurisprudence）"的时代。

二、被几乎所有人忽略的结构性后果：俱乐部 CFO 的权力，正在悄悄超过 Sporting Director

表面上这是 Everton 赔 Burnley 钱的事，但真正的隐性变革是 —— 从今往后，每一个转会窗口的决策权，会从体育总监手里漂移到财务合规团队。

这和金融业 2008 年之后的演化惊人地相似：雷曼之后，华尔街的"明星交易员文化"被合规官（compliance officer）文化取代，风控部门从成本中心变成了否决权中心。Premier League 现在正在经历同样的"合规化转向"——而触发点不是监管机构本身，而是同业之间互相起诉的可能性。

更深一层的反讽：Charlie 提到的那些"50、60 岁、习惯了砸钱解决问题"的高管文化，其实和金融危机前那批"too big to fail"银行家是同一种心理结构——用别人的钱去赌短期野心，并把"赌"包装成"ambition"。这次判决摧毁的不只是 Everton 的钱包，而是这一整代足球高管的世界观。

三、隐藏的系统真相：Premier League 在"主动制造混乱"以巩固自己的主权

最反直觉的洞察是 —— 这个看起来对 Premier League 来说"公关灾难"的结果，其实是它精心维护的主权工具。

注意一个细节：所有这些纠纷都被强制留在 Premier League 自己设的 arbitration commission 里，不能上普通法院。这看起来像"行业自治"，但实际上是 Premier League 在做和中世纪商人法庭（Lex Mercatoria）、现代体育仲裁法庭 CAS、甚至加密世界里的 DAO 治理一样的事：通过给成员一个"内部安全阀"，换取他们放弃外部主权。

换句话说，Burnley 赢了 3500 万，看起来是 Burnley 的胜利；Everton 输了 3500 万，看起来是 Everton 的失败。但真正的赢家是 Richard Masters —— 他成功地证明了： - 联盟可以惩罚成员 - 成员可以惩罚成员
- 但任何事都不需要走进英国高等法院

这是一个被几乎所有评论忽略的权力转移：Premier League 正在通过"允许内部诉讼"来预防外部司法介入。这和金融监管里"自律组织（SRO）"（如 FINRA）的逻辑一模一样——给行业一点自我惩戒的牙齿，是为了不让国家把更大的牙齿伸进来。

啊哈时刻：Burnley 的胜诉，表面上是俱乐部对俱乐部的赢，实质上是英超联盟对英国法院的赢。

四、一个还没有人问的问题

如果损失机会（loss of chance）可以被量化、被赔偿，那么反向命题呢？——一个因为对手被扣分而意外升级或保级的俱乐部，会不会有一天被要求退还那笔"不当得利"？目前的判决只走单向（受害方索赔），但概率因果一旦被司法接受，对称性问题迟早会浮现。这可能是下一个 5 年里最有趣的法律边界。

📝 转录文本

引子：一个被严重低估的判决

Our first topic is one that you and I both looked at and said, "Do you know what? I cannot believe this has not got more attention." Which makes me excited because it generally means that there's not a wealth of material for everyone to dive into, but it also means that there's quite a responsibility on this, I think, because it's not just a huge topic for now, it's a huge topic for the future.

And we are, of course, talking about the landmark case of Burnley versus Everton. If you haven't seen this, Burnley have won a case that allows them to claim 35 million pounds worth of — could be called damages. Compensation is probably the official word, isn't it? Compensation against Everton, because Everton were found to have breached PSR rules in July 2022.

Of that season, Burnley were relegated in May 2022. Burnley have claimed that if Everton had been deducted points that season, then they would have stayed up on goal difference — because Everton's 10-point deduction that was given to them by an independent panel was then reduced to six points, which was the difference between staying up and going down.

Without going into the depths of this case, because it was a while ago and it's been covered pretty well, I think the reason this is so big for us, and where we really wanted to cover it, is firstly how this actually happened, and secondly what it means for the future.

一、Burnley 如何打赢：10 万次模拟

I was really interested, Charlie. The thing that stood out to me — I was looking at how did Burnley manage to do this? And I don't know if you've seen this, but they actually employed a hugely detailed data model to do it.

So, they got a data scientist to run basically 100,000 simulations of the situation. And they put in the financials with Everton overspending and the likelihood of them, with that extra money, getting more points or not. I think they did it about 100,000 times, and it came out 51% of the time that that money gave them an extra seven points, give or take, and therefore had materially helped them remain in the league. And on the flip side, put Burnley down.

So, what an outcome? And two, what the hell does it mean for the future?

二、关键法理：这不是"上法院"，而是英超内部的仲裁

Yeah. This is huge. This is absolutely huge.

I think to unpack this, I want to go back to some fundamental underlying principles, right? When clubs enter the Premier League, they have to sign up to an agreement that says that they will not bring claims externally against other Premier League clubs.

So, you spoke about a case — but this isn't like a case which is being held in a court of law. This is a case which is being held in an arbitration meeting, which fundamentally is set up by the Premier League. So, it's an independent commission of the Premier League. Now, as it happens, the independent commission will then be staffed by the type of people who would be adjudicating cases of law. And in this case, the three-person panel was David Phillips KC, Nick Igoe ACA, and His Honor Alan Greenwood, former judge. So, these are people who are qualified to look at these types of things in detail. But it is very specifically a Premier League procedure.

As the competition organizer, what they don't want is a whole bunch of their members fighting each other through the courts.

Cuz previously when we've seen these cases, it's been the Premier League going for a club, right? So it's league versus member.

Yeah — not member versus member.

That's right. But it's always been there in the statutes of the Premier League that if you want to take on one of the other members, you need to do it through this route rather than going externally and suing them in a different way.

为什么这个机制重要？

Okay, so why is that important? How does it change this process?

I think it's important because it's a way of saying there is a precedent that has been set. But this is not a new departure. This is not something which has been created out of nothing — which is what I have heard listening to various radio discussions on it: "Oh my god, this is totally new. The Premier League has gone off in a totally different direction." It's always been the case.

You need this safety valve, because if the Premier League doesn't have a mechanism for dealing with disputes, then effectively the courts have to become involved. The problem with the courts becoming involved is, first of all, if it happens to go to jury trial, anything can happen, frankly. Secondly, there can be a massive waiting time for normal court matters to come before a court. So at least this way, the Premier League controls the process and the timing of the process, even if it's the independent commission itself that decides.

三、原始事件回顾：19.5 百万英镑的违规

I just want to go back and lay out the original issue. You've said it already, but: Everton breached PSR in 21–22 by 19.5 million pounds. Quite a big breach, but not a massive breach.

And that's 19.5 million pounds over the 105 million pound allowed threshold that the Premier League has put in place.

Exactly that. Thanks very much. So that season, Everton finished two places above the relegation spots, and they were four points ahead of Burnley. Burnley had a superior goal difference — significantly superior goal difference. So it really is four points, not four points could be five points; it's four points.

Effectively what happened is that when, in November 2023, the EPL Premier League's independent commission decided that they had breached these rules significantly, David Phillips KC, who was the chairman of the commission, in his judgment specified that it would be possible for other clubs to make claims. So this was specified. This wasn't something Burnley have come up with out of the blue. This was part of the entire process. It's part of the safety valve.

It's important that people understand this: if these types of disputes don't get settled in this way, the disputes don't go away. They have to then get settled in a different way, and that probably does mean having the traditional court system starting to get involved in football — which is something that we definitely don't want. So, binding arbitration by an EPL independent commission is, to my mind, exactly the right way to go in terms of sorting out these types of disputes.

四、Leeds 先和解、Burnley 走到底

So, what happens next? Burnley and Leeds, who were the team that finished one place above the relegation zone, both come out and say that they are considering taking action through this protocol against Everton.

Everton then reached a private settlement with Leeds in September of last year, September 2025. What were Leeds claiming about? Leeds were really claiming about the merit payment — if we'd finished one place higher and you'd finished one place lower, that would've been two or three million quid. It sounds like a small amount of money, but nonetheless, it's still reasonably substantial.

That was settled — I was about to say out of court — out of commission. The fact that it was settled, and it's never been clear exactly what the settlement was, but that basically to me says that Everton accepted prima facie that Leeds did have a case of some kind, otherwise they could have said, "No, take it all the way to the commission."

So, in other words, "the lady is protesting quite a lot here" in the case of Everton, when they've already tacitly accepted that there was this potential route — which they kind of had to do, because the independent commission had already said that this route is open.

"Loss of chance" 与 Burnley 的索赔

But that was a relatively small amount, whereas what Burnley were claiming for was clearly going to be a much bigger amount: well, we got relegated.

Effectively what we're talking about here is a legal term called loss of chance. The loss of chance to Burnley of not being in the Premier League for that following season was obviously much more considerable than Leeds' loss of chance in finishing one place higher in the Premier League that season.

Burnley's original claim was for quite a bit more — they were saying it should be 50–55 million or something. So, if it went up to 26 million, effectively the commission's gone: "Well, okay, we don't go with you all the way on this, but we do think that there has been material damage caused here, and we now require, on behalf of the competition organizers, Everton to make good on that."

五、对整个英超的威慑效应

Just to finish off my initial thoughts on this: I think this is an appropriate process and an appropriate outcome. I think it makes the jeopardy of breaking these spending rules much greater.

Up until this point, I've been at so many conferences and speaking to people who work with or around Premier League clubs, and they've been treading this very fine line on spending rules. Almost the attitude's been: most of the time we'll get away with it, and on the odd occasion that we might not, we might get a points deduction, we might get a bit of a fine, but we'll probably just deal with that. It's okay.

I think what this does is it says: be very careful about thinking that way. You want to make sure that you are well within the spending regulations, because actually this could go very badly wrong for you if what you do is then deemed to have gained an unfair advantage over other clubs who then have loss of chance.

Everton 的辩护：他们"不知道"自己违规？

And I think it's important to state that in this case in particular — which probably hasn't helped Everton — after year two they were very close to the 105 million threshold, which actually set in motion a number of things that the Premier League then did to observe. This wasn't a massive surprise at the end of year three when they posted that loss, because the Premier League had warned them. They'd basically had every player deal that they'd done in that time approved by the Premier League. They said, "Look, if this is going to happen, you have to be aware that you're going to be pushing those boundaries."

Everton's claim at the end was that they didn't know that they had been in breach.

Well, really?

It was. And that wasn't accepted. In other words, they basically said, "Look, if we had known in May of that year that we were potentially in breach, we'd have just sold a player, or we'd have sold our women's team or something."

So their defense is that Burnley were relegated in May, and the year-end accounts are end of June. So 1st of July — anything before 1st of July, Everton are basically claiming doesn't matter, because they could have rectified.

Well, certainly anything before June the 25th, say. Their defense is based on the timeframe. But there's no way they couldn't have been aware that they were going to breach that, because at the time, if you look at the Premier League reporting on this, they were continually in contact with Everton on their overspending and the danger that they were sitting on breaching these positions.

六、为什么这是"里程碑案"：连锁效应

What did Everton not account for? Another team being able to come and take that from them — whether it's their understanding of the rules of the Premier League and individual clubs not being able to go after each other, or, as you say, just an acceptance of the previous penalties.

But what does this do now? Over the last few years, we have seen a number of financial punishments for clubs breaching profit and sustainability. We have seen Nottingham Forest get penalized. We have seen Leicester get penalized. We have seen Manchester City — with fairness, the huge one — 115 charges over their head. February 2023, this is all happening since February 2023, those punishments.

So, while Man City are sitting here with those charges, these clubs with a far fewer number of charges are living out the real-life penalties.

And then don't even get started on Chelsea and the way that they've been punished — not yet for PSR-related, but potentially for more severe breaches if you look at the conduct with which they were managed: the "backhand" payments, if I'm allowed to say that, to agents, and their acceptance of a 10 million fine for fees being paid to agents between 2011 and 2018.

Which now, by the way, Charlie — just to reassure you wearing your Chelsea hat — now sits outside the statute of limitations.

Does it?

It does. And one of the reasons why: during 2024, Arsenal, Liverpool, Man U and Tottenham all served notice that in the event that Man City were to be found guilty of those 115 charges — and they still deny those allegations, to be absolutely clear — that they reserve their right to pursue claims against them.

Why did they do that? Because the statute of limitations was coming up on those claims. This case has been going on for so long that there was a real risk to those clubs. They needed in 2024 to get ahead of the case and say, "It's not our fault this case is taking so long. We're effectively playing a provisional, in golfing terms. We're going to put our claims in so that if this does go the way in which we think it might do, that we're not timed out." So those are their reservation of rights notes that they put in.

七、损失机会的量化难题

What's the difference between when the breaches were committed versus when the breaches were found to have been committed? Because for Chelsea, this was a relatively new finding, but it happened quite a few years ago.

As I understand it, this is about when the situations happened. Let's just say, for the sake of argument, that some historic misdemeanor by a major club in the late 1990s came to light now, 30 years on — you wouldn't be able to go back and say, "Well, hang on a second. We would have won this league or that league."

We've been fortunate enough to be sent an absolutely comprehensive note on this entire situation by our friends at Onside Law. It's brilliantly incisive in understanding where the real issues are.

I think what we're looking at here is — we've already mentioned the situation with Man City allegations and where that might lead. But you also have to start about scale as well. In this case, a judgment was made on: what was the actual real loss of chance to Burnley, and what was the probability of it? They came up with something that said, "Well, it was 51% X or Y." So, effectively, if the panel accepts that, then they might apply that 51% to a loss of chance and say: "Let's say the total amount you actually suffered was 50 million, which is what you're claiming for, but we think that there's only a 50% chance of you having actually had to suffer that — then we come to 25 million," which is the 26 million that they've been awarded.

Man City 案的复杂性

In a situation like Manchester City, where the loss of chance to the teams who have made those claims is the loss of chance to have won the Premier League in certain years when Man City did win the Premier League — assessing that loss of chance is going to be a whole lot more complicated.

In the case of Burnley, actually, it's quite easy. You say: "Burnley, you went down and you got your first-year parachute payment. Your first-year parachute payment was 55 million pounds." If you had stayed in the Premier League, you'd have got a full basic payment from the Premier League, which would have been about double that — about 110 million pounds. Therefore, the amount of financial damage you've effectively lost (because we know that your stadium was still full and so on) is basically 55 million pounds, and therefore we're going to apply a probability to that.

Okay, so then the 50% of that, as you said, comes to 25. So actually that 25 makes sense.

In some ways, Everton were very fortunate that Burnley bounced straight back and got promoted again. Otherwise you could have stretched it out into the future seasons.

But let's just try and think — this is going to be much more complicated. In the event that clubs like Tottenham do get to make their claim, let's just speculate they do for whatever reason — assessing what the financial damage has been to Tottenham of never having become the Premier League champion that they might have become, that is a very, very difficult assessment to make.

Even the valuation of the club — Spurs haven't come close when City have won. Spurs came close when Chelsea did. So Spurs would have the strongest case if they could go for Chelsea.

八、这会失控吗？

So this is my point, and I've struggled with it because I don't want to say that just because the outcomes are complex and the punishments are complex, it doesn't mean that there shouldn't be punishment. But when you're starting to look at this — if you applied punishment similar to what we've seen here between Burnley and Everton, could this get very quickly out of hand if City get punished by the Premier League and then have a number of claims from clubs who have not won the league, who have not qualified for European competition? Could a club who's been relegated and lost six points to City throughout a season have a case because those six points that they lost to City were detrimental in them going down? How far can this go?

Well, I think in this situation four clubs have made clear their statement of claim, and other clubs will be timed out on that now in the case of City. So I think we have to park the City case, not least because I'm just very conscious always in discussing what are still ongoing cases or situations — we have to keep on reminding ourselves that City do deny these charges, and City do basically say, "Look, we don't think we've done anything substantial wrong here." So I think we should also just reserve our judgment on this particular case.

CEO 视角下的未来

But let's look to the future and ask ourselves the question: if I'm a chief executive of a Premier League club and I see this whole situation playing out — what do I do? What kind of instructions do I push out internally within my own organization?

Don't lose money. Whatever you do, do not breach spending regulations. Whatever you do, do not even come close. Don't even put us in a situation where, if we've made one slight miscalculation or something that we were expecting to happen doesn't quite happen, or if the Premier League decide that something that we thought sat within this year should sit within another year — make absolutely sure that you are well within whatever the spending regulations are.

So really what we're looking at is a messy outcome for the short term, but something that's going to influence the future of the game in the way that really it needs to happen if we're going to get this game back in order and make it viable. You need to stop this kind of behavior. It stops that "treading the fine line" sort of act which so many clubs have been going through in recent years.

九、与西班牙模式的比较：从 PSR 到 Squad Cost Ratio

I think this is another reinforcement on top of having the independent football regulator, on top of moving to squad cost ratios rather than the old P&S system. This was, by the way, a breach under the old PSR system. But we're now moving on to a system which is easier to apply and clearer, and where the Premier League and the football regulator will be peering over the shoulders of clubs and pretty much at the time be able to sort of stop them in real time — as happens in Spain.

In Spain, I don't know if you remember when Barcelona tried to sign a couple of players a couple of years ago, La Liga just stepped in and said, "Well, you can't register them. You just can't play them."

We've been a little bit behind in this country, and I think that's because there's been this rather "wild west" culture in our football industry for some time, where executives who've been around the game for quite a long time — and I came across a lot of these people, mostly now in their 50s, 60s, who have been around for 30, 40 years — who quite like the idea of being able to just wang lots of cash at things if they're in trouble. So they've always sort of made the argument: "Well, you know, you don't want to stamp ambition out of the game." As if wanging loads of cash at some short-term problem is ambition. It's not.

Cuz it's not their money.

Absolutely, it's not their money. It's other people's money. And it makes it much easier for them then to speak with their owners and say, "Look, if you really want to make sure you're going to stay up, I'll tell you what — the thing to do is give me an extra 50 million to spend, right?"

十、Moshiri 时代的遗产 & Friedkins 的尴尬

It's interesting, isn't it? This breach of Everton's, we should say, does not relate to the current ownership.

Farhad Moshiri.

Right. This is the Moshiri–Usmanov era of Everton, where things were unbelievably chaotic. And I think if there's anyone to feel sorry for in this whole sorry saga, it's the Friedkins, who now —

Well, I've seen it reported that there's a chance — let's say there's a chance — that they could go and claim it off any money owed to Moshiri. So, Moshiri, I think, interestingly, he got 25 million in this whole transaction. Which, if you're looking at this fine, if they have reasonable grounds to do it, could wipe off that money.

So it will all depend on what the reps and warranties in that sale agreement actually look like. The problem with Moshiri having received such a small amount of money in the first place for the sale of the club is that one would suspect the reps and warranties weren't necessarily all that favorable to the buyer. It's probably Moshiri who was saying, "Okay, you're going to have the club cheaper, effectively, but I want to make damn sure you can't pursue me in the future." That would be quite a normal way to go in a situation where the club's being handed over for a relatively small amount of money.

十一、会计层面：罚款不算 PSR，赔偿能否算收入？

It's worth also saying that Everton's financial punishment here isn't going to be put towards their PSR. So that is something that they were very keen to understand very early on. And that's very important money at this level.

Charlie, I'd also be interested to know — would Burnley be able to use that money as income? If we're moving to these new squad cost ratio rules, and you're looking at what you're able to spend versus money coming in — I would imagine if it's not going towards Everton's PSR, Burnley couldn't claim that that's revenue that they could then leverage to spend more on players.

Well, you really put me on the spot on that one. Would this be regarded as being football-related income? I'll tell you what, Charlie — I'll get back to you next week on that one. That's a really interesting question. I'll have to get on to some people who understand the precise application of rules and regs more acutely than I do.

十二、上诉前景与 Premier League 的"主权胜利"

They got to sell it immensely. While the appeal's going on, at least Everton have said they don't want to make the payment, because they see Burnley's financial situation as pretty poor and they believe that if they give them the money and the money's spent and Everton win an appeal, they won't get it back. So there's a lot of play.

But there is a lot of play. Reading the judgment and reading legal views on the judgment — including the excellent note that we've received from Onside Law — I don't think personally that that appeal is going to be successful. The grounds they're laying out, if you read the detail as to what the grounds on which an appeal even can be successful are, I don't think it's easily arguable that those things have been breached.

The grounds on which you can appeal a judgment that's made by an independent commission of the Premier League are very, very narrow. It's not good enough to say, "Well, effectively like you would do in a civil court, I don't really like that verdict. I want effectively a retrial." That's not the way it works. You have to say that there's been a specific thing which hasn't been taken into consideration at all and which we now need to be taken into consideration.

This is a really bad look for the Premier League.

I put it in the basket of: you need to break a few eggs to make an omelet. And I think there's a lot of stuff going on at the Premier League right now which I really applaud.

I think it went through a really shaky period when effectively the executive of the Premier League rather lost control over the constituent clubs, and you started to see some quite knife-edge votes happening in terms of even having proper rules. Now they've got a proper set of rules which are roughly in line with UEFA.

UEFA did them a massive favor, because once UEFA said "we're going to move to proper spending restrictions," that then meant that half of the Premier League who want to play in UEFA's competitions were going to have to move on to a proper set of spending regulations. That then meant that those clubs then wanted the rest of the Premier League to be on proper spending regulations, because they were going to be forced to anyway. And that has reinforced the Premier League executive's hands. I think stuff like this reinforces the fact: don't dick around. Don't piss us around. Bad stuff can happen to you if you do that.

So yes, in the short run, I'm sure that the PR people of the Premier League aren't jumping up and down saying, "Wow, this is a great look." But behind closed scenes, do I think that Richard Masters, the Premier League chief executive, is starting to feel "I've got control back of the situation"? I think this is heading in the right direction. I think he probably is.

Interesting.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最被低估的细节：这场胜诉的真正"武器"是一个数据科学家，不是律师
大多数评论把焦点放在"俱乐部之间能不能互告"的法律新颖性上，但真正改变游戏规则的，是 Burnley 用 **10 万次蒙特卡洛模拟** 来"反事实地重写"那个赛季。这意味着英格兰足球的争议解决，正在悄悄地从**法理论证（legal reasoning）**转向**概率论证（probabilistic reasoning）**。
这是一个让人意外的转折：仲裁庭实质上接受了"如果……那么……"这种**反事实因果（counterfactual causality）**作为索赔基础——这种方法论在**反垄断诉讼**里早已成熟（美国法院在评估市场操纵造成的损失时常用），在**保险精算和气候诉讼**里也是核心工具，但在足球世界里这是第一次被"判赔 2600 万英镑"地确认。
> 跨领域连接：足球俱乐部之间的"损失机会（loss of chance）"诉讼，本质上和**烟草集体诉讼、气候变化归因诉讼**走的是同一条认识论路径——用概率模型把"假如世界另一种走向"货币化。Burnley 这次胜诉，其实是把足球拽进了"计

item.title: [All-In Podcast] World's First Trillionaire, Anthropic Fable Banned, The New Oligarchs, Iran Peace Deal
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的元结构：这段转录本身就是它所批判内容的镜像

最让我意外的观察是——这份转录稿在技术层面"卡带"了二十多遍，同一段开场白和Freeberg的"政治局"演讲被一字不差地循环播放。而这恰好就是Freeberg论点的形式化证明：当一种叙事（无论是"自由vs暴政"还是"公平vs精英"）被高频重复、剥离上下文、不断回环时，它就从"观点"变成了"环境噪音"——即所谓的意识形态背景辐射。

播客作为媒介本身已经异化为它所批评的东西：一个循环灌输的系统。Freeberg激烈反对"控制媒体"的政治局，却在一个把他自己的话重复14次的频道上被消费。这是麦克卢汉式的反讽——媒介即讯息，而该讯息是：所有政治批判最终都会被它依附的媒介结构所反噬。

二、跨领域连接：Freeberg的"基于度曲线"与马斯洛倒置

Freeberg的同伴们调侃说：身价越涨他越"based"（敢说狠话）——"九位数Freeberg有顾虑，十位数Freeberg直接YOLO"。这看似玩笑，却揭示了一个鲜少被命名的现象：财富-言论自由的U形曲线。

把它放到马斯洛需求层次里看，会得到一个反直觉结论： - 底层：因恐惧失业而自我审查 - 中产：因要保护既得利益而最沉默（"九位数阶段"） - 顶端：因不再依赖任何系统反馈而重获言论自由（"十位数YOLO"）

这与社会学中的"经济独立悖论"完美呼应：只有完全脱离市场反馈循环的人，才能说出真正不受市场约束的话。这就是为什么硅谷亿万富翁突然集体开始"政治觉醒"——不是他们变了，是他们终于到达了可以承担说真话成本的阈值。讽刺的是：他们正在控诉的"寡头"，本质上也是同一现象的镜像——只是站在政治资本的顶端而非财务资本顶端。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：左右两派在共用同一套"剥夺感操作系统"

Freeberg说政治局派系（Warren、Sanders、Khanna）通过"我们给你免费教育、免费医疗、免费食物"来收买中间选民。但有趣的是——他和Sacks自己的播客叙事，运行的是结构完全相同的程序，只是输出端不同： - 左派承诺：剥夺富人 → 给予你物质 - 右派/科技自由派承诺：剥夺政府 → 给予你机会/自由/AI红利

两边都在告诉观众："你之所以痛苦，是因为某个邪恶集团在剥夺你。" 这是同一个心理操作系统在跑不同的应用程序。

更深的洞察是：当一个社会的两端都在使用"剥夺叙事"作为主流政治语言时，说明这个社会真正进入了零和心态阶段——大家都不再相信"做大蛋糕"，只剩下"谁在偷走我的那块"。这是帝国晚期的典型症状，从罗马的"面包与马戏"到拜占庭的派系斗争都遵循同一模式。

真正可怕的不是政治局是否在形成，而是无论谁掌权，叙事结构都已经无法逃离"敌我剥夺"的二元框架。这才是Anthropic的Fable被禁、千万富翁焦虑、伊朗和平协议——这些看似无关的标题能并置在一个视频里的深层原因：它们都是注意力经济在零和时代的副产品。

📝 转录文本

开场与互相调侃

All right everybody, welcome back to the number one podcast in the world — it's the All-In Podcast. The original quartet is here.

Bizarre — David Sacks, how you doing, brother?

"I'm good. You got a busy week — a lot of long posts from you on X, we'll get into it."

"Great. A lot of long posting? A lot of long posts?"

"I had like one long post."

"Oh yeah, how's the carpal tunnel going over there?"

"Yeah, two long posts. Yeah, that carpal tunnel flaring up. I got a third one teed up — I might use it."

"You got one in the chamber?"

"I got one in the chamber, but I was gonna save it for the pod. It's a good one."

"All right, oh, coming in hot. I wonder who's gonna get this bullet — a cackling, of course."

"独裁者"登场

It's the dictator, it's my best teacher mom, Holly Hop-patia — how you doing, brother? Getting ready for summer? Gilets have been laid on the bed, you're ready to pack it up?

"Opposite. I got a lot of travel grinding."

"Oh, I forgot — you're doing founder-led sales, so the summer of relaxation is over. Now he's in the CEO slot, got to get out there and do those enterprise sales."

Freeberg 大爆发：评论区起火

And of course, coming off a pretty hot week — comment section was on fire. Very pro-Freeberg, very very Freeberg. Perhaps his greatest performance — also greatest the first performance when he said more than three sentences.

What's going on, Freeberg? You went off last episode, and then I think you also with the long posts...

"You know what's so funny? As the valuation of Ohalo ticks up, he gets more and more based. I love it. He is getting — welcome to the club, welcome to the club. He's got trace commas based — unleash the beast."

"When you're just a mere singleton, you kind of just keep it to yourself. And then all of a sudden, nine-figure Freeberg — he's like, 'I got something to lose.' Wait for ten-figure Freeberg. Ten-figure Freeberg's like YOLO."

"You also with the long posts — you and Nancy Pelosi, uh, making sweet sweet..."

"美利坚政治局"宣言

"Here's — here's what's happening. He's starting..."

"What's happening — what is being formed in the United States right now is the great American... okay, the great American Politburo has formed. The new oligarchs are taking their seats."

"The great American Politburo is being formed. The new oligarchs are taking their seats. They're arranging the chairs.
